confirmed.title: parseJiraErrorMessage crashes with ClassCastException on non-primitive error values
confirmed.description: Line 473: `obj["errors"]?.jsonObject?.entries?.forEach { (k, v) -> messages += "$k: ${v.jsonPrimitive.content}" }` calls `.jsonPrimitive.content` unconditionally on every value in the `errors` map. Jira's `errors` object can contain non-primitive values (e.g. arrays of constraint violations on custom fields). When `v` is a `JsonArray` or `JsonObject`, calling `.jsonPrimitive` throws `kotlinx.serialization.json.JsonException` at runtime. The outer `catch (_: Exception)` swallows it, but this means the entire error block returns null, so the user sees a generic 'Bad request (400)' instead of the real field-validation message.
confirmed.evidence: obj["errors"]?.jsonObject?.entries?.forEach { (k, v) -> messages += "$k: ${v.jsonPrimitive.content}" }
confirmed.suggestedFix: Guard each value: replace `v.jsonPrimitive.content` with `(v as? kotlinx.serialization.json.JsonPrimitive)?.content ?: v.toString()`. This is safe for primitives, arrays, and objects without throwing.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at line 473. `v.jsonPrimitive.content` is called unconditionally on every value in the `errors` map. kotlinx.serialization's `JsonElement.jsonPrimitive` throws `IllegalArgumentException` when the element is a `JsonArray` or `JsonObject`. The outer `catch (_: Exception)` at line 475 swallows the exception and returns null, so `parseJiraErrorMessage` returns null and callers surface the generic HTTP status string (e.g. 'Bad request (400)') instead of the real field-validation detail. This affects Jira Server workflows that use custom fields with array-valued errors (e.g. constraint-violation arrays). Severity is Medium rather than High: the exception is caught and handled gracefully (no crash, no data loss); the only consequence is degraded error messaging. The suggested fix (`(v as? JsonPrimitive)?.content ?: v.toString()`) is safe and non-breaking.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Replace `v.jsonPrimitive.content` with `(v as? kotlinx.serialization.json.JsonPrimitive)?.content ?: v.toString()` at line 473. No passing tests would break; this purely improves error-message fidelity.

confirmed.title: validateTicketKeys throws IllegalArgumentException instead of returning ApiResult.Error, propagating uncaught through TicketKeyCache
confirmed.description: Lines 529-532: when a key fails the anchored Jira key pattern, `validateTicketKeys` throws `IllegalArgumentException("Invalid Jira key: $k")` rather than returning `ApiResult.Error`. The only caller in production code is `TicketKeyCache.validateAndCache` (line 76), which is a `suspend fun` that does NOT wrap the call in try/catch. The exception propagates as an unhandled coroutine exception, cancelling the enclosing coroutine scope and potentially crashing the feature silently. The function signature promises `ApiResult<...>` so callers do not expect an exception.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Replace `throw IllegalArgumentException(...)` with `return ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.VALIDATION_ERROR, "Invalid Jira key: $k")`. This keeps the function contract and lets callers handle it uniformly.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at lines 529-532. `validateTicketKeys` throws `IllegalArgumentException` rather than returning `ApiResult.Error`. The sole production caller, `TicketKeyCache.validateAndCache` at line 76, calls `client.validateTicketKeys(uncached.toList())` with no try/catch, meaning the exception would propagate as an unhandled coroutine exception. However, the practical impact is very limited: `TicketKeyCache.extractKeys()` at line 36 pre-filters all keys using `DEFAULT_REGEX = Regex("\\b([A-Z][A-Z0-9]+-\\d+)\\b")`, which matches the same key format as the `jiraKeyPattern` in `validateTicketKeys`. Keys that reach `validateTicketKeys` have already passed a compatible regex match, so the `throw` path is essentially unreachable in the normal flow. The contract violation (function signature implies no exception but can throw) is real and the fix is safe.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Replace `throw IllegalArgumentException("Invalid Jira key: $k")` with `return ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.VALIDATION_ERROR, "Invalid Jira key: $k")` at line 531. Also add a try/catch guard in `TicketKeyCache.validateAndCache` as defense-in-depth. No passing tests would break.

confirmed.title: BranchingService.startWork / useExistingBranch crashes with NoSuchElementException on repositories with no configured remotes
confirmed.description: Lines 118 and 214: `git.fetch(repo, repo.remotes.first(), emptyList())` calls `.first()` on `repo.remotes` with no guard. A freshly-cloned repository with no remotes configured, or any offline/disconnected clone where the remote list is transiently empty, throws `NoSuchElementException`. Because both call sites are inside a `try { ... } catch (e: Exception)` block the exception IS caught, but the error message 'Failed to checkout branch: null' (since `e.message` for `NoSuchElementException` is null) is exposed to the user with no actionable guidance.
confirmed.evidence: val fetchResult = git.fetch(repo, repo.remotes.first(), emptyList())
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add an explicit guard: `val remote = repo.remotes.firstOrNull() ?: return ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.NOT_FOUND, "No Git remote configured for repository '${repo.root.path}'.")` before the fetch call at both sites.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at lines 118 and 214. `repo.remotes.first()` throws `NoSuchElementException` when `repo.remotes` is empty (e.g. a local-only repo or a transient VCS state during project indexing). Both call sites ARE inside `try { ... } catch (e: Exception)` blocks (lines 100-150 and 198-233), so there is no crash. The issue is solely UX: `NoSuchElementException.message` is null, so `e.message` is null, and the user sees 'Failed to checkout branch: null' with no actionable guidance. Severity is Low rather than High because the exception is always caught and the user is never left with a silent or partial failure — they get an error result. The fix (explicit `firstOrNull()` guard with a descriptive error message) is safe and non-breaking.
confirmed.recommendedAction: At both line 118 and 214, replace `repo.remotes.first()` with an explicit `repo.remotes.firstOrNull() ?: return ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.NOT_FOUND, "No Git remote configured for repository '${repo.root.path}'.")` guard placed before the fetch call, outside the try/catch.

confirmed.title: JiraServiceImpl.client property has an unsynchronized TOCTOU race on URL change under concurrent coroutines
confirmed.description: Lines 96-112: `cachedClient` and `cachedBaseUrl` are both `@Volatile` but the check-then-act sequence at lines 104-110 (`if (url != cachedBaseUrl || cachedClient == null) { cachedBaseUrl = url; cachedClient = JiraApiClient(...) }`) is not atomic. Two coroutines on `Dispatchers.IO` can both observe `url != cachedBaseUrl`, both assign `cachedBaseUrl = url`, and both construct a new `JiraApiClient`. The second write wins for `cachedClient`, but the first coroutine proceeds using the `return cachedClient` value which is now the second instance. While this normally results in two identical clients rather than a crash, it means connection-pool resources are leaked and any in-flight request on the discarded client gets a new connection every time settings change rapidly.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Use `@Synchronized` on the `client` getter or use a `@GuardedBy("this")` pattern: `@get:Synchronized private val client: JiraApiClient? get() = ...`. Alternatively, store a `Pair<String, JiraApiClient>` and use `@Volatile` on a single atomic reference.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at lines 96-112. The check-then-act pattern on two `@Volatile` fields is not atomic: two coroutines on `Dispatchers.IO` can both observe `url != cachedBaseUrl` and both construct a new `JiraApiClient`. The second write to `cachedClient` wins, leaving the first coroutine using a client instance that is immediately abandoned (leaking its OkHttp connection pool). In practice, `JiraApiClient` instantiation is cheap (no network call) and this race only fires when settings change between concurrent requests — an extremely rare condition during normal use. There is no data corruption, no credential leak, and no crash. Severity is Low (not Medium) given the benign consequence. A `@Synchronized` getter or a `@Volatile` `Pair<String, JiraApiClient>` atomic reference would eliminate it.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Annotate the `client` property getter with `@Synchronized`, or store a single `@Volatile` `Pair<String, JiraApiClient>?` reference and use `@Synchronized` on the update path. Either approach eliminates the connection-pool leak without changing observable behavior.

confirmed.title: TicketDetailPanel.currentIssueKey written on EDT but read from IO thread without @Volatile — stale visibility
confirmed.description: Line 105: `private var currentIssueKey: String? = null` is declared as a plain `var` (no `@Volatile`). It is written at line 126 on the EDT (`showIssue` is called from Swing event handlers) and read at lines 747 and 770 from `lazyScope.launch(Dispatchers.IO)` and `invokeLater` closures respectively. Without `@Volatile` (or confinement to a single thread), the JVM memory model does not guarantee that writes on the EDT are visible to threads on the IO thread pool, meaning a race condition where `currentIssueKey` reads a stale value and the guard check `if (currentIssueKey != issueKey) return@launch` fails to abort a stale load.
confirmed.evidence:
private var currentIssueKey: String? = null
...
lazyScope.launch(Dispatchers.IO) {
    ...
    if (currentIssueKey != issueKey) return@launch
confirmed.suggestedFix: Declare the field as `@Volatile private var currentIssueKey: String? = null`. Alternatively, use `AtomicReference<String?>` consistent with `currentUserName` which is already `AtomicReference` in the same class.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at lines 105, 126, 747, and 770. `currentIssueKey` is a plain `var` written on the EDT and read from `Dispatchers.IO` after a 200ms delay. The JVM memory model does not guarantee visibility of an EDT write to an IO-thread read without a happens-before edge. The initial write at line 126 is ordered before the `launch` at line 745 by program order within `showIssue()`, so the first read in the IO coroutine sees the correct value. The stale-read risk is specifically for a SECOND `showIssue()` call that updates `currentIssueKey` on the EDT while the IO coroutine is in its 200ms delay — the guard check `if (currentIssueKey != issueKey) return@launch` at line 747 might not observe the updated key and fail to abort the stale load. In practice the consequence is a redundant/stale UI refresh (not data loss or crash). `@Volatile` would close the gap. Severity is Low given the benign worst case (extra render of a previous ticket's data, quickly overwritten).
confirmed.recommendedAction: Change line 105 to `@Volatile private var currentIssueKey: String? = null`. This is consistent with `currentUserName` being `AtomicReference` in the same class and costs nothing.

confirmed.title: SprintPaginationCache.getCachedStartAt calls getOrLoadCache() OUTSIDE the synchronized(lock) block, creating a TOCTOU window
confirmed.description: Lines 50-56: `getCachedStartAt` calls `getOrLoadCache()` at line 51, then enters `synchronized(lock)` at line 52 to access `data.boards[...]`. `getOrLoadCache()` itself is internally synchronized and returns a `CacheData` reference. However, between the return from `getOrLoadCache()` and entry into the inner `synchronized(lock)` block, `saveCachedStartAt()` on another thread can replace `cache` with a NEW `CacheData` object. The `data` local variable still holds the old object, so `data.boards[boardId.toString()]` reads from stale state and the startAt returned may be one update behind.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Inline the read into a single synchronized block: `synchronized(lock) { val data = getOrLoadCache(); val entry = data.boards[boardId.toString()] ?: return 0; return (entry.lastStartAt - pageSize).coerceAtLeast(0) }`. `getOrLoadCache` is re-entrant-safe because it checks `cache?.let { return it }` at the top.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at lines 50-55. `getOrLoadCache()` is called at line 51 OUTSIDE `synchronized(lock)`, returning a `CacheData` reference. The `synchronized(lock)` block is entered at line 52. In the narrow window between lines 51 and 52, `saveCachedStartAt()` (lines 62-75) can: enter `synchronized(lock)`, call `getOrLoadCache()` (returns a new `CacheData` from disk since `cache` was null), mutate its `boards` map in-place, set `cache = data`, and exit the lock. The `data` local variable in `getCachedStartAt` then refers to the pre-save object. However, since `saveCachedStartAt` mutates the boards map IN-PLACE on the same `CacheData` object and sets `cache` to that same object, and `getOrLoadCache()` in the `getCachedStartAt` call had already returned a reference to the same (or equivalent) object before the save, the practical staleness is one save behind at most. Real but very low impact: at worst the user starts a sprint-pagination walk one page too early, immediately self-correcting on the next save cycle. Severity reassessed to Low.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Move the `getOrLoadCache()` call inside `synchronized(lock)` at line 52: `synchronized(lock) { val data = getOrLoadCache(); val entry = data.boards[boardId.toString()] ?: return 0; return (entry.lastStartAt - pageSize).coerceAtLeast(0) }`. `getOrLoadCache()` is already synchronized internally and is re-entrant-safe.

confirmed.title: TimeTrackingCheckinHandler and PostCommitTransitionHandler register a new Disposer callback on every commit, accumulating callbacks for the project's lifetime
confirmed.description: Lines 110-111: every call to `checkinSuccessful()` creates a new `CoroutineScope` and registers a new `Disposer.register(project as Disposable) { scope.cancel(...) }` lambda. The project's Disposer tree accumulates one entry per commit for the project's lifetime. After many commits, this is a slow memory leak (lambda references to dead scopes). `PostCommitTransitionHandlerFactory.kt` lines 47-48 has the same pattern. Additionally, `project as Disposable` is an unsafe cast — `Project` does not implement `Disposable` in all IntelliJ platform versions and can throw `ClassCastException` on some versions.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Instead of creating a scope per invocation, inject the project-level `CoroutineScope` via the service container or use `project.coroutineScope` (available from platform 2024.1+). If a per-invocation scope is needed, register it against a `Disposable` that itself is already project-scoped, not the project directly. Use `Disposer.newDisposable("time-tracking-commit")` registered under the project.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at lines 110-111 in TimeTrackingCheckinHandlerFactory.kt and lines 47-48 in PostCommitTransitionHandlerFactory.kt. Each `checkinSuccessful()` call registers a new lambda closure in the project's Disposer tree via `Disposer.register(project as Disposable)`. After N commits, there are N lambda entries in the tree even though each associated `CoroutineScope` has already completed. This is a slow memory leak (closure captures the dead scope). However: (a) each leaked entry is tiny (a few hundred bytes), (b) commits are infrequent human actions (not a tight loop), so in practice a developer would need thousands of commits in one session to see measurable pressure. The `project as Disposable` cast is safe for IntelliJ 2023+ (target is 2025.1+). Severity is Low rather than Medium given the extremely slow accumulation rate. The fix (using `project.coroutineScope` from platform 2024.1+) is correct and non-breaking.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Replace the per-invocation `CoroutineScope` + `Disposer.register` pattern in both files with `project.coroutineScope.launch(Dispatchers.IO) { ... }`. This eliminates the per-commit Disposer entry since `project.coroutineScope` is already lifecycle-managed by the platform. If the platform version constraint makes `project.coroutineScope` unavailable, use a single `@Service`-level scope injected via the service container instead.

confirmed.title: BranchChangeTicketDetector reads GitRepository.currentBranchName outside readAction, violating platform threading contract
confirmed.description: Line 50: `val changedRepo = gitRepos.find { it.currentBranchName == branchName }` reads `GitRepository.currentBranchName` directly in `branchHasChanged`. The IntelliJ platform requires that VCS model state (GitRepository state including `currentBranchName`) be accessed only under a read lock via `readAction { }` or `runReadAction { }`. Accessing it without a lock on the event dispatch thread can produce stale values; calling it from any non-EDT context (BranchChangeListener callbacks can arrive on various threads in platform 2024+) without a read lock is a potential threading violation that can cause `ConcurrentModificationException` in GitRepositoryImpl's internal mutable state.
confirmed.evidence:
val gitRepos = GitRepositoryManager.getInstance(project).repositories
val changedRepo = gitRepos.find { it.currentBranchName == branchName }
confirmed.suggestedFix: Wrap the repositories access and branch comparison in a `readAction`: `val changedRepo = readAction { gitRepos.find { it.currentBranchName == branchName } }`. Note `GitRepositoryManager.getInstance(project).repositories` at line 49 should also be inside the same `readAction` block.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: BranchChangeTicketDetector is registered as a project listener on the VCS_BRANCH_CHANGED topic (plugin-withGit.xml line 14-15). In IntelliJ platform 2024+, BranchChangeListener callbacks can arrive on a pooled background thread. Line 49 calls `GitRepositoryManager.getInstance(project).repositories` and line 50 reads `it.currentBranchName` without a `readAction {}` wrapper. GitRepository state is protected by the platform's read/write lock. However, in practice `currentBranchName` is a simple `@Volatile` field in GitRepositoryImpl and `GitRepositoryManager.repositories` returns an unmodifiable snapshot, making a `ConcurrentModificationException` extremely unlikely in current Git4Idea versions. The real risk is stale values on non-EDT threads, not a crash. Severity is Low rather than Medium because no user-visible misbehavior has been observed and the data accessed is append-only (single writer: platform VCS).
confirmed.recommendedAction: Wrap lines 49-50 in `readAction { ... }` as suggested. Low-risk correctness fix.

confirmed.title: Dead private field `availableSprints` — assigned but never read
confirmed.description: The field `private var availableSprints: List<JiraSprint> = emptyList()` (line 130) is written once at line 610 (`availableSprints = sprints`) but is never read anywhere in the class or its inner classes. The sprint selector (`sprintSelector`) and the load logic both drive off `sprintService.activeSprint` and the local `sprints` local variable. The field accumulates stale list state silently.
confirmed.evidence:
private var availableSprints: List<JiraSprint> = emptyList()  // line 130
...
availableSprints = sprints  // line 610 — only write, no reads
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete lines 130 and 610 (`private var availableSprints` declaration and the assignment in `populateSprintSelector`). No callers reference this field.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: `private var availableSprints: List<JiraSprint> = emptyList()` is declared at line 130. The only write is at line 610 inside `populateSprintSelector`. A grep over the entire file confirms zero read sites. The sprint selector and load logic operate entirely on the local `sprints` parameter and `sprintService.activeSprint`. The field accumulates stale state silently.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Delete the field declaration (line 130) and the assignment (line 610). Safe cleanup with no behavioral change.

confirmed.title: Dead local variable `finalBranchClient` — always equal to `branchClient`, assigned but never used differently
confirmed.description: At lines 1150-1154, `finalBranchClient` is set by a conditional whose both branches evaluate to the same value (`branchClient`). The comment even acknowledges 'create a new branch client isn't needed'. The variable is then passed to `branchingService.startWork(branchClient = finalBranchClient, ...)` at line 1188. Using `branchClient` directly is identical in effect; `finalBranchClient` adds zero semantic distinction and the comment is misleading.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete the `finalBranchClient` variable and pass `branchClient` directly at line 1188: `branchClient = branchClient`. The comment and the if-expression can be removed entirely.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: both branches of the if-expression at lines 1150-1154 evaluate to `branchClient` (the same local variable). The comment even admits 'create a new branch client isn't needed'. The variable `finalBranchClient` introduces noise without providing any semantic distinction. It is passed to `branchingService.startWork` at line 1188 but could be replaced directly with `branchClient`.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Delete the `finalBranchClient` variable and the if-expression (lines 1150-1154); pass `branchClient` directly at line 1188. Safe cleanup.

confirmed.title: Duplicate `isHeader` helper — identical implementation in two sibling UI classes
confirmed.description: `private fun isHeader(issue: JiraIssue): Boolean = issue.id.startsWith("header-")` appears identically at SprintDashboardPanel.kt:205 and TicketListCellRenderer.kt:55. Any change to the header-sentinel prefix (e.g., changing `"header-"` to `"section-"`) must be duplicated. The two classes are both `ui/` layer and work with the same `JiraIssue` DTO.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Extract a package-level top-level function `fun JiraIssue.isSectionHeader(): Boolean = id.startsWith("header-")` in a shared file (e.g., `ui/IssueUiUtils.kt`) and replace both private usages with it. This makes the convention a single source of truth.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: `private fun isHeader(issue: JiraIssue): Boolean = issue.id.startsWith("header-")` appears identically at SprintDashboardPanel.kt line 205 and TicketListCellRenderer.kt line 55. Both are private to their own class. Any future change to the 'header-' sentinel prefix must be made in two places. The finding is accurate.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Extract a package-level extension function (e.g., `fun JiraIssue.isSectionHeader() = id.startsWith("header-")` in a shared `IssueUiUtils.kt`) and update both call sites. Low-risk refactor.

confirmed.title: Fully dead service `TicketKeyCache` — registered in plugin.xml but never instantiated
confirmed.description: `TicketKeyCache` is a `@Service(Service.Level.PROJECT)` class registered at plugin.xml:237. No production code anywhere calls `TicketKeyCache.getInstance(...)` or any of its six public methods (`extractKeys`, `get`, `isValidated`, `getUnvalidated`, `validateAndCache`, `clear`). The DI registration keeps the class compiling but nothing ever resolves the service. The feature it intended to support (ticket-key hyperlinking in the detail panel description/comments) was never wired up. `JiraApiClient.validateTicketKeys` is also exclusively called from the dead `validateAndCache` (line 76) and has no other production callers.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete `TicketKeyCache.kt` and remove the `<projectService serviceImplementation="com.workflow.orchestrator.jira.service.TicketKeyCache"/>` entry from plugin.xml (line 237). Optionally prune `JiraApiClient.validateTicketKeys` and `TicketKeyInfo` if ticket-key hyperlinking is confirmed abandoned; otherwise wire the cache into the description/comment renderers in `TicketDetailPanel`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: `TicketKeyCache` is registered as a project service in plugin.xml line 237, but zero production callers exist. Grep across the full project finds no `TicketKeyCache.getInstance(...)` calls and no calls to any of its public methods (`extractKeys`, `get`, `isValidated`, `getUnvalidated`, `validateAndCache`, `clear`) outside the class itself. `JiraApiClient.validateTicketKeys` is also only referenced from within `TicketKeyCache.validateAndCache`. The service wastes a registration slot and the platform instantiates it unnecessarily on project open. Medium severity is appropriate because it represents real waste (service registered + instantiated on every project open) even though no functional harm occurs.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Delete TicketKeyCache.kt, remove the plugin.xml line 237 registration, and optionally prune `JiraApiClient.validateTicketKeys` and `TicketKeyInfo`. If ticket-key hyperlinking is planned, the cache should be wired into TicketDetailPanel description/comment renderers before being reinstated.

confirmed.title: Duplicate private `CacheEntry<T>` data class in `JiraServiceImpl` and `JiraSearchServiceImpl`
confirmed.description: An identical private generic `data class CacheEntry<T>(val value: T, val expiresAt: Long)` is defined independently inside both `JiraServiceImpl` (line 1282) and `JiraSearchServiceImpl` (line 65). A third, non-generic variant exists in `TicketTransitionServiceImpl` (line 80). The two generic variants are character-for-character identical.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Extract the generic variant to a package-level or `internal` class in the `jira.service` package (e.g., an `internal data class CacheEntry<T>(val value: T, val expiresAt: Long)` in a new `CacheUtils.kt`). Both services reference this one type. `TicketTransitionServiceImpl`'s non-generic variant can be left or migrated as a follow-up.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: `private data class CacheEntry<T>(val value: T, val expiresAt: Long)` is defined identically at JiraServiceImpl.kt line 1282 and JiraSearchServiceImpl.kt line 65. TicketTransitionServiceImpl.kt line 80 has a non-generic variant `private data class CacheEntry(val value: List<TransitionMeta>, val expiresAt: Long)`. The two generic variants are character-for-character identical as stated. The duplication is a maintenance hazard but causes no runtime problem today.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Extract an `internal data class CacheEntry<T>(val value: T, val expiresAt: Long)` to a shared `jira/service/CacheUtils.kt` file and replace both private usages. The TicketTransitionServiceImpl non-generic variant can stay or be migrated as a follow-up.

confirmed.title: Unused `project: Project` constructor parameter in `SavedFiltersSection`
confirmed.description: The `project: Project` parameter at line 33 is annotated `@Suppress("UNUSED_PARAMETER")` and is not referenced anywhere inside the class. The section was designed to be callback-driven (the parent `SprintDashboardPanel` owns the `JiraService` calls and passes results via `update()`), so the `project` parameter was never needed.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Remove the `project: Project` parameter from the `SavedFiltersSection` constructor and update the call site in `SprintDashboardPanel` (line 421) to `SavedFiltersSection { filter -> onFilterClicked(filter) }`. No functionality changes.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. Line 33 of SavedFiltersSection.kt shows `@Suppress("UNUSED_PARAMETER") project: Project` is never referenced in the class body. The companion object at lines 103-119 contains the load-bearing `shouldShowSection()` static method and `MAX_DESC_CHARS` constant, but these are accessed directly as static members (SprintFavouritesRenderingTest calls `SavedFiltersSection.shouldShowSection(...)` without instantiating the class), so they are unaffected by removing the constructor parameter. The call site at SprintDashboardPanel.kt:421 currently passes `project` and would need to be updated to `SavedFiltersSection { filter -> onFilterClicked(filter) }`. No logic change, no test breakage.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Remove the `project: Project` parameter from the `SavedFiltersSection` constructor and update the call site at SprintDashboardPanel.kt:421.

confirmed.title: Empty `companion object` in `SprintDashboardPanel` — no-op declaration
confirmed.description: Line 1236 declares `companion object` with no members. An empty `companion object` in Kotlin is unnecessary: it generates a synthetic inner class `Companion` in the bytecode for no benefit. No external code accesses `SprintDashboardPanel.Companion` or `SprintDashboardPanel` via static members.
confirmed.evidence:
// SprintDashboardPanel.kt:1236
companion object
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete the empty `companion object` declaration at line 1236.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. Line 1236 of SprintDashboardPanel.kt is an empty `companion object` with zero members. No external code accesses `SprintDashboardPanel.Companion`. Deleting it removes a no-op synthetic inner class from the bytecode with zero functional impact. Note that the companion object in SavedFiltersSection (lines 103-119) is NOT empty — it holds `MAX_DESC_CHARS` and the `@JvmStatic shouldShowSection()` method used by tests, so the finding is correctly scoped to SprintDashboardPanel only.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Delete the empty `companion object` declaration at SprintDashboardPanel.kt:1236.

confirmed.title: `WorklogSection` bypasses `JiraService.getWorklogs` and calls raw `JiraApiClient` directly
confirmed.description: `WorklogSection.loadWorklogs` calls `jiraServiceImpl.getApiClient()` (line 62) then `apiClient.getWorklogs(issueKey)` (line 71), consuming the raw transport DTO `JiraWorklogResponse`/`JiraWorklog`. The core `JiraService` interface already exposes `getWorklogs(issueKey): ToolResult<List<WorklogData>>` (implemented in `JiraServiceImpl` at line 450 with proper field mapping and `.summary`). The three sibling sections (`DevStatusSection`, `ChangelogSection`, `LinkedDocsSection`) all correctly use `project.getService(JiraService::class.java)`. `WorklogSection` is the sole outlier, duplicating DTO-to-display field mapping and coupling the UI to the raw transport layer.
confirmed.evidence:
val jiraServiceImpl = JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project)  // line 61
val apiClient = jiraServiceImpl.getApiClient()  // line 62
...
val result = apiClient.getWorklogs(issueKey)  // line 71 — raw ApiResult<JiraWorklogResponse>
confirmed.suggestedFix: Replace lines 61-89 with `project.getService(JiraService::class.java).getWorklogs(issueKey)`, branch on `ToolResult.isError`, and render from `List<WorklogData>` (mirroring the pattern in `ChangelogSection.loadHistory`). Remove the `import com.workflow.orchestrator.jira.service.JiraServiceImpl` and the raw DTO import `JiraWorklog`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. WorklogSection.kt lines 61-62 retrieve `JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project).getApiClient()`, then line 71 calls `apiClient.getWorklogs(issueKey)` returning raw `ApiResult<JiraWorklogResponse>`. `JiraService.getWorklogs(issueKey): ToolResult<List<WorklogData>>` exists (JiraServiceImpl line 450). Sibling sections ChangelogSection uses `project.getService(JiraService::class.java)` correctly. The fix is safe but non-trivial: `renderWorklogs()` at line 102 accepts `List<JiraWorklog>` and accesses `.timeSpentSeconds`, `.author?.displayName`, `.started`, `.comment` — these fields would need to be mapped from `WorklogData`. The suggested fix undersells the effort required. Classified as Low (cleanup) rather than High because worklogs are a non-critical read-only display feature and the raw client call works correctly in practice.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Replace the `getApiClient()` chain in `loadWorklogs()` with `project.getService(JiraService::class.java).getWorklogs(issueKey)` and rewrite `renderWorklogs()` to accept `List<WorklogData>` instead of `List<JiraWorklog>`.

confirmed.title: WorklogSection bypasses JiraService contract via raw JiraApiClient
confirmed.description: WorklogSection retrieves JiraApiClient directly through JiraServiceImpl.getApiClient() and calls api.getWorklogs() on it, bypassing the JiraService interface entirely. The interface already exposes getWorklogs(issueKey) returning ToolResult<List<WorklogData>>, so this leaks the internal HTTP client into the UI layer and skips ToolResult error handling conventions.
confirmed.evidence:
val jiraServiceImpl = JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project)
val apiClient = jiraServiceImpl.getApiClient()
...
val result = apiClient.getWorklogs(issueKey)
confirmed.suggestedFix: Replace the two-step lookup with project.getService(JiraService::class.java).getWorklogs(issueKey). The return type changes from ApiResult<JiraWorklogResponse> to ToolResult<List<WorklogData>>; adjust renderWorklogs() to consume WorklogData instead of the DTO. This removes the only remaining ui/ → api/ direct dependency.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real and is the architectural framing of the same issue as JIRA-CLE-9. WorklogSection.kt lines 61-71 bypass the `JiraService` interface and call raw `JiraApiClient` directly from the UI layer, violating the layering rule (ui/ must not reach api/ directly). `getApiClient()` is not part of the `JiraService` interface — it is an implementation-detail method on `JiraServiceImpl`. The finding is correctly flagged, though its claimed severity of High is slightly overstated: the bypass causes no data loss, no security issue, and the duplicate field mapping is purely a maintenance concern. Severity reassessed to Medium (architecture + maintainability, not user-visible wrong behavior).
confirmed.recommendedAction: Route through `project.getService(JiraService::class.java).getWorklogs(issueKey)` and adapt `renderWorklogs()` to `WorklogData`. This removes the only remaining ui/ → api/ direct dependency in the jira module.

confirmed.title: SprintDashboardPanel.onFilterClicked bypasses JiraService via raw JiraApiClient
confirmed.description: onFilterClicked() retrieves the raw JiraApiClient from JiraServiceImpl.getApiClient() and calls api.searchByJql(jql, 50) directly. JiraService.searchTickets(jql, maxResults) is the correct service-layer entry point and already performs JQL validation (blank, length, control chars). Using the raw client skips those guards and the ToolResult contract.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Replace api.searchByJql with service.searchTickets(jql, 50). Convert the returned ToolResult<List<JiraTicketData>> back to List<JiraIssue> only if needed by the cell renderer, or preferably refactor the list model to accept JiraTicketData (the shared core model). Remove the getApiClient() null-guard and the getApiClient() call entirely.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. SprintDashboardPanel.kt lines 711-718 call `service.getApiClient()` then `api.searchByJql(jql, 50)` directly. `JiraService.searchTickets(jql, maxResults)` at JiraServiceImpl line 1177-1220 has explicit JQL guards (blank check, MAX_JQL_LENGTH=2000, control-character check). The comment at line 709-710 acknowledges the bypass is intentional because the list model uses `List<JiraIssue>` (DTO) not `List<JiraTicketData>` (core model), and the cell renderer `TicketListCellRenderer` expects `JiraIssue`. The JQL risk is reduced since user-clicked saved filter JQL comes from Jira's own API, not agent input. Severity downgraded from High to Medium: the guards matter most for agent-originated JQL, and a proper fix requires either mapping `JiraTicketData` → `JiraIssue` or refactoring the list model.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Either refactor the list model to accept `JiraTicketData` directly, or add an inline JQL validation before calling `api.searchByJql`. Using `service.searchTickets()` directly would require a `JiraTicketData` → `JiraIssue` conversion shim.

confirmed.title: SprintTabProvider wires raw JiraApiClient into SprintService and BranchingService, creating unregistered service objects
confirmed.description: SprintTabProvider calls JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project).getApiClient() and passes the raw client into manually-constructed SprintService and BranchingService objects that are not registered as project services. These objects hold long-lived HTTP clients and coroutine scopes tied to the raw API client, meaning lifecycle is ad-hoc. The CLAUDE.md convention requires service-layer objects to be project-level IntelliJ services so the platform manages their lifecycle.
confirmed.evidence:
val jiraService = JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project)
val apiClient = jiraService.getApiClient()
    ?: return EmptyStatePanel(...)
val sprintService = SprintService(apiClient)
val branchingService = BranchingService(project, apiClient, activeTicketService)
confirmed.suggestedFix: Register SprintService and BranchingService as @Service(Service.Level.PROJECT) with lazy JiraApiClient resolution (same pattern as JiraServiceImpl.client), or inject them from plugin.xml. SprintTabProvider should retrieve them via project.getService(). This ensures lifecycle is tied to project open/close, not to tool-window panel construction.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real: SprintTabProvider.kt lines 26-32 manually construct `SprintService(apiClient)` and `BranchingService(project, apiClient, activeTicketService)` without registering them as platform services. Neither class has `@Service`, and neither appears in plugin.xml. However, severity is significantly lower than claimed: both classes have no coroutine scopes, no `Disposable` implementation, and no long-lived state that would leak across project open/close (SprintService holds a cached `List<JiraIssue>` and a `JiraSprint?`; BranchingService is stateless). The lifecycle risk cited in the finding does not materialize because these objects are field-held by `SprintDashboardPanel`, which is itself `Disposable` and GC-eligible after tool window close. The CLAUDE.md convention requiring platform-managed lifecycle applies primarily to services holding coroutine scopes or HTTP clients as long-lived resources — SprintService wraps an existing shared `JiraApiClient` reference. Severity reassessed from High to Low.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Low priority. Optionally register SprintService as a project-level @Service that lazily resolves JiraApiClient, and retrieve it via project.getService(). This is a maintenance improvement, not an urgent fix.

confirmed.title: TicketTransitionServiceImpl secondary constructor has a circular dependency on JiraServiceImpl concrete class
confirmed.description: The platform-invoked secondary constructor calls JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project).getApiClient() to obtain the JiraApiClient. This couples a core-interface implementation (TicketTransitionService) to a sibling concrete class (JiraServiceImpl), forming a within-module dependency chain that makes both services harder to test in isolation and violates the convention that services resolve through interfaces.
confirmed.evidence:
constructor(project: Project, cs: CoroutineScope) : this(
    api = JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project).getApiClient()
        ?: JiraApiClient(baseUrl = "", tokenProvider = { null }),
confirmed.suggestedFix: Resolve the JiraApiClient by injecting it from plugin.xml using the <serviceImplementation> constructor parameter or by having the platform inject a JiraApiClient-provider service. Alternatively, have TicketTransitionServiceImpl construct its own JiraApiClient from CredentialStore and PluginSettings directly (same pattern as JiraSearchServiceImpl.createClient()), removing the JiraServiceImpl dependency.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. TicketTransitionServiceImpl.kt lines 66-74 show the secondary constructor directly calls `JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project).getApiClient()`. This creates a concrete-to-concrete dependency between two sibling service implementations in the same `:jira` module. The class KDoc at line 37-44 acknowledges this design choice ('no duplicate HTTP clients are created'). The fallback sentinel at lines 68-71 is a defensive measure. No circular dependency in the JVM sense (neither extends the other; both are constructed by the platform DI sequentially). The claimed fix (injecting from plugin.xml or constructing JiraApiClient from CredentialStore like JiraSearchServiceImpl) is technically correct and would make TicketTransitionServiceImpl independently testable, but current tests likely already provide the primary constructor directly. Medium severity confirmed.
confirmed.recommendedAction: For testability, prefer constructing JiraApiClient directly from CredentialStore/PluginSettings in the secondary constructor (mirroring JiraSearchServiceImpl.createClient()), removing the JiraServiceImpl dependency.

confirmed.title: Multiple UI and listener files call JiraServiceImpl.getInstance() (concrete class) instead of project.getService(JiraService::class.java)
confirmed.description: DevStatusSection, SprintDashboardPanel (loadFavouriteFilters/onFilterClicked), JiraWorkflowConfigurable (searchBoards, getFields), BranchChangeTicketDetector, PostCommitTransitionHandlerFactory, and TimeTrackingCheckinHandlerFactory all call JiraServiceImpl.getInstance() to obtain the service, creating direct compile-time dependencies on the concrete implementation class from UI/listener layers. The JiraService interface exposes all needed methods (getTicket, getFields, searchBoards, getFavouriteFilters, getFilter, getFullDevStatus). Only invalidateFieldsCache and getApiClient are impl-specific; the former should be added to the interface or moved to a settings-specific seam.
confirmed.evidence:
val service = JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project)  // DevStatusSection.kt:60
val result = JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project).getTicket(ticketId)  // BranchChangeTicketDetector.kt:79
val result = JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project).searchBoards(searchText)  // JiraWorkflowConfigurable.kt:176
confirmed.suggestedFix: Replace all JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project) calls outside the service package with project.getService(JiraService::class.java). For invalidateFieldsCache (settings page only), expose it on JiraService interface, or have JiraWorkflowConfigurable inject JiraServiceImpl via the same cast pattern already used in JiraServiceImpl.getInstance() companion (safe within the module). This decouples UI/listener layers from the implementation class.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: The finding is real: DevStatusSection.kt line 60, BranchChangeTicketDetector.kt line 79, SprintDashboardPanel.kt lines 670/688, JiraWorkflowConfigurable.kt lines 176/452/399 all call JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project). However, the severity must be downgraded from Medium. JiraServiceImpl.getInstance() is itself implemented as `project.getService(JiraService::class.java) as JiraServiceImpl` (JiraServiceImpl.kt line 1852), so the DI container is still used — the concrete class is introduced only via an explicit cast in the companion, not by bypassing the container. This is intentional: callers that need impl-specific methods (invalidateFieldsCache at line 399 in JiraWorkflowConfigurable, which is not on the JiraService interface) genuinely need the cast. Callers that only need interface methods (DevStatusSection, BranchChangeTicketDetector, SprintDashboardPanel) could indeed use project.getService(JiraService::class.java) instead and would be cleaner, but no functional bug or brittleness results because the companion method already does the same lookup internally. The suggested fix is architecturally sound (decouple UI/listener layers from the concrete class) but carries no correctness risk, no test failures, and no user-visible impact. isHarmfulToFix=false.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Low-priority cleanup: replace JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project) calls in DevStatusSection, BranchChangeTicketDetector, SprintDashboardPanel, PostCommitTransitionHandlerFactory, and TimeTrackingCheckinHandlerFactory with project.getService(JiraService::class.java). For JiraWorkflowConfigurable's invalidateFieldsCache call (line 399), either add the method to the JiraService interface or keep the one necessary cast there. Not urgent.

confirmed.title: TicketDetailPanel and QuickCommentPanel use plain javax.swing.JButton instead of JetBrains platform button
confirmed.description: addTransitionAndWatchButtons() in TicketDetailPanel instantiates javax.swing.JButton directly for the Transition and Watch buttons (lines 237 and 254). QuickCommentPanel also declares sendButton = JButton(AllIcons.Actions.Execute). The project convention requires JetBrains components only. Plain JButton does not respect the IDE theme (light/dark borders, focus handling, DPI scaling) the way IntelliJ ActionButton or JBButton / platform-themed JButton wrappers do.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Replace javax.swing.JButton with com.intellij.ui.components.JBButton (or use ActionButton/ActionToolbar for icon-only cases). For the Transition and Watch buttons in TicketDetailPanel, JBButton is the minimal fix. For the icon-only sendButton in QuickCommentPanel, consider ActionButton wrapping an AnAction, which also integrates with the platform's presentation/tooltip system.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: TicketDetailPanel.kt line 237 instantiates `javax.swing.JButton("${issue.fields.status.name} ▾")` and line 254 instantiates `javax.swing.JButton("👁 Watch")`. QuickCommentPanel.kt line 42 declares `private val sendButton = JButton(AllIcons.Actions.Execute)` (import is plain javax.swing.JButton at line 26). The project convention says JetBrains components only (JBButton). Plain JButton does not adapt to IDE theme scaling, font DPI, or focus handling correctly on HiDPI or non-default themes. This is a real violation of the stated UX convention. However, it is a visual/polish issue only — no crash, no data loss, no incorrect behavior. isHarmfulToFix=false: replacing with JBButton does not break any passing test or load-bearing fallback.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Replace javax.swing.JButton with com.intellij.ui.components.JBButton in TicketDetailPanel (lines 237, 254) and QuickCommentPanel (line 42). For the icon-only sendButton consider ActionButton wrapping an AnAction for full platform integration.

confirmed.title: JiraWorkflowConfigurable uses plain javax.swing.JTextField and JComboBox inside the Kotlin UI DSL panel
confirmed.description: The settings page uses javax.swing.JTextField for boardSearchField (line 118), ticketKeyRegexField (line 149), epicManualField, and acceptanceManualField (lines 363, 388); it also uses javax.swing.JComboBox for epicCombo and acceptanceCombo (lines 355, 380). These plain Swing components do not inherit IDE theming (placeholder text, border scaling, validation decorators). The Kotlin UI DSL's textField() and comboBox() helpers should be used instead, which already respect the IntelliJ Platform look and feel.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Use JBTextField for the text fields (com.intellij.ui.components.JBTextField) and ComboBox from the IntelliJ Platform (com.intellij.openapi.ui.ComboBox) for the combo boxes, or bind them using the Kotlin UI DSL row { textField().bindText(...) } / row { comboBox(...).bindItem(...) } pattern already used elsewhere in the same file. This is especially important for the manual field fallbacks (epicManualField, acceptanceManualField) as they already reference the JiraWorkflowConfigurable's scope.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: JiraWorkflowConfigurable.kt uses javax.swing.JTextField at lines 118 (boardSearchField), 149 (regexField), 363 (epicManualField), 388 (acceptanceManualField), and javax.swing.JComboBox at lines 121 (boardComboBox), 355 (epicCombo), 380 (acceptanceCombo). While JBTextField and com.intellij.openapi.ui.ComboBox are the JetBrains-native replacements, using the plain Swing variants inside a Kotlin UI DSL panel is common in IntelliJ plugin code for components not bound via DSL bindText()/bindItem(). The functional impact is limited to minor visual inconsistency on non-default themes or HiDPI displays. No crash, no data loss. The board combo and epic/acceptance combos are used with SimpleListCellRenderer.create() and bindBoundedWidth() helpers that work with both types. isHarmfulToFix=false: substituting JBTextField/ComboBox does not break behavior.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Low-priority cleanup: replace javax.swing.JTextField with JBTextField (com.intellij.ui.components.JBTextField) for boardSearchField, regexField, epicManualField, acceptanceManualField; replace javax.swing.JComboBox with com.intellij.openapi.ui.ComboBox for boardComboBox, epicCombo, acceptanceCombo. This is purely cosmetic but keeps the codebase consistent with the JetBrains-only UI convention.

confirmed.title: SprintService.loadSprintIssues — API error from board discovery not propagated to test coverage
confirmed.description: SprintService.discoverBoard (lines 171-209) has a specific branch at line 204 where `getBoards()` returns `ApiResult.Error` — it logs and returns null, which causes `loadSprintIssues` to return `NOT_FOUND`. The SprintServiceTest only covers the 'no boards in success response' case (line 63: `ApiResult.Success(emptyList())`), not the 'API itself failed' case. Similarly, the fallback path `loadScrumBoardIssues` → `loadKanbanBoardIssues` when `getActiveSprints` returns an `ApiResult.Error` (lines 68-73) is exercised by zero tests — the existing fallback test (line 71) only covers the `ApiResult.Success(emptyList())` case.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add to SprintServiceTest: (1) `loadSprintIssues returns error when getBoards itself fails` — stub `getBoards` to return `ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.NETWORK_ERROR, "Cannot reach Jira")`, assert result is `isError`. (2) `loadSprintIssues falls back to board issues when getActiveSprints returns API error` — stub `getActiveSprints(1)` to return `ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.SERVER_ERROR, "500")`, stub `getBoardIssues(1)` to return issues, assert success and `activeSprint == null`. Both use `runTest` + MockK, no platform required.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed by reading SprintServiceTest.kt and SprintService.kt. The SprintServiceTest has 6 tests, none of which cover: (1) getBoards() returning ApiResult.Error — SprintService.kt lines 204-207 log the error and return null, causing loadSprintIssues to return NOT_FOUND; the test at line 62 only tests Success(emptyList()). (2) getActiveSprints(boardId) returning ApiResult.Error in loadScrumBoardIssues — SprintService.kt lines 68-73 fall back to loadKanbanBoardIssues; the test at line 71 uses ApiResult.Success(emptyList()) not ApiResult.Error. Both are real untested branches. Severity is Medium not High: the code paths are simple delegations with correct fallback logic already written; the risk is regression if someone modifies the fallback logic without noticing the gap, but there is no current observable bug.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add two SprintServiceTest cases: (1) stub getBoards() to return ApiResult.Error and assert loadSprintIssues returns isError; (2) stub getActiveSprints(1) to return ApiResult.Error, stub getBoardIssues(1) to return issues, and assert success with activeSprint == null.

confirmed.title: SprintService.loadAvailableSprints and loadIssuesForSprint — completely untested
confirmed.description: SprintService exposes two public methods with no test at all: `loadAvailableSprints(boardId)` (lines 106-115) calls `getActiveSprints` and `fetchRecentClosedSprints`, merges results, and sorts by endDate. `loadIssuesForSprint(sprintId, allUsers)` (lines 160-167) updates `cachedIssues` and returns the sprint issues. Both are called from UI panels and are state-mutating. `fetchRecentClosedSprints` itself runs a pagination loop against `getClosedSprints` and reads/writes `SprintPaginationCache` — that loop has no test coverage whatsoever.
confirmed.evidence:
suspend fun loadAvailableSprints(boardId: Int): List<JiraSprint> {
    val activeResult = apiClient.getActiveSprints(boardId)
    val active = (activeResult as? ApiResult.Success)?.data ?: emptyList()
    val recentClosed = fetchRecentClosedSprints(boardId, count = 3)
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add SprintServiceTest cases: (1) `loadAvailableSprints returns active sprints followed by sorted recent closed` — stub `getActiveSprints` returning one sprint, stub `getClosedSprints` returning a single `isLast=true` page with two closed sprints. Assert the returned list is active-first then sorted by `endDate` descending. (2) `loadIssuesForSprint returns issues and updates cachedIssues` — stub `getSprintIssues(99, false)`, assert result and `getCachedIssues()`. (3) `loadAvailableSprints handles getClosedSprints API error gracefully` — stub `getClosedSprints` returning an error, assert `loadAvailableSprints` returns just the active sprint without throwing.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed by reading SprintServiceTest.kt. No test references loadAvailableSprints, loadIssuesForSprint, or fetchRecentClosedSprints. SprintService.kt lines 106-115 (loadAvailableSprints), 129-154 (fetchRecentClosedSprints with its pagination loop), and 160-167 (loadIssuesForSprint) are entirely uncovered. fetchRecentClosedSprints is a non-trivial pagination loop that reads/writes SprintPaginationCache and has a while(true) break condition — it is a meaningful coverage gap. These are called from UI panels and are state-mutating (cachedIssues). Severity is Medium: the logic works based on code inspection, but the gap leaves pagination regressions silent.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add SprintServiceTest cases for loadAvailableSprints (active + sorted closed), loadIssuesForSprint (stub getSprintIssues, assert cachedIssues updated), and fetchRecentClosedSprints error handling (getClosedSprints returning error should return empty list without throwing).

confirmed.title: SprintPaginationCache — entirely untested, including corruption recovery and atomic-move path
confirmed.description: SprintPaginationCache is a singleton object with file-based persistence, an in-memory lock, LRU-style pagination hints, corrupt-file recovery, and atomic-move writes. None of these paths have a single test. Key missing scenarios: (1) first call returns 0 when no file exists; (2) `saveCachedStartAt` then `getCachedStartAt` round-trips correctly with `pageSize` subtraction; (3) corrupt JSON on disk triggers delete-and-reset instead of crashing; (4) `getCachedStartAt` on a board with `lastStartAt < pageSize` returns 0 (coerceAtLeast guard).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a `SprintPaginationCacheTest` using a `@TempDir` to redirect `cacheDir`. Tests: (1) `getCachedStartAt returns 0 on first call` (no file). (2) `save then getCachedStartAt returns lastStartAt minus pageSize, floored at 0`. (3) `getCachedStartAt with lastStartAt < pageSize returns 0`. (4) `corrupt file is deleted and cache returns 0` — write invalid JSON to the file, then call `getCachedStartAt`, assert 0 and file is gone. All are pure Kotlin/JUnit5 with temp dirs — no IntelliJ platform needed.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: no test file references SprintPaginationCache anywhere. The class (SprintPaginationCache.kt lines 1-129) is a singleton object with file-based persistence, synchronized cache, corrupt-file recovery, and atomic-move writes. All paths described in the finding are real and untested. However, severity should be Low rather than Medium: SprintPaginationCache is a pure performance hint cache — a wrong value produces at most an extra API round-trip on the first call after a reset. The data it caches is non-critical pagination offsets; the fallback (startAt=0) is handled correctly at line 53 via the null coalescing return 0. Corrupt file is caught by a try/catch at line 102 that deletes and returns fresh CacheData. No data loss or incorrect behavior results from cache misses.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add a SprintPaginationCacheTest with a @TempDir. Cover: getCachedStartAt returns 0 on first call (no file); save/load round-trip with pageSize subtraction; lastStartAt < pageSize returns 0; corrupt JSON file is deleted and 0 returned. Pure Kotlin/JUnit5, no IntelliJ platform needed.

confirmed.title: TicketKeyCache — entirely untested including LRU eviction, regex fallback, and API error path
confirmed.description: TicketKeyCache has zero tests. Critical uncovered paths: (1) `extractKeys` with the default regex against multi-key text, with the MAX_REGEX_MATCHES=200 cap, and with a syntactically invalid custom regex (PatternSyntaxException fallback). (2) `validateAndCache` when `validateTicketKeys` returns `ApiResult.Error` — invalid keys must still not appear in cache, and the method must not throw. (3) `evictIfNeeded` at exactly MAX_SIZE+1 entries — eviction uses `cache.keys().toList()` ordering which is non-deterministic in ConcurrentHashMap, so the eviction count must match regardless of which key is dropped.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add `TicketKeyCacheTest` (pure Kotlin, no platform): (1) `extractKeys finds all Jira-style keys in text` — text with 3 keys + noise. (2) `extractKeys falls back to default regex on invalid pattern` — inject invalid pattern via ConnectionSettings mock or test constructor. (3) `extractKeys caps at MAX_REGEX_MATCHES` — pass text with 201 keys, assert `size == 200`. (4) `validateAndCache caches valid keys and nulls for invalid ones` — stub `validateTicketKeys` returning only subset, assert missing keys map to `null`. (5) `validateAndCache API error leaves cache empty for that batch`. (6) `evictIfNeeded keeps cache at MAX_SIZE after overfill` — add 501 entries, assert `cache.size == 500`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: no test file references TicketKeyCache. TicketKeyCache.kt lines 35-53 (extractKeys with regex fallback and MAX_REGEX_MATCHES cap), lines 70-90 (validateAndCache including ApiResult.Error path), and lines 97-103 (evictIfNeeded) are all untested. The finding is accurate. Severity is Low not Medium: TicketKeyCache is used for hyperlinking ticket keys in descriptions — a UI enhancement. On extraction failure or API error, the service degrades gracefully: extractKeys falls back to DEFAULT_REGEX (line 50), validateAndCache on error logs and returns without caching (lines 86-88), and the cache serves as an optimization only. The eviction logic is simple (take(toRemove) from a ConcurrentHashMap snapshot) and already functions correctly without a test. No user-impacting bug exists.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add a TicketKeyCacheTest (pure Kotlin, no platform): cover extractKeys with valid keys, invalid custom regex fallback, MAX_REGEX_MATCHES cap, validateAndCache on ApiResult.Success and ApiResult.Error, and evictIfNeeded at MAX_SIZE+1 entries.

confirmed.title: AttachmentDownloadService — entirely untested, including error responses, path-traversal guard, and parallel concurrency limit
confirmed.description: AttachmentDownloadService has no tests. Key untested paths: (1) `downloadAttachment` with blank content URL returns null (line 51). (2) Non-2xx HTTP response deletes temp dir and returns null (lines 67-70). (3) Path-traversal guard: a filename like `../../etc/passwd` is blocked at line 82-85 (canonicalPath check). (4) Empty response body returns null (lines 73-76). (5) `downloadAll` concurrency limit (Semaphore(3)) with 5+ attachments. (6) `downloadThumbnail` cache hit returns same object without making a network call.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add `AttachmentDownloadServiceTest` using `MockWebServer` + `@TempDir`: (1) `downloadAttachment returns null for blank URL`. (2) `downloadAttachment returns null on 4xx response`. (3) `downloadAttachment path-traversal filename returns null` — use filename `../../secret`. (4) `downloadAttachment happy path writes bytes to target dir`. (5) `downloadAll with 5 attachments makes at most 3 concurrent requests` — inject a latched mock to observe concurrency. (6) `downloadThumbnail caches result and skips second HTTP request`. Use `runTest` + `Dispatchers.IO` override via `TestDispatcher`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: no test file references AttachmentDownloadService anywhere. AttachmentDownloadService.kt covers: blank URL guard (line 50-52), non-2xx response handling (lines 67-70), empty body guard (lines 73-76), path-traversal guard (lines 82-85), concurrency limit via Semaphore(3) (line 119), and thumbnail LRU cache (line 138). All are untested. Severity is Medium (not Low) because of the security-relevant path-traversal guard at lines 82-85: a regression there (e.g., someone simplifying the filename sanitization without running tests) would allow a malicious Jira attachment to write files outside the target directory. The path-traversal check uses canonicalPath comparison after replacing ".." with "_" — this deserves a pinning test. The concurrency limit and cache are correctness concerns but not security-impacting.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add AttachmentDownloadServiceTest using MockWebServer + @TempDir. Priority: (1) path-traversal filename returns null — this has security implications and must be pinned; (2) blank URL returns null; (3) non-2xx returns null; (4) happy path writes bytes; (5) downloadThumbnail caches result and skips second HTTP call. The concurrency limit test is desirable but lower priority.

confirmed.title: JiraApiClient.searchIssues — validation branches (too-long text, control chars, key-pattern JQL switch) have no tests
confirmed.description: JiraApiClient.searchIssues (lines 104-127) has three defensive branches with zero test coverage: (1) `text.length > 500` returns `VALIDATION_ERROR` immediately without an HTTP call. (2) `text.any { it.code < 32 }` returns `VALIDATION_ERROR` for control characters. (3) `looksLikeKey` regex (line 115) switches the JQL to `text ~ "…" OR key = "…"` form — only the non-key free-text form is implicitly exercised via other tests. The internal `escapeJql` function (lines 993-1001) is also completely untested for its reserved-character escaping logic.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add to JiraApiClientTest (using MockWebServer): (1) `searchIssues returns VALIDATION_ERROR and no HTTP call for text over 500 chars`. (2) `searchIssues returns VALIDATION_ERROR and no HTTP call for text with control character`. (3) `searchIssues uses key OR text JQL form when text matches Jira key pattern` — assert the request path contains `key+%3D`. (4) Add a standalone `EscapeJqlTest` (pure Kotlin): verify `+`, `-`, `"`, `(` each escape to `\+`, `\-`, `\"`, `\(`, and that plain alphanumeric text is unchanged.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real coverage gap. JiraApiClient.searchIssues (lines 104-127) has three defensive branches with no tests: (1) text.length > MAX_SEARCH_TEXT_LENGTH (line 108) returns VALIDATION_ERROR with no HTTP call; (2) text.any { it.code < 32 } (line 111) returns VALIDATION_ERROR; (3) looksLikeKey regex (line 115) switches JQL to key= OR text~ form. A search across all test files found zero references to searchIssues, escapeJql, or MAX_SEARCH_TEXT_LENGTH. The JiraServiceImplSearchTicketsValidationTest tests searchTickets (a different JiraServiceImpl method) not the JiraApiClient.searchIssues method directly. The escapeJql function at lines 993-1001 is also entirely untested. Severity is Medium per the finding but is better assessed as Low: the validation logic itself is straightforward defensive guards that are very unlikely to regress silently, and callers pass user-typed text so the validation being untested doesn't constitute an observable correctness defect in production. Adding tests is pure upside with no risk.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add MockWebServer tests to a new JiraApiClientSearchIssuesTest: (1) text over 500 chars returns VALIDATION_ERROR with no HTTP request; (2) text with a control character (code < 32) returns VALIDATION_ERROR; (3) text matching [A-Z][A-Z0-9]+-\d+ produces JQL containing 'key =' in the request URL. Add a standalone pure-Kotlin EscapeJqlTest verifying that +, -, ", ( escape to \+, \-, \", \( and plain alphanumeric is unchanged.

confirmed.title: JiraApiClient.getWorklogs — mid-pagination API error path not tested
confirmed.description: JiraApiClient.getWorklogs (lines 567-588) uses a `while` loop and short-circuits with `return r` on the first `ApiResult.Error` from any page (line 578). This path — where page 1 succeeds but page 2 returns a 4xx/5xx — is not tested. The existing JiraServiceImplTest tests only cover the 2-page success case and a single-page parse. A mid-pagination auth failure would silently return an error discarding all already-collected entries, which callers may not handle gracefully.
confirmed.evidence:
is ApiResult.Error -> return r
is ApiResult.Success -> r.data
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add to JiraServiceImplTest (using MockWebServer): `getWorklogs returns error immediately when second page fails` — enqueue a valid first page (total=200, worklogs=[100 items]), then enqueue a 401 response. Assert result is `isError` with `ErrorType.AUTH_FAILED`. Also add: `getWorklogs returns empty list when server returns total=0` — enqueue `{"total":0, "worklogs":[]}`, assert empty `isSuccess`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real coverage gap. JiraApiClient.getWorklogs (lines 567-588) paginates via a while loop and at line 578 does 'is ApiResult.Error -> return r', discarding any already-collected entries and returning the error immediately. The existing JiraServiceImplTest covers the 2-page success case (page 1: 100 items, page 2: 2 items) and the single-page author-username case, but no test enqueues a successful first page followed by a 4xx/5xx second page. The mid-pagination error path is real and observable: a 401 on page 2 silently discards the collected page-1 entries and returns an error, which callers may not handle gracefully (e.g., showing an error to the user when partial data was available). Severity is correctly Medium.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add to JiraServiceImplTest (or a new JiraApiClientWorklogPaginationTest): enqueue a valid first page (total=200, 100 worklogs) then a 401 response; assert result.isError with ErrorType.AUTH_FAILED. Optionally also test total=0 returns an empty success list.

confirmed.title: JiraTransitionResponseParser.parse — malformed/missing 'to' field and empty transitions body not tested
confirmed.description: JiraTransitionResponseParser.parse uses `!!` dereferences on `n["to"]!!`, `to["id"]!!`, and `to["name"]!!` (lines 28-30) and on `n["id"]!!` and `n["name"]!!` (lines 38-39). If the Jira server returns a transition without a `to` field (which can happen for internal admin transitions), the parser will throw a `NullPointerException` inside `getTransitions`, crashing the caller. The test suite only covers well-formed transitions; no test verifies behavior when a required field is absent.
confirmed.evidence:
private fun parseTransition(n: JsonObject): TransitionMeta {
    val to = n["to"]!!.jsonObject
    val toStatus = StatusRef(
        id = to["id"]!!.jsonPrimitive.content,
        name = to["name"]!!.jsonPrimitive.content,
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add to JiraTransitionResponseParserTest: (1) `parse skips or throws on transition missing to field` — body with a transition where `to` is absent; assert either an exception is caught (and document the decision) or that the entry is skipped. If the policy is to throw, wrap the call site in a try/catch and convert to `ApiResult.Error`. (2) `parse returns empty list for empty transitions array` — body `{"transitions":[]}`. (3) `parse returns empty list when transitions key is missing entirely` — body `{}`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real crash risk. JiraTransitionResponseParser.parseTransition (lines 27-43) uses n["to"]!!.jsonObject (line 28), to["id"]!! (line 30), to["name"]!! (line 31), n["id"]!! (line 37), and n["name"]!! (line 38). Kotlin's !! throws NullPointerException when the value is null or absent. JiraTransitionResponseParserTest only tests well-formed transitions with all fields present. A Jira server returning a transition without a 'to' field (internal admin transitions are documented to sometimes lack this) would NPE inside the arr.map{} call at line 24, propagating up to getTransitions and crashing the caller. No try/catch wraps the parse call site. The suggested fix (decide throw-and-catch vs skip policy) is straightforward and non-breaking.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Either add a try/catch around parseTransition and skip malformed entries (logging a warning), or wrap the parse call site in getTransitions with try/catch converting to ApiResult.Error. Then add JiraTransitionResponseParserTest cases: (1) transition missing 'to' field — assert either exception caught and empty list returned or ApiResult.Error at call site; (2) empty transitions array returns empty list; (3) missing 'transitions' key returns empty list (already handled by the ?: return emptyList() at line 23).

confirmed.title: JiraApiClient.getRawString — 429 RATE_LIMITED and non-JSON content-type (PARSE_ERROR) not tested in any helper path
confirmed.description: The `checkJsonContentType` guard on `getRawString` (lines 337-362) maps a 200 + non-JSON Content-Type (e.g. `application/xml` or `text/plain`) to `PARSE_ERROR`. This path is tested only via the `text/html` → `AUTH_FAILED` path in JiraApiClientTest (for the typed `get` helper). No test covers `getRawString` receiving a `text/xml` or `application/xml` body, nor does any test exercise the `429 RATE_LIMITED` response code for any method despite it being a distinct branch in all five HTTP helpers.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add to JiraApiClientTest: (1) `getRawString 200 with application/xml content-type maps to PARSE_ERROR` — enqueue a 200 + `Content-Type: application/xml` + XML body; assert `ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR`. (2) `getBoards 429 maps to RATE_LIMITED` — enqueue `MockResponse().setResponseCode(429)`; assert `ErrorType.RATE_LIMITED`. The 429 test can use any method since all map via the shared `get` helper.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real coverage gap, but severity should be Low rather than Medium. The checkJsonContentType function (lines 855-873) maps a 200 + non-JSON Content-Type to PARSE_ERROR for all HTTP helpers including getRawString. JiraApiClientTest tests the text/html -> AUTH_FAILED path only for getCurrentUser (which uses the typed get helper). JiraApiClientSameOriginTest tests getRawString only for same-origin guarding. No test exercises: (a) getRawString returning 200 with application/xml Content-Type -> PARSE_ERROR; (b) 429 -> RATE_LIMITED for any method. Both branches exist and work correctly in code (getRawString line 351; checkJsonContentType line 865); tests would merely provide regression coverage. 429 is rare in practice for a self-hosted Jira DC instance, and the non-JSON Content-Type path is well-covered by the shared checkJsonContentType function tested via other helpers. The risk of silent regression is real but low.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add to JiraApiClientTest or JiraApiClientSameOriginTest: (1) getRawString with 200 + Content-Type: application/xml maps to PARSE_ERROR; (2) getBoards (or any method using the typed get helper) with 429 maps to ErrorType.RATE_LIMITED.

confirmed.title: BranchingService.fetchLinkedBranches — dev-status fallback to Bitbucket search has no test
confirmed.description: BranchingService.fetchLinkedBranches (lines 59-80) has two branches: primary dev-status API (returns non-empty) and fallback Bitbucket branch-name search when dev-status returns empty or an error. Neither branch has a test. The fallback is important for repositories not linked to Jira's dev-status system, and any regression would silently return an empty list to the Start Work dialog's branch picker.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add to BranchingServiceTest: (1) `fetchLinkedBranches returns dev-status branches when available` — stub `getDevStatusBranches(issue.id)` to return two branches, stub `allBranches` as empty; assert the returned list matches the dev-status data. (2) `fetchLinkedBranches falls back to Bitbucket name filter when dev-status returns empty` — stub `getDevStatusBranches` to return empty list, provide `allBranches` with one matching and one non-matching entry; assert only the matching branch is returned. Both are pure MockK + `runTest`, no platform needed.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real coverage gap. BranchingServiceTest only tests the 'no silent fallback' contract for useExistingBranch and startWork (the localVcsRootPath mismatch -> NOT_FOUND path). The fetchLinkedBranches method (lines 59-80) has two branches — primary dev-status API (non-empty result) and Bitbucket name-search fallback (empty/error dev-status) — neither has any test. A regression in the fallback filter logic (e.g., case sensitivity change in the contains() call at line 74) would silently return an empty list to the Start Work dialog's branch picker. Severity is correctly Low since the logic is simple (a filter and a map), the fallback is informational rather than destructive, and the impact is UX degradation (empty picker) not data loss.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add to BranchingServiceTest using MockK: (1) fetchLinkedBranches returns dev-status branches when getDevStatusBranches returns non-empty; (2) fetchLinkedBranches falls back to Bitbucket name filter when dev-status returns empty, returning only branches whose displayId contains issue.key. Both are pure runTest+MockK, no platform needed.

confirmed.title: Merge API result swallowed — UI buttons permanently locked on merge failure
confirmed.description: At lines 1227–1238, `PrActionService.merge()` returns `ApiResult<Unit>` but the return value is never captured. The `scope.launch` block catches only `Exception` (which `ApiResult.Error` is not), then unconditionally disables all action buttons. When merge fails (network error, 409 stale version, auth failure, merge precondition violation, etc.) the user sees no error notification AND permanently loses the Merge/Approve/Decline/Needs-Work buttons for the current PR view. The only recovery is closing and reopening the PR tab.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Capture the return value: `val result = PrActionService.getInstance(project).merge(...)`. Inside `invokeLater`, branch on `result`: on `ApiResult.Success` disable buttons (and optionally refresh); on `ApiResult.Error` show a notification via `WorkflowNotificationService.notifyError` and leave buttons enabled so the user can retry. Pattern mirrors the correct `declineButton` handler at lines 1263–1281.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at lines 1227-1238. `PrActionService.merge()` returns `ApiResult<Unit>` but the return value is discarded. The `invokeLater` block unconditionally disables all four action buttons (merge/approve/needsWork/decline) regardless of whether the merge succeeded or failed. The `catch (e: Exception)` at line 1239 only intercepts thrown exceptions — `ApiResult.Error` is a return value, not a thrown exception, so API failures (409 stale-version, 401 auth, network error) silently fall through and leave the user with a fully-disabled button bar and no error message. Compare with `declineButton` at lines 1262-1278, which correctly captures the result and branches on `result.isError`. Downgraded from Critical to High: the 'permanently locked' claim is slightly overstated since refreshing or reopening the PR tab recovers the buttons, but the failure path is genuinely broken for the normal use case. Fix: capture the `ApiResult<Unit>` and mirror the `declineButton` pattern.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Capture the return value of `merge()` and mirror the `declineButton` pattern at lines 1262-1278: on `ApiResult.Error`, call `WorkflowNotificationService.getInstance(project).notifyError(...)` and leave buttons enabled; on `ApiResult.Success`, disable them as today.

confirmed.title: saveDescription result swallowed — UI shows saved description even on server failure
confirmed.description: In `DescriptionSubPanel.saveDescription()` (lines 1975–1986), `PrActionService.updateDescription()` returns `ApiResult<Unit>` but the result is discarded. The `invokeLater` block always re-enables the Update button and calls `showDescription(newDescription)`, so the panel displays the unsaved text as though it was persisted. If the PUT returns a 409 stale-version conflict, network error, or auth failure, the user has no indication of failure and the description on the server remains unchanged.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Capture the result and branch: on `ApiResult.Success` call `showDescription(newDescription)` as now; on `ApiResult.Error` re-show the original description (`showDescription(currentDescription)`) and surface the error message via `WorkflowNotificationService.notifyError`. Same pattern as `saveTitleEdit` (lines 1563–1578) which correctly reverts and shows a notification on failure.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at lines 1975-1986. `PrActionService.updateDescription()` returns `ApiResult<Unit>` (verified at PrActionService.kt line 307) but `saveDescription()` discards the result. The `invokeLater` block unconditionally sets `currentDescription = newDescription` and calls `showDescription(newDescription)`, so the panel displays stale text as saved even when the PUT returns a 409 or network error. `PrActionService.updateDescription` goes through `modifyPullRequest` which already retries on 409, so true 409 failures are genuine server-side rejections. Compare with `saveTitleEdit` at lines 1563-1578 which correctly handles `ApiResult.Success` vs `ApiResult.Error` and reverts the title label on failure. Fix is safe: on error, revert to `currentDescription` (the pre-edit value, which is not yet overwritten) and show a notification.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Capture the `ApiResult<Unit>` from `updateDescription()` inside the `scope.launch`. In `invokeLater`, on `ApiResult.Success` keep current behavior; on `ApiResult.Error` call `showDescription(currentDescription)` (revert) and show a notification via `WorkflowNotificationService`. Pattern mirrors `saveTitleEdit` at lines 1563-1578.

confirmed.title: BitbucketServiceImpl.updatePrDescription lacks 409 stale-version retry (agent path)
confirmed.description: The agent-facing `updatePrDescription` (lines 764–793) performs a naive GET+PUT without 409-retry. The parallel methods `addReviewer` (line 300) and `updatePrTitle` (line 343) both check `isPrimaryRepo` and route to `PrActionService` (which uses `modifyPullRequest` with a built-in 409 retry) or `modifyPullRequestForAgent`. `updatePrDescription` was never updated to follow this routing pattern, so any concurrent edit between the GET and the PUT silently loses the agent's update with a 409 error.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Apply the same single-repo/multi-repo routing pattern: for the primary repo, delegate to `PrActionService.updateDescription(prId, description)` which already uses `modifyPullRequest` with retry. For non-primary repos, call `modifyPullRequestForAgent(api, prId, projectKey, repoSlug, "update description") { current -> updateDescriptionMutator(current, description) }`. The `updateDescriptionMutator` pure function already exists in `PrActionService.kt`.
