confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at lines 764-793 in BitbucketServiceImpl.kt. `updatePrDescription` does a bare GET+PUT with no 409-retry and no single-repo / multi-repo routing. `addReviewer` (line 300) and `updatePrTitle` (line 343) both check `isPrimaryRepo` and route: primary-repo → `PrActionService` (which uses `modifyPullRequest` with built-in 409 retry); non-primary → `modifyPullRequestForAgent` (also uses `modifyPullRequest`). `updatePrDescription` was never updated to follow this pattern: for the primary repo it bypasses `PrActionService.updateDescription` and does a raw GET+PUT, so it has no 409 protection; for non-primary repos it also does a raw GET+PUT. `updateDescriptionMutator` internal function already exists in `PrActionService.kt` (line 536) so the routing logic is straightforward to add. Medium severity is correct: this is an agent-path function, not triggered by typical UI interaction.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add the same `isPrimaryRepo` guard: for primary repo, delegate to `PrActionService.getInstance(project).updateDescription(prId, description).toToolResult(...)`. For non-primary repos, call `modifyPullRequestForAgent(api, prId, projectKey, repoSlug, "update description") { current -> updateDescriptionMutator(current, description) }`. The `updateDescriptionMutator` function is already internal in PrActionService.kt (line 536); make it internal-accessible or inline the equivalent lambda.

confirmed.title: CommentsViewModel.reply: Long comment ID silently truncated to Int without overflow guard
confirmed.description: At line 95, `reply(parentCommentId: Long, ...)` calls `service.replyToComment(parentCommentId = parentCommentId.toInt(), ...)`. Bitbucket comment IDs are stored as `Long` in the server API and can exceed `Int.MAX_VALUE` (2,147,483,647) on busy projects. The overflow produces a negative Int, which causes the reply to target the wrong comment ID or receive a 404/400 from Bitbucket. `ActivitySubPanel.buildReplyLink()` has an explicit SEC-24 guard for this exact case (lines 2397–2399) but `CommentsViewModel.reply` does not.
confirmed.evidence:
suspend fun reply(parentCommentId: Long, text: String): Boolean {
    val result = service.replyToComment(
        ...
        parentCommentId = parentCommentId.toInt(),  // truncates silently
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add the same guard as `ActivitySubPanel` before the conversion: `if (parentCommentId < Int.MIN_VALUE || parentCommentId > Int.MAX_VALUE) { lastError = "Comment ID $parentCommentId exceeds Int range"; fire(); return false }`. Alternatively, update `BitbucketService.replyToComment` to accept `Long` throughout and remove the conversion (the deeper architectural fix).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at CommentsViewModel.kt line 95: `parentCommentId.toInt()` with no overflow guard. `ActivitySubPanel.buildReplyLink` at lines 2397-2399 has the explicit SEC-24 guard (`if (rawId < Int.MIN_VALUE || rawId > Int.MAX_VALUE) return ...`) before the same conversion. The interface chain (`BitbucketService.replyToComment`, `PrActionService.replyToComment`, `BitbucketBranchClient.replyToComment`) all take `Int`, so the type mismatch propagates through the entire stack. However, Bitbucket Server comment IDs are auto-increment integers that would require billions of comments to exceed `Int.MAX_VALUE`; in practice this overflow is exceedingly unlikely on any real Bitbucket Server instance. The inconsistency with the SEC-24 guard is real but the exploitable surface is theoretical. Downgraded from Medium to Low. The deeper architectural fix (changing the stack to `Long`) is out of scope, but adding the ViewModel guard is a safe incremental improvement.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add the same guard as ActivitySubPanel SEC-24 before the `toInt()` conversion at CommentsViewModel.kt line 95: `if (parentCommentId < Int.MIN_VALUE || parentCommentId > Int.MAX_VALUE) { lastError = "Comment ID $parentCommentId exceeds Int range"; fire(); return false }`. The full architectural fix (changing `replyToComment` parameter to `Long` throughout) is a larger refactor and can be tracked separately.

confirmed.title: currentPr read on Dispatchers.IO without @Volatile — stale value visible on ARM
confirmed.description: `currentPr` (line 100) is a plain `var` written on the EDT by `showPr()`, `showPrDetail()`, and `refreshCurrentPr()`, but read on `Dispatchers.IO` by two `scope.launch {}` blocks: (1) `renderPrHeader`'s coroutine at line 1427 (`val pr = currentPr`) which determines the approve/unapprove toggle label, and (2) `FilesSubPanel.openDiffViewer` at line 2581 (`val pr = currentPr ?: return@launch`) which reads `pr.toRef`/`pr.fromRef`. The JVM memory model does not guarantee visibility of non-volatile writes across threads. On ARM processors (macOS M1/M2/M3, which is the dev/CI environment) a CPU can serve a stale cached value, causing either wrong approve-button state or the diff viewer silently aborting on a false null read. `cachedUsername` at line 1456 correctly uses `@Volatile` but `currentPr` and `currentPrId` do not.
confirmed.evidence:
private var currentPr: BitbucketPrDetail? = null  // line 100, no @Volatile
...
scope.launch {  // Dispatchers.IO
    val currentUsername = resolveCurrentUsername()
    val pr = currentPr  // line 1427: cross-thread read without visibility guarantee
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add `@Volatile` to `currentPr` and `currentPrId`: `@Volatile private var currentPr: BitbucketPrDetail? = null` and `@Volatile private var currentPrId: Int? = null`. This is the same fix applied to `cachedUsername` (line 1456). No logic changes are needed; `@Volatile` establishes the happens-before edge between the EDT write and the IO read.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. `currentPr` at PrDetailPanel.kt line 100 is `private var currentPr: BitbucketPrDetail? = null` with no `@Volatile`. It is written on the EDT by `showPr()`/`showPrDetail()`/`refreshCurrentPr()` and read on Dispatchers.IO at two sites: line 1427 (`val pr = currentPr` inside a `scope.launch` on Dispatchers.IO that determines approve-button label) and line 2581 (`val pr = currentPr ?: return@launch` in `FilesSubPanel.openDiffViewer`). `cachedUsername` at line 1456 already uses `@Volatile` with an explicit KDoc comment explaining the EDT↔IO visibility guarantee. Under the JVM memory model, reads on a non-volatile field from a different thread than the writer are not guaranteed to see the latest value; on ARM/JVM (macOS M-series) the processor's relaxed memory model makes this practically observable. The fix (`@Volatile` on `currentPr` and `currentPrId`) is minimal and correct with no behavioral side-effects.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add `@Volatile` to `currentPr` (line 100) and `currentPrId` (line 99): `@Volatile private var currentPrId: Int? = null` and `@Volatile private var currentPr: BitbucketPrDetail? = null`. Same pattern already applied to `cachedUsername` at line 1456.

confirmed.title: Dead inline Create PR form in PrDetailPanel (~300 lines of unreachable code)
confirmed.description: The entire CARD_CREATE card — `showCreateForm()` (line 466), `buildCreatePanel()` (line 555), `submitCreatePr()` (line 682), `renderCreateReviewers()` (line 760), `showCreateReviewerPopup()` (line 792), and all the fields they depend on (`createSourceBranchLabel`, `createTargetBranchCombo`, `createTitleField`, `createDescriptionArea`, `createReviewersPanel`, `createAddReviewerLink`, `createButton`, `createBackLabel`, `selectedReviewerUsernames`, `selectedReviewerDisplayNames`, `createRepoConfig`) — is dead code. `showCreateForm()` is the only public entry point to this card and it has zero callers outside the file; the CLAUDE.md and code comments confirm the only live Create PR path is `CreatePrLauncherImpl → CreatePrDialog`.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete: `showCreateForm`, `buildCreatePanel`, `submitCreatePr`, `renderCreateReviewers`, `showCreateReviewerPopup`, `CARD_CREATE`, and all ~15 associated fields (`createRepoConfig`, `createSourceBranchLabel`, `createTargetBranchCombo`, `createTitleField`, `createDescriptionArea`, `createReviewersPanel`, `createAddReviewerLink`, `createButton`, `createBackLabel`, `selectedReviewerUsernames`, `selectedReviewerDisplayNames`). Remove `add(createPanel, CARD_CREATE)` from `init`. Remove the `import com.workflow.orchestrator.core.settings.RepoConfig` import if it becomes unused.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed dead code. `grep -rn 'showCreateForm' pullrequest/src/` returns only the definition at line 466 — zero callers. The CLAUDE.md for :pullrequest explicitly states 'Single entry point to PR creation: PR tab's "+Create PR" button (via CreatePrLauncher.getInstance()?.launch(project, scope)). Build tab's entry was removed in the 2026-04-27 PrBar redesign.' `createRepoConfig` is already referenced at line 103 (class field) and used only within the dead `showCreateForm`/`buildCreatePanel`/`submitCreatePr` path. `buildCreatePanel` and `submitCreatePr` are private so they are also only reachable from `showCreateForm`. The ~300 lines inflate compile time and obscure the active code path. Cleanup is safe since there are no callers. Severity downgraded from Medium to Low: dead code is a maintainability issue, not a correctness/behavior issue.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Delete `showCreateForm`, `buildCreatePanel`, `submitCreatePr`, `renderCreateReviewers`, `showCreateReviewerPopup`, the `CARD_CREATE` constant, and all ~15 associated fields listed in the finding. Remove `add(createPanel, CARD_CREATE)` from `init`. Verify `createRepoConfig` at line 103 (class field) becomes unused and remove it. Clean up any now-unused imports.

confirmed.title: Dead AUTHOR/REVIEWER branches in fetchAllPages and effectively-unused username param
confirmed.description: `fetchAllPages` is called from exactly one site (line 170) with `role = "ALL"`. The `"AUTHOR"` and `"REVIEWER"` arms of the `when` expression (lines 250–251) are therefore dead — they were the pre-dashboard per-repo loop replaced by `fetchDashboardPrs` but never removed. Consequently, the `username` parameter (line 243) is also never read at any live call site (always passed as `null` and only consumed in the dead arms).
confirmed.evidence:
val results = fetchAllPages(client, projectKey, repoSlug, null, "ALL")  // line 170
// inside fetchAllPages:
"AUTHOR" -> client.getMyPullRequests(...)  // line 250, dead
"REVIEWER" -> client.getReviewingPullRequests(...)  // line 251, dead
confirmed.suggestedFix: Collapse the `when` to just `client.getRepoPullRequests(projectKey, repoSlug, currentState, start, 25)` (the `else` branch). Remove the `username: String?` parameter from `fetchAllPages`. No behavior changes at any live call site.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. `fetchAllPages` is invoked at exactly one site (PrListService.kt line 170) with `role = "ALL"` and `username = null`. The `"AUTHOR"` and `"REVIEWER"` branches at lines 250-251 are dead; they were the pre-dashboard per-repo role-filtered calls replaced by `fetchDashboardPrs` (which correctly uses `BitbucketBranchClient.getDashboardPullRequests`) as documented in the CLAUDE.md Bitbucket DC 9.4 audit changes section. The `username: String?` parameter is passed as `null` at the only call site and only consumed in those dead branches. Collapsing the `when` to just the `else` branch and removing the `username` parameter is a safe refactor with no behavioral change at any live call site. Severity is Low: the dead branches are not executable, they pose no correctness risk.

Remove the `username: String?` parameter from `fetchAllPages` and update the one call site at line 170 accordingly.

confirmed.title: Unfinished public method resolveDefaultReviewersForBranch never called from outside its file
confirmed.description: `CreatePrPrefetch.resolveDefaultReviewersForBranch` (line 106) is a `suspend fun` marked public on the companion object. Its KDoc says the dialog "re-runs this on target-change via `reloadDefaultReviewersForBranch`", but no such call exists in `CreatePrDialog` or anywhere else. It has zero callers outside its own file. The `@Suppress("UNUSED_PARAMETER")` on `project` (line 107) is an additional signal that the wiring was never completed.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Either wire the call from `CreatePrDialog.onModuleChanged` / target-field `DocumentListener` (which the KDoc promises), or delete the method if the re-resolution feature is no longer planned. The method itself is correct; this is purely an unused-export cleanup.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. `grep -rn 'resolveDefaultReviewersForBranch' pullrequest/src/` returns only three hits, all within `CreatePrPrefetch.kt` (definition + 2 log.warn lines inside the implementation). Zero callers from `CreatePrDialog`, `CreatePrLauncherImpl`, or any other file. The KDoc at line 95-105 describes wiring that was never implemented: 'the dialog re-runs this on target-change via reloadDefaultReviewersForBranch' — no such method exists in `CreatePrDialog`. The `@Suppress("UNUSED_PARAMETER")` on `project` at line 107 is an additional code signal that the wiring was planned but never completed. The method itself is functionally correct; it is unused public API. Low severity: no behavioral impact, purely a dangling export.

If wired, also remove the `@Suppress("UNUSED_PARAMETER")` on `project` once it is used.

confirmed.title: Force-dereference !! on ToolResult.data after isError guard in AiReviewTabPanel
confirmed.description: Line 85 uses `commits.data!!` after `if (commits.isError) "" else commits.data!!…`. The same pattern was explicitly called out as a bug (`pullrequest:F-6 — forced !! NPE`) and fixed in `PrDetailPanel.kt` (lines 880–890) with a null-check instead. `ToolResult.data` is typed as `T` but is effectively nullable when `isError=true`, and the `isError=false` contract can be violated. The identical defect was already fixed elsewhere in the same module, so this is an oversight.
confirmed.evidence:
val commits = service.getPullRequestCommits(prId, repoName = null)
if (commits.isError) "" else commits.data!!.firstOrNull()?.id.orEmpty()  // line 85
confirmed.suggestedFix: Replace with `commits.data?.firstOrNull()?.id.orEmpty()`. Remove the `isError` guard entirely, since `?.` handles both the null case and the empty-success case uniformly.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at AiReviewTabPanel.kt line 85: `commits.data!!` is used after `if (commits.isError) "" else commits.data!!…`. ToolResult.data is typed as `T?` (nullable) per ToolResult.kt line 26, and the KDoc says non-null when !isError is only a 'convention', not a type-level guarantee. The identical pattern was explicitly fixed in PrDetailPanel.kt lines 880-890 (the F-6 fix). However, the entire expression is wrapped in `runCatching { }` at line 83, so any NPE from `data!!` is caught and falls back to an empty `toHash` string — users see degraded (floating-anchor) AI review push, not a crash. The defect is real and inconsistent with the adjacent fix, but the `runCatching` eliminates the crash risk. The fix (`commits.data?.firstOrNull()?.id.orEmpty()` — dropping the isError guard) is safe and correct.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Replace line 85 with `commits.data?.firstOrNull()?.id.orEmpty()` and remove the `isError` branch. Non-harmful cleanup that matches the F-6 fix already applied in PrDetailPanel.

confirmed.title: Redundant popup.cancel() in showAddReviewerPopup — the callee already cancels before calling back
confirmed.description: `showUserSearchPopup` calls `popup.cancel()` at line 1727 and then immediately invokes `onUserSelected(selected, popup)`. The `showAddReviewerPopup` lambda (the only caller of `showUserSearchPopup` that calls `popup.cancel()` inside the callback) duplicates this at line 1621, so the popup is cancelled twice. The second `cancel()` on a JBPopup that is already cancelled is benign but misleading.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Remove the `popup.cancel()` call at line 1621 in `showAddReviewerPopup`. The closure still receives `popup` (needed as the `JBPopup` parameter in the lambda signature), but no action on it is needed.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: `showUserSearchPopup` calls `popup.cancel()` at line 1727 before invoking the `onUserSelected` callback, and `showAddReviewerPopup` (the sole callback wired at line 1619) calls `popup.cancel()` again at line 1621. The second cancel on an already-cancelled JBPopup is a no-op on the Swing side but is misleading. Removing line 1621's redundant `popup.cancel()` is safe — the popup parameter is still received in the lambda (the signature requires it) but nothing else in the lambda body uses `popup`. No tests or behavior changes.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Remove the `popup.cancel()` call at PrDetailPanel.kt line 1621. Low-priority cleanup only.

confirmed.title: Dead cachedVersion parameter in PrActionService.decline()
confirmed.description: The `cachedVersion: Int = 0` parameter (line 196) is documented as "retained only for API compatibility" but is never read inside the function body — the implementation always fetches a fresh version via `fetchAndDecline`. The sole caller at `PrDetailPanel.kt:1263` passes no argument (uses the default). The parameter is pure dead weight that contradicts the project's `feedback_no_compat_shims.md` convention.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Remove the `cachedVersion` parameter from `decline`. Update the KDoc to remove the outdated "retained only for API compatibility" sentence. The sole caller needs no change as it already passes no argument.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: `cachedVersion: Int = 0` at PrActionService.kt line 196 is documented as 'retained only for API compatibility' but the implementation always delegates to `fetchAndDecline` which fetches the version internally — `cachedVersion` is never referenced in the function body. The sole caller at PrDetailPanel.kt line 1263 passes no argument (uses the default). Removing the parameter and updating the KDoc is a clean non-breaking change — the caller uses no argument so no call-site change is needed.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Remove the `cachedVersion: Int = 0` parameter from PrActionService.decline() and delete the outdated 'retained only for API compatibility' KDoc sentence.

confirmed.title: BitbucketServiceImpl.declinePullRequest bypasses PrActionService's 409-retry logic
confirmed.description: The agent-facing `declinePullRequest` (lines 741–762) does a single GET then a bare `api.declinePullRequest(…, prDetail.version)` call. `PrActionService.decline()` (service/PrActionService.kt:196–243) has an explicit 409 detect-and-retry path with cache invalidation that was introduced as an audit fix. The agent tool path silently misses that retry, so a concurrent PR update between the GET and the POST will give the agent a spurious error rather than transparently retrying. `BitbucketServiceImpl.updatePrDescription` (lines 764–792) has the same gap: it calls `api.updatePullRequest` directly instead of `api.modifyPullRequest` (which owns the retry). This is inconsistent with the comment at line 291–308 in the same file that explicitly routes `addReviewer` through `PrActionService.addReviewer` (primary-repo case) or through `modifyPullRequestForAgent` (non-primary case) for exactly this reason.
confirmed.suggestedFix: For `declinePullRequest`: mirror the `addReviewer` pattern. For primary-repo case, delegate to `PrActionService.getInstance(project).decline(prId)` and map the result to `ToolResult<Unit>`. For non-primary repo, add a private `declinePullRequestForAgent` helper analogous to `modifyPullRequestForAgent` that uses `BitbucketBranchClient.modifyPullRequest`-style retry. For `updatePrDescription`: route through `api.modifyPullRequest(RepoCoords(…), prId) { current -> updateDescriptionMutator(current, description) }` (the mutator already exists as a top-level function in PrActionService.kt) so the retry fires on 409.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: BitbucketServiceImpl.declinePullRequest (lines 741-762) performs a single GET then calls `api.declinePullRequest(...)` directly at line 754, bypassing PrActionService.decline()'s 409-detect-and-retry path (PrActionService.kt lines 196-243). Similarly, `updatePrDescription` (lines 764-792) calls `api.updatePullRequest` at line 785 directly instead of routing through `api.modifyPullRequest` with its retry-on-409. By contrast, `addReviewer` (lines 300-337) and `removeReviewer` (lines 852-881) both explicitly route through `modifyPullRequestForAgent` / `PrActionService` for exactly this reason (with detailed comments at lines 288-299). The inconsistency is real: a concurrent PR update between the agent's GET and its POST produces a spurious error rather than a transparent retry. This is the direct agent-facing path — a user asks the agent to decline or update a description and it fails under concurrent edits when the UI path would succeed.
confirmed.recommendedAction: For declinePullRequest: delegate to PrActionService.getInstance(project).decline(prId) on the primary repo; add a private fetchAndDeclineForAgent helper for non-primary repos that mirrors the retry pattern. For updatePrDescription: replace the direct api.updatePullRequest call with api.modifyPullRequest using the updateDescriptionMutator already defined in PrActionService.kt.

confirmed.title: PrDetailPanel.showCreateForm and submitCreatePr bypass the shared BitbucketBranchClientCache
confirmed.description: Three sites in `PrDetailPanel` call `BitbucketBranchClient.fromConfiguredSettings()` directly (lines 528, 714, 1706) to construct ad-hoc clients, bypassing the `BitbucketBranchClientCache` that all PR services share. The cache exists specifically to (a) reuse HTTP connections and OkHttp thread pools across the entire PR module, and (b) guarantee that a (url, client) pair is always consistent under concurrent URL changes (see `BitbucketBranchClientCache` lines 29–43). Ad-hoc calls always spin up a new OkHttp client and thread pool on each use, and can produce a client built for an old URL if the settings change between the `ConnectionSettings.getInstance()` read inside `fromConfiguredSettings()` and the client's first real call. A fourth site in `resolveCurrentUsername` (line 1460) does the same. `CreatePrDialog.searchUsers` (line 683) falls back to `fromConfiguredSettings()` when `BitbucketBranchClient.forRepo` returns null, which is acceptable, but the create-PR submit path at line 1035 uses `BitbucketBranchClient.forRepo` correctly.
confirmed.evidence:
528:            val client = BitbucketBranchClient.fromConfiguredSettings() ?: return@launch
714:            val client = BitbucketBranchClient.fromConfiguredSettings() ?: return@launch
1460:        val client = BitbucketBranchClient.fromConfiguredSettings() ?: return null
confirmed.suggestedFix: Inject a `BitbucketBranchClientCache` instance into `PrDetailPanel` (constructed once in the class body, like every other PR service) and replace the three `fromConfiguredSettings()` call sites with `clientCache.get()`. For `resolveCurrentUsername` (line 1460) this also means the username-resolution client is always consistent with the project/repo used everywhere else in the panel.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: PrDetailPanel.kt calls `BitbucketBranchClient.fromConfiguredSettings()` at lines 528, 714, 1460, and 1706, bypassing the BitbucketBranchClientCache that all PR services use. The cache class (BitbucketBranchClientCache.kt lines 29-43) exists specifically to atomically enforce (url, client) consistency under concurrent URL changes. These ad-hoc calls each spin up a fresh OkHttp client and thread pool, and can produce a client built for a stale URL in the gap between the ConnectionSettings read and first use. PrDetailPanel has no `clientCache` field — the oversight is structural. The fix (adding a `private val clientCache = BitbucketBranchClientCache()` field and replacing the four call sites) is safe and follows the exact pattern used by all other PR services.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add `private val clientCache = BitbucketBranchClientCache()` to PrDetailPanel and replace all four `BitbucketBranchClient.fromConfiguredSettings()` call sites (lines 528, 714, 1460, 1706) with `clientCache.get()`.

confirmed.title: PrListService.getClient() throws instead of returning null, risking uncaught exception in polling
confirmed.description: `getClient()` (line 96–97) calls `error("Bitbucket URL not configured")` when the URL is blank, throwing `IllegalStateException`. `refresh()` at line 107 calls `getClient()` only after an early-exit check on `bitbucketUrl.isBlank()` (line 102), so the happy path is safe. However, the `SmartPoller` action lambda at line 54 calls `refresh()` directly, and any race where the URL becomes blank between the poller's scheduling and the actual call will propagate the exception up through the poller, potentially cancelling or corrupting the `SupervisorJob` child for that poll cycle. The CLAUDE.md threading contract states polling runs under `CoroutineScope+SupervisorJob` to isolate failures; an uncaught `IllegalStateException` escapes that isolation. All other PR services return `null` from their `getClient()` equivalents.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Change `getClient()` to `return clientCache.get()` (nullable), and update `refresh()` to treat a null client as a no-op early-return with a log message (same pattern as `PrDetailService.getDetail`, `PrActionService.approve`, etc.). The `bitbucketUrl.isBlank()` pre-check in `refresh()` can be removed since `clientCache.get()` already returns null when the URL is blank.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: PrListService.getClient() at lines 96-97 throws `IllegalStateException` via `error(...)` when the URL is blank, while PrActionService.getClient() (line 40) returns null. The finding's claim that this would 'cancel or corrupt the SupervisorJob child' is overstated: SmartPoller's action lambda is wrapped in a try-catch (lines 55-67 of SmartPoller.kt) that catches all `Exception` types including `IllegalStateException`, logs a warning, and increases backoff — the job is NOT cancelled. The real race window (URL becomes blank between line 102's isBlank check and line 107's getClient call) is extremely narrow in practice. The issue is an inconsistency with the module's own pattern (every other service returns null) and does add unnecessary noise to logs, but it does not produce the crash/corruption described. Fixing it to match the nullable pattern is safe.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Change PrListService.getClient() to return `clientCache.get()` (nullable) and update refresh() to return early on null client, matching the pattern used by PrActionService and the rest of the PR services. Severity should be treated as Low given SmartPoller catches the exception anyway.

confirmed.title: PersistentStateComponent bitbucketUsername written from IO dispatcher, not EDT
confirmed.description: The comment at lines 117–119 claims the mutation was moved to `Dispatchers.EDT`. The actual code at lines 119–121 wraps only the `connSettings.bitbucketUsername = result.data` assignment inside `withContext(Dispatchers.EDT)`. However, `connSettings` is a direct reference to the mutable state object obtained at line 100 with `ConnectionSettings.getInstance().state`. The CLAUDE.md threading rule (D2 in the comment) requires that `PersistentStateComponent` state mutations happen on the EDT. While the assignment is dispatched to EDT, the surrounding code still reads `connSettings` from the IO thread (line 111: `connSettings.bitbucketUsername.takeIf {…}`). A concurrent EDT write (e.g., the user saving Settings) could produce a torn read on the IO thread. The fix is narrowly scoped but the underlying issue is that `connSettings` is a mutable state snapshot used across two threads.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Read `ConnectionSettings.getInstance().state.bitbucketUsername` freshly on the EDT inside the `withContext(Dispatchers.EDT)` block rather than holding a reference captured on IO. Better yet, use the canonical pattern: call `ConnectionSettings.getInstance().bitbucketUsername` (via the ApplicationService accessor) on EDT. The outer IO-thread check for a saved username (`connSettings.bitbucketUsername.takeIf { it.isNotBlank() }`) should access the state via a snapshot read: `withContext(Dispatchers.EDT) { ConnectionSettings.getInstance().state.bitbucketUsername }` before the suspend network call.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: at PrListService.kt line 100, `connSettings` is captured as a direct mutable state reference on the IO dispatcher. Line 111 reads `connSettings.bitbucketUsername` on IO, while line 120 writes `connSettings.bitbucketUsername` on EDT. These are concurrent accesses to the same mutable object across two thread pools. However, the practical risk is narrow: this code path only executes once (on first refresh, before `cachedUsername` is set), the field is a simple String property on a POJO, and the worst case is a torn read (the IO thread sees a partially-written string) not data loss or corruption. The write-to-EDT fix was already partially applied (line 119 dispatch); the remaining gap is the IO-thread read. Fixing it by reading `ConnectionSettings.getInstance().state.bitbucketUsername` freshly on EDT before the network call is safe, but the claim of 'High' severity overstates a narrow single-pass race on a string field.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Read the cached username check via `withContext(Dispatchers.EDT) { ConnectionSettings.getInstance().state.bitbucketUsername }` before the network call, matching the write dispatch already present at line 119. Low-priority threading hygiene.

confirmed.title: BitbucketServiceImpl.mergePullRequest (agent path) does not use PrActionService.merge's retry-on-409
confirmed.description: `BitbucketServiceImpl.mergePullRequest` (lines 709–739) performs a GET to obtain the current `version` then calls `api.mergePullRequest(…, prDetail.version, …)` directly. `PrActionService.merge` (PrActionService.kt:153–183) routes through `client.mergePullRequestWithRetry` which fetches the version internally and retries on a stale-version 409. The agent tool therefore silently misses the retry logic on concurrent merge attempts. The comment in `PrActionService` (lines 143–151) documents this as the deliberately correct path for the UI; the agent should use the same hardened path.
confirmed.suggestedFix: For the primary-repo case, delegate to `PrActionService.getInstance(project).merge(prId, strategyId=strategy, deleteSourceBranch=deleteSourceBranch, commitMessage=commitMessage)` and convert its `ApiResult` to `ToolResult<Unit>` with the same `.toToolResult(…)` helper already used by `addReviewer`/`updatePrTitle`. For non-primary repos, introduce a `mergePullRequestForAgent` private helper that calls `client.mergePullRequestWithRetry(RepoCoords(projectKey, repoSlug), …)` directly.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: BitbucketServiceImpl.mergePullRequest (lines 709-739) performs a GET at line 720 to fetch the current version, then calls `api.mergePullRequest(...)` directly at line 728 — no retry on 409. PrActionService.merge (lines 153-183) routes through `client.mergePullRequestWithRetry` at line 165, which fetches the version internally and retries once on a stale-version 409. The pattern is identical to the gap in declinePullRequest (ARC-1). For the agent, a concurrent merge attempt or PR detail update between the GET and the POST produces a spurious error. This is the same architectural inconsistency as ARC-1 — merge and decline are the two most version-sensitive operations, and both bypass the retry logic in the agent path while the UI path benefits from it.
confirmed.recommendedAction: For the primary repo case, delegate to PrActionService.getInstance(project).merge(prId, strategyId=strategy, deleteSourceBranch=deleteSourceBranch, commitMessage=commitMessage) and convert ApiResult to ToolResult. For non-primary repos, add a private mergePullRequestForAgent helper that calls client.mergePullRequestWithRetry with the resolved RepoCoords.

confirmed.title: PrDetailPanel's inline Create PR form (CARD_CREATE) is a dead code path — CreatePrLauncher EP is the sole entry point
confirmed.description: The `CARD_CREATE` card and its entire `buildCreatePanel()` / `submitCreatePr()` / `showCreateForm()` implementation (lines 466–810, ~345 lines) exists alongside the dedicated `CreatePrDialog` + `CreatePrLauncherImpl` EP. The module's `CLAUDE.md` states: 'Single entry point to PR creation: PR tab's "+Create PR" button (via CreatePrLauncher.getInstance()?.launch(…))' and 'Build tab's entry was removed'. The `showCreateForm()` is public but is never called from within the module. The `CreatePrAction` inner class in `PrDashboardPanel` goes through `CreatePrLauncher.getInstance().launch(…)` exclusively. The inline form implements a subset of the dialog's features (no multi-ticket input, no AI description, no module selector, no Jira transition) and creates its own `BitbucketBranchClient` directly (line 714). This dead path adds maintenance burden and breaks the architecture convention of a single EP-driven entry point.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Remove `CARD_CREATE`, `buildCreatePanel()`, `submitCreatePr()`, `showCreateForm()`, and all related fields (`createPanel`, `createSourceBranchLabel`, `createTargetBranchCombo`, `createTitleField`, `createDescriptionArea`, `createReviewersPanel`, `createAddReviewerLink`, `createButton`, `createBackLabel`, `selectedReviewerUsernames`, `selectedReviewerDisplayNames`, `createRepoConfig`). The `CreatePrLauncher` EP (invoked from `PrDashboardPanel.CreatePrAction`) is the correct and complete path.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed dead code. A global search for `showCreateForm` across the entire worktree returns exactly one result: its own definition at PrDetailPanel.kt:466. No caller exists anywhere in the codebase. `CARD_CREATE` is built in `init` (line 312-313) and shown only from within `showCreateForm` itself (line 500). `PrDashboardPanel.CreatePrAction` (lines 498-515) exclusively goes through `CreatePrLauncher.getInstance().launch(project, scope)` — the EP path. The inline form lacks multi-ticket input, AI description, Jira transitions, and the module selector that `CreatePrDialog` provides. Removing it is safe: the only reachable PR-creation path is the EP-driven dialog.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Remove `CARD_CREATE`, `buildCreatePanel()`, `submitCreatePr()`, `showCreateForm()`, `renderCreateReviewers()`, `showCreateReviewerPopup()`, and all related private fields (`createPanel`, `createSourceBranchLabel`, `createTargetBranchCombo`, `createTitleField`, `createDescriptionArea`, `createReviewersPanel`, `createAddReviewerLink`, `createButton`, `createBackLabel`, `selectedReviewerUsernames`, `selectedReviewerDisplayNames`, `createRepoConfig`). Coordinate removal with any test that might reference `CARD_CREATE`.

confirmed.title: PrListPanel empty-state label text does not follow the project's canonical empty-state convention
confirmed.description: The project convention (CLAUDE.md UI section) requires empty states to read "No [entity] [state]." followed by an action link. `PrListPanel`'s empty label reads "No pull requests found. Click Refresh to update." (line 75) — the 'Click Refresh to update' text is a prose instruction embedded in the label rather than a separate clickable action link. No action link component is provided. The same panel also uses this label unconditionally for both 'not yet loaded' and 'loaded but empty' states, meaning the label text is misleading when polling is active but returned an empty result.
confirmed.evidence: 75:    private val emptyLabel = JBLabel("No pull requests found. Click Refresh to update.").apply {
confirmed.suggestedFix: Split into two states: (1) a 'No pull requests [state].' label (e.g., 'No pull requests open.') matching the convention, plus (2) a separate `JBLabel` with `foreground = LINK_COLOR` and a mouse listener that calls `PrListService.refresh()` as the action link. Pass the current state string into `updatePrs()` so the label reads 'No pull requests open.', 'No pull requests merged.', etc.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at PrListPanel.kt:75. The label text is 'No pull requests found. Click Refresh to update.' — prose instruction embedded in the label with no separate clickable action link component. The project convention in CLAUDE.md mandates 'No [entity] [state].' plus an action link. Additionally, `showEmpty()` is called unconditionally for both 'not loaded' and 'loaded but empty' states, so the label text is always the same regardless of context. The fix is straightforward UI refactoring with no risk of breaking existing behavior.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Replace the single static label with two components: a state-appropriate JBLabel (e.g. 'No pull requests open.') and a separate link-styled JBLabel that triggers PrListService.refresh(). Pass the current state string into showEmpty() so the label is contextual.

confirmed.title: AiReviewViewModel.pushFinding — service error path and already-pushed/discarded guard untested
confirmed.description: pushFinding has three distinct guard/error branches that have zero test coverage: (1) the early-return guard `if (finding.pushed || finding.discarded) return false` (lines 63-64), (2) the service failure path `if (result.isError) { lastError = …; fire(); return false }` (lines 83-87), and (3) the pushAllKept() iterator (lines 100-109) which delegates to pushFinding but is not tested at all. The happy-path tests cover only the service-call dispatch (addPrComment / addInlineComment), not what the VM does when the push fails at the API layer or when findings are already archived.
confirmed.suggestedFix: In AiReviewViewModelTest, add: (1) a test that calls pushFinding with a finding where pushed=true and asserts the return value is false and service is never called; (2) a test where service.addPrComment returns ToolResult.error("network error") and asserts vm.lastError is set and the finding is NOT marked pushed; (3) a test for pushAllKept that mixes already-pushed, already-discarded, and fresh findings and verifies only the fresh ones are pushed. All are fully testable via MockK without the IntelliJ platform.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed by reading AiReviewViewModelTest.kt in full. The test class has 5 tests: refresh, pushFinding-general-success, pushFinding-inline-no-toHash, pushFinding-inline-with-toHash, and discard. None test the early-return guard at lines 63-64 (`if (finding.pushed || finding.discarded) return false`), none test the service error path at lines 83-87 (`if (result.isError)`), and `pushAllKept()` (lines 100-109) has zero coverage. These are behaviorally significant — callers inspect the return value and `lastError` to show UI error states. The missing paths are purely testable via MockK without any platform dependency.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add three tests to AiReviewViewModelTest: (1) pushFinding with pushed=true returns false without calling service; (2) service returns ToolResult(isError=true) → pushFinding returns false and vm.lastError is set; (3) pushAllKept with a mix of pushed/discarded/fresh findings verifies only fresh ones are pushed and the count is correct.

confirmed.title: CommentsViewModel mutation error paths (postGeneralComment, reply, resolve, reopen) have no failure-path tests
confirmed.description: Four mutation methods — postGeneralComment, reply, resolve, reopen — each have an explicit `if (result.isError) { lastError = …; fire(); return false }` branch (lines 83-86, 99-103, 112-116, 127-131). The CommentsViewModelTest covers the success path for postGeneralComment, reply, and resolve, but not a single failure case for any of the four. The `reopen()` method (lines 123-137) has no test at all (success or failure). These are the paths users hit when the Bitbucket token is expired, the PR is merged, or the server returns 403.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add to CommentsViewModelTest: (1) `postGeneralComment returns false and sets lastError when service returns error`; (2) `reply returns false and sets lastError on service error`; (3) `resolve returns false and sets lastError on service error`; (4) `reopen calls service reopenPrComment and refreshes on success`; (5) `reopen returns false and sets lastError on service error`. All are fully testable via MockK coEvery returning ToolResult(isError=true).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed by reading CommentsViewModelTest.kt in full. The file has 6 tests: refresh-success, refresh-error, postGeneralComment-success, resolve-success, reply-success, and PrCommentsUpdated event. There is no failure-path test for postGeneralComment, reply, or resolve, and `reopen()` (CommentsViewModel.kt lines 123-137) has zero tests — neither success nor failure. The error branches at lines 83-86, 99-103, 112-116, and 127-131 all set `lastError` and return false; callers rely on these to surface error state in the UI. All are testable with MockK coEvery returning ToolResult(isError=true).
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add failure-path tests for postGeneralComment, reply, and resolve (each asserting lastError is set and return value is false), plus both success and failure tests for reopen().

confirmed.title: PrReviewSessionRegistry — updateStatus on non-existent key is a silent no-op with no test; all() method also untested
confirmed.description: updateStatus (lines 33-38) silently does nothing when the prId is not in the registry (`val existing = current[prId] ?: return@withLock`). This is the expected safety gate, but callers (e.g. AgentLoop completing a review for a session whose registry entry was deleted) see no error and get no indication the status was dropped. There is no test that calls updateStatus on a non-existent prId and asserts the state is unchanged. Additionally, the `all()` method (line 42) that returns all entries for display in the AI Review tab has zero coverage — not tested for empty registry, single entry, or multiple entries.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add to PrReviewSessionRegistryTest: (1) `updateStatus on unknown prId is a no-op — all() returns empty` (call updateStatus before any register, assert get returns null); (2) `all() returns all registered entries` (register 3 PRs, call all(), assert size=3 and all entries match); (3) `all() returns empty map on fresh registry`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed by reading PrReviewSessionRegistryTest.kt. The file has 4 tests: register-persists, get-unknown-null, register-overwrites, updateStatus-preserves. The `updateStatus` early-return at line 35 (`val existing = current[prId] ?: return@withLock`) when called on a non-existent key is not exercised by any test. The `all()` method at line 42 — used by the AI Review tab to display all sessions — has no test at all (not for empty, single, or multiple entries). These are low-risk to add and provide a safety net for the caller's assumption that unknown-ID calls are no-ops.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add: (1) test that updateStatus on unknown prId leaves the registry unchanged; (2) test that all() returns all registered entries (register N, all() returns size N); (3) test that all() returns empty map on fresh registry.

confirmed.title: PrReviewSessionRegistry — corrupt/malformed JSON file silently returns emptyMap() with no test
confirmed.description: readFile() uses `runCatching { json.decodeFromString<RegistryFile>(...) }.getOrElse { emptyMap() }` (lines 58-60). If the file on disk is corrupt (truncated write, manual edit, schema change from a plugin downgrade), every subsequent register/get/updateStatus silently overwrites history with an empty base — the in-flight write after a corrupt read destroys all existing entries. The `getOrElse { emptyMap() }` recovery is correct behaviour, but there is no test proving it does not crash or throw, and no test that verifies a register() call after a corrupt read produces a single-entry file (rather than a zero-entry one).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add to PrReviewSessionRegistryTest: (1) `get() returns null and does not throw when file is corrupt JSON` (write `{ not valid json }` to the registry path, then call get()); (2) `register() after corrupt file creates a fresh single-entry file` (pre-write corrupt JSON, then register a PR, assert get() finds it with correct sessionId and the file is valid JSON).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at PrReviewSessionRegistry.kt lines 58-60: `runCatching { json.decodeFromString<RegistryFile>(...) }.getOrElse { emptyMap() }`. The corrupt-read path is correct behaviour, but it has no test. The concern is valid: a register() call after a corrupt-file read starts from emptyMap(), discarding all prior entries. The PrReviewSessionRegistryTest uses @TempDir which makes it easy to pre-seed a corrupt file. No existing test writes corrupt JSON and verifies graceful recovery. Not a runtime bug — the code is correct — but the lack of a test means future changes to readFile() could silently regress this safety gate.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add two tests using @TempDir: (1) pre-write corrupt JSON to the registry path, call get() and assert null is returned without exception; (2) pre-write corrupt JSON, then register a PR, assert get() returns the newly registered entry and the file is valid JSON.

confirmed.title: PrListService.fetchDashboardPrs — 100-result cap boundary and per-role API error propagation untested
confirmed.description: fetchDashboardPrs (lines 200-232) has two critical untested paths: (1) the `results.size < 100` cap — when exactly 100 results accumulate, pagination stops even if isLastPage=false; there is no test that verifies the capped list is returned instead of continuing to fetch. (2) when getDashboardPullRequests returns ApiResult.Error (line 225), the method silently breaks out of the loop and returns whatever was collected so far (possibly an empty list), with only a log.warn. No test verifies that a mid-pagination API error still returns the partial results collected on prior pages. The only PrListService test in the suite (PrListServiceEdtMutationTest) is a source-text guard for EDT dispatch, not a behaviour test.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a standalone test class PrListServiceFetchDashboardTest at the BitbucketBranchClient seam (using MockWebServer, same pattern as BitbucketServiceImplCommitsPaginationTest): (1) queue 4 pages of 25 PRs (total 100) with isLastPage=false — assert exactly 100 results returned and no further HTTP request made; (2) queue page-1 success + page-2 ApiResult.Error — assert partial results from page-1 are returned and the method does not throw.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. PrListService.kt lines 200-232 implement `fetchDashboardPrs` with a `results.size < 100` cap and an error-break path (lines 224-229). The only existing test (PrListServiceEdtMutationTest) is a source-text pattern check for the EDT dispatch — it has no behavioral coverage whatsoever for fetchDashboardPrs. The 100-cap boundary (stop when results.size reaches 100 even if isLastPage=false) is untested, as is the partial-results return on mid-pagination ApiResult.Error. The suggested MockWebServer-based approach matches the existing BitbucketServiceImplCommitsPaginationTest pattern in the module.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add PrListServiceFetchDashboardTest using MockWebServer at the BitbucketBranchClient seam: (1) serve exactly 4 pages of 25 PRs with isLastPage=false, assert exactly 100 results and no further HTTP request; (2) serve page-1 success + page-2 error, assert partial page-1 results are returned without throwing.

confirmed.title: PrListService.setState — concurrent setState + refresh race: @Volatile currentState read under coroutineScope untested
confirmed.description: setState (lines 81-84) writes `currentState` (a @Volatile var) and immediately launches a coroutine on Dispatchers.IO to call refresh(). refresh() reads `currentState` inside an async block (line 210). If setState("MERGED") fires while a prior refresh() coroutine is still mid-flight reading currentState="OPEN", the two coroutines race on a @Volatile field. A @Volatile field prevents torn reads but does not prevent stale reads between setState's write and refresh's coroutineScope launch boundary. The codebase has no test verifying that two rapid setState calls produce a single final consistent state rather than interleaved or stale reads.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a source-text contract test (same pattern as PrListServiceEdtMutationTest) asserting that currentState is accessed only as a @Volatile var (not as a MutableStateFlow or AtomicReference). Additionally add a behavioural note that setState's Dispatchers.IO launch is inherently best-effort (no sequential ordering guarantee). A concrete behaviour test: call setState("OPEN") then setState("MERGED") in rapid succession with a mock client that records which state each refresh sees, and assert the final _myPrs emission was fetched using "MERGED" (not "OPEN").
confirmed.verdictReasoning: The race condition described is real at the level of @Volatile semantics: `setState` writes `currentState` then launches a new coroutine; a prior refresh coroutine already reading `currentState` on Dispatchers.IO will see the old value. @Volatile prevents torn reads but not stale reads between the write and the new coroutine's first read. However, this is the intended design: the field is @Volatile precisely to provide a best-effort snapshot — each refresh invocation reads the field at its own pace, and the StateFlow emissions that result are eventually consistent. The consequence of a stale read is a one-cycle UI refresh showing the wrong state bucket, not data corruption or a crash. Severity is lower than claimed (Medium→Low) since this is a best-effort eventual-consistency model with no data loss. A behavioral test as described is valuable but the gap is cosmetic rather than a user-visible bug in normal usage.
confirmed.recommendedAction: The source-text contract test asserting @Volatile is already implicitly satisfied by the existing code. A behavioral test (rapid double setState, assert final emission used the last state) would add confidence but is low priority. If tighter ordering is desired, replace @Volatile with an AtomicReference and use getAndSet, but this is an optional architectural improvement, not a bug fix.

confirmed.title: BitbucketServiceImpl.listPrComments — onlyOpen and onlyInline post-fetch filters have no test
confirmed.description: After pagination, listPrComments applies two client-side filters (lines 1020-1022): `if (onlyOpen) mapped = mapped.filter { it.state == PrCommentState.OPEN }` and `if (onlyInline) mapped = mapped.filter { it.anchor != null }`. The pagination tests (BitbucketServiceImplListPrCommentsPaginationTest) operate at the BitbucketBranchClient layer where these filters don't exist. The BitbucketServiceImpl is not unit-testable directly, but the filter logic is a pure transformation on a List<PrComment> that can be extracted and tested independently — or tested via a minimal wrapper that exercises the service layer filter independently of the client layer.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Extract the two filter predicates as internal/companion functions (similar to how PrActionService exposes internal mutators) so they can be called directly in unit tests. Add tests: (1) `onlyOpen=true filters out RESOLVED comments`; (2) `onlyInline=true filters out comments with null anchor`; (3) `both flags combine via AND (intersection)`; (4) `both flags false returns all comments unfiltered`. Alternatively, add a source-text contract test verifying the exact filter expressions are present in the body of listPrComments.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. Lines 1020-1022 of BitbucketServiceImpl.kt apply two pure client-side filters after pagination: `if (onlyOpen) mapped = mapped.filter { it.state == PrCommentState.OPEN }` and `if (onlyInline) mapped = mapped.filter { it.anchor != null }`. The pagination test (BitbucketServiceImplListPrCommentsPaginationTest) explicitly documents in its architecture note that it operates at the BitbucketBranchClient layer where these filters do not exist. No test anywhere verifies the filter logic. However, both predicates are trivially correct one-liners over a List — the OPEN state enum comparison and null-anchor check carry essentially no branching risk. Severity is better assessed as Low: these are pure-function coverage gaps on straightforward predicates, not behavioral bugs users encounter. The suggested fix (extract as internal companion functions and add 4 tests) is safe and does not harm any existing behavior.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add unit tests for the four filter combinations (onlyOpen, onlyInline, both, neither). The simplest approach is a small test helper that builds a list of PrComment stubs with varying states and anchors and invokes the filter logic inline — extraction of the predicates is optional since they are trivial lambdas.

confirmed.title: BitbucketServiceImpl.toPrComment — enum valueOf fallback for unknown state/severity/lineType strings untested
confirmed.description: The private toPrComment extension (lines 1315-1348) uses `runCatching { PrCommentState.valueOf(state) }.getOrDefault(PrCommentState.OPEN)` and identical patterns for severity, lineType, and fileType. If Bitbucket Server returns a new state value (e.g. "PENDING" introduced in a newer DC release) or a lineType the client doesn't recognise, the comment silently maps to OPEN/NORMAL/null. There is no test verifying this fallback for any of the four fields, nor a test that an unrecognised enum value does not throw.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Extract toPrComment as an internal top-level function (same pattern as the mutators in PrActionService.kt) so it is unit-testable. Add tests: (1) `unknown state string falls back to OPEN without throwing`; (2) `unknown severity string falls back to NORMAL without throwing`; (3) `unknown lineType string falls back to null anchor lineType without throwing`; (4) `known state RESOLVED maps correctly`. These exercise a parsing path that runs on every listPrComments call and silently masks server version mismatches.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. Lines 1315-1348 of BitbucketServiceImpl.kt show the private `toPrComment` extension using `runCatching { PrCommentState.valueOf(state) }.getOrDefault(PrCommentState.OPEN)` and identical patterns for severity (NORMAL fallback), lineType (null fallback via getOrNull), and fileType (null fallback via getOrNull). There is no test that passes an unknown state/severity/lineType string and asserts no exception plus the correct fallback. The patterns are correct and defensive — the `runCatching` wrapping means unknown values silently map to defaults at runtime. The risk is real but already mitigated by the fallback logic itself. Severity is Low rather than Medium: the fallback is already in place and the code is correct; the gap is solely test coverage, not a latent bug. Fixing by extracting `toPrComment` as an internal top-level function is safe and non-breaking.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Promote `toPrComment` to an internal top-level function (same file), then add tests verifying (1) unknown state falls back to OPEN without throwing, (2) unknown severity falls back to NORMAL, (3) unknown lineType leaves anchor.lineType null, (4) known RESOLVED state maps correctly.

confirmed.title: PrDescriptionGenerator diff-cap retry ladder — ContextOverflow and non-retryable Other paths untested
confirmed.description: The generate() method (lines 49-137) has a 4-step DIFF_CAP_LADDER retry loop (lines 76-109). The loop has three distinct branch outcomes: (1) TextGenerationOutcome.Success — returns the text; (2) TextGenerationOutcome.ContextOverflow — continues with the next (halved) cap; (3) TextGenerationOutcome.Other — breaks and falls through to Tier 2. The existing PrDescriptionGeneratorTest covers only the internal `buildFallbackDescription` helper (Tier 3). No test exercises the ContextOverflow path that triggers cap halving, nor the Other path that skips remaining retry slots and falls to Tier 2. The Tier 2 no-diff fallback branch (lines 113-131) is also untested.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add PrDescriptionGeneratorLadderTest using a fake TextGenerationService injected via a test seam or by making generate() accept the service as a parameter: (1) `ContextOverflow on first cap retries with halved cap and succeeds`; (2) `ContextOverflow on all four caps falls through to Tier 2`; (3) `Other failure on first cap skips remaining ladder and falls to Tier 2`; (4) `Tier 2 failure (null aiResult) falls to Tier 3 fallback`. Since TextGenerationService.getInstance() is a global singleton, the tests should ideally use the testProvider injection seam pattern already established in TicketChipInputTest.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. PrDescriptionGenerator.kt lines 76-109 show the DIFF_CAP_LADDER for-loop with three branch outcomes: Success (return), ContextOverflow (continue), and Other (break). PrDescriptionGeneratorTest only covers the `internal fun buildFallbackDescription` (Tier 3). The ContextOverflow cap-halving path (line 100-103), the Other early-break path (lines 104-107), and the entire Tier 2 no-diff fallback (lines 112-131) are untested. The claimed severity of High is overstated — this is a coverage gap, not a known behavioral defect. The actual ladder logic is straightforward Kotlin control flow. Downgraded to Medium because the missing coverage is for non-trivial branches (ContextOverflow retry semantics and Tier 2 fallthrough) that could silently regress. The fix is not harmful; `generate()` uses `TextGenerationService.getInstance()` (line 62) with no injection seam, so tests would need to either use the `testProvider` pattern referenced in the suggestion or restructure the method to accept the service — neither breaks existing behavior.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add a test seam to `generate()` (e.g., an internal overload accepting TextGenerationService directly, as the suggestion describes for TicketChipInputTest), then add tests for ContextOverflow-then-success, all-overflow-falls-to-Tier2, Other-breaks-to-Tier2, and Tier2-null-falls-to-Tier3.

confirmed.title: PrActionService.decline — first-attempt fetch failure path (getPullRequestDetail returns Error) untested
confirmed.description: fetchAndDecline (lines 249-263) first calls getPullRequestDetail. If this GET fails (404 — PR deleted, 403 — permission revoked, timeout), it returns the error directly (line 258: `is ApiResult.Error -> return r`). The retry path in decline() (lines 215-242) checks for 409 specifically, so a 404 on the pre-fetch produces an error at `first` that is neither a 409 nor a success, and is returned to the caller via the `if (!is409)` path (lines 218-221). The existing PrActionServiceDeclineRetryTest tests the 409 retry cycle and the successful two-step sequence, but not the case where the initial GET itself fails. This is a real user path when a PR is closed (404) between the time the PR list loaded and the user clicks Decline.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add to PrActionServiceDeclineRetryTest: `decline returns error when getPullRequestDetail returns 404` (enqueue a 404 for the GET, assert the returned ApiResult is an Error with appropriate type, and assert that no POST to the decline endpoint was made using server.takeRequest()). This exercises the fetchAndDecline early return and confirms the caller receives a meaningful error rather than a silent no-op.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. PrActionService.kt lines 249-263 show `fetchAndDecline`: if `getPullRequestDetail` returns `ApiResult.Error`, it immediately returns `r` (line 257). The caller at lines 218-221 checks for 409 specifically — a 404/403 on the GET is not a 409 and so is returned to the caller as an error. PrActionServiceDeclineRetryTest tests (1) `declinePullRequest sends version in query string`, (2) `declinePullRequest maps 409 to VALIDATION_ERROR`, (3) `getPullRequestDetail returns current version`, (4) `fetchAndDecline pattern succeeds when GET then POST returns 200`, and (5) the 409-then-success two-step. None of these enqueue a 4xx on the GET leg. The gap is real. However, the early return on GET failure is the correct and safe behavior: the service does not fire the POST. The risk is that this path is unexercised and could regress silently if the early return were accidentally removed. Severity is Low not Medium: the behavior is provably correct from static analysis; there is no ambiguity in the code path and no observed regression. The fix is harmless.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add to PrActionServiceDeclineRetryTest: enqueue a 404 for getPullRequestDetail, call `client.getPullRequestDetail` directly and verify it returns ApiResult.Error, then verify (via server.requestCount) that declinePullRequest was never reached. This exercises the fetchAndDecline early-return and guards against future regressions.

confirmed.title: MarkdownToHtml — unclosed code block, empty input, and adjacent list-then-heading transitions untested
confirmed.description:
renderBody() (lines 28-96) has three untested edge cases: (1) an unclosed fenced code block (the while-loop ends with `if (inCodeBlock) html.append("")` on line 93 — no test verifies this safety-close fires and produces well-formed HTML rather than an unclosed `

` in the output.

Line 92 shows the matching list close. MarkdownToHtmlTest.kt covers 10 cases (wrapper, fragment, bold, italic, code block, links, headings, lists, HTML escaping) but none of the three claimed gaps: (1) unclosed code block auto-close, (2) empty string input producing valid html/body wrapper, (3) heading immediately after a list item closing the ul. All three gaps are real and the code paths are active lines. Severity Low is appropriate: this is pure formatting/rendering logic with no data loss or security impact. The fix is safe — adding these three tests cannot break any existing passing test.

…

confirmed.title: BitbucketServiceImpl.mergePullRequest — getPullRequestDetail failure on version pre-fetch untested
confirmed.description: mergePullRequest (lines 709-739) fetches the current PR version before merging (lines 720-726): `val currentPr = api.getPullRequestDetail(...)`. If this GET fails (e.g. 404 because the PR was already merged, or 403), it returns early with an error (line 723-725). This is a user-visible failure path — when a user races another merge from the UI — but there is no test for it. The existing PrActionServiceModifyRetryTest covers the merge retry path via mergePullRequestWithRetry at the client layer, not the BitbucketServiceImpl.mergePullRequest GET-then-POST pattern.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a test at the BitbucketBranchClient seam using MockWebServer: enqueue a 404 for the getPullRequestDetail GET call and assert that mergePullRequest returns a ToolResult with isError=true containing a descriptive error message, and that no POST to the merge endpoint was made.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. BitbucketServiceImpl.kt lines 720-726 show that `mergePullRequest` calls `api.getPullRequestDetail(...)` and returns a ToolResult error immediately if the GET fails (lines 723-725): `is ApiResult.Error -> return ToolResult(data = Unit, summary = "Error fetching PR #$prId for merge: ${currentPr.message}", isError = true, ...)`. PrActionServiceModifyRetryTest tests `mergePullRequestWithRetry` at the `BitbucketBranchClient` layer (lines 29, 252+) which takes a version parameter directly — there is no GET involved at that layer. Therefore the service-level GET-then-POST pattern in BitbucketServiceImpl.mergePullRequest is untested. The early return on GET failure is the correct behavior. Severity is Low rather than Medium: static analysis confirms the early return is correct and safe; the gap is coverage-only. The suggested fix (MockWebServer test enqueuing a 404 for the GET) is safe and does not affect production behavior.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add a test using MockWebServer: enqueue a 404 for the getPullRequestDetail GET call and assert that the returned ToolResult has isError=true with a meaningful message, and that no POST to the merge endpoint was made (server.requestCount == 1).

confirmed.title: defaultRevList timeout is ineffective — readText() blocks before waitFor() can fire
confirmed.description: The REV_LIST_TIMEOUT_SECONDS guard is checked only after `process.inputStream.bufferedReader().readText()` returns. On a slow/hung network-mounted repository or a corrupted git index, `readText()` will block indefinitely reading stdout before `waitFor()` is ever evaluated. The comment claims this guards against slow network drives, but the timeout can never interrupt the blocking `readText()` call — the guard only applies to the time spent waiting for the process to exit AFTER readText() has already drained stdout.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Read stdout on a separate thread (or with a deadline) so the timeout can fire concurrently. The standard pattern is to use `ProcessBuilder` with `inheritIO()` + a separate thread for stdout, or to use `withTimeoutOrNull` around the entire block inside a `Dispatchers.IO` coroutine. The simplest correct fix: launch a background thread to drain stdout into a StringBuilder, call `process.waitFor(timeout, unit)` on the current thread, then destroy-and-return-empty if the timeout fires, or collect the thread result if it completes in time.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at PlanDetectionService.kt lines 401-411. `process.inputStream.bufferedReader().readText()` is called synchronously BEFORE `process.waitFor(REV_LIST_TIMEOUT_SECONDS, TimeUnit.SECONDS)`. Since `readText()` blocks the calling thread until the subprocess closes its stdout (i.e., until the process exits), the `waitFor()` call on the next line can never interrupt a hung `git rev-list` process — it will only observe the exit code after `readText()` has already returned. The timeout guard is therefore dead code for the hang scenario it was designed to protect against. On a network-mounted repository or corrupted git index where git hangs indefinitely, `defaultRevList` will block forever. The KDoc comment at line 398 explicitly claims this guards against slow network drives, making the misimplementation a correctness bug. The fix (drain stdout on a background thread, call `waitFor` on the current thread) does not remove any useful behaviour — it makes the stated contract actually true.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Drain stdout on a background thread: launch a coroutine or Java Thread to read stdout into a StringBuilder, then call `process.waitFor(REV_LIST_TIMEOUT_SECONDS, TimeUnit.SECONDS)` on the calling thread. If the timeout fires, call `destroyForcibly()` and interrupt/join the reader thread, then return emptyList(). The simplest Kotlin idiom is `val readerThread = thread(start = true) { output.append(process.inputStream.bufferedReader().readText()) }; if (!process.waitFor(REV_LIST_TIMEOUT_SECONDS, TimeUnit.SECONDS)) { process.destroyForcibly(); readerThread.interrupt(); return emptyList() }; readerThread.join(); return output.toString().lines()...`.

confirmed.title: Swing component (JBCheckBox) constructed on IO background thread in ManualStageDialog
confirmed.description: Inside the `scope.launch { }` block in the `init` (where `scope` is the caller's `panelScope`, which runs on `Dispatchers.IO`), `JBCheckBox(name, preChecked)` is constructed at line 163 on a background thread. The Swing/AWT threading model requires all Swing component construction and mutation to happen on the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT). Constructing a `JBCheckBox` off-EDT causes an unsynchronized access to Swing's component tree, which can corrupt the component state or produce rendering artefacts.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Move all Swing component construction into the `invokeLater` block that already exists at the end of the `scope.launch`. Compute `stageNames` (a plain `List<String>`) on the IO coroutine, then in `invokeLater` create the `JBCheckBox` instances, assign `stageCheckboxes`, and call `refillSlots()` + `updateOkButton()`. This keeps IO work on IO and Swing construction on the EDT.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at ManualStageDialog.kt lines 143-183. The `scope.launch { }` block runs on whatever dispatcher `scope` (the caller's `panelScope`) is bound to — `panelScope` is declared as `CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.IO)` in BuildDashboardPanel (line 89). Inside this IO coroutine, `JBCheckBox(name, preChecked)` is constructed at line 163 and `cb.addActionListener { ... }` is called at line 164 — both are Swing component operations that the AWT threading model requires to happen on the EDT. The `invokeLater` at line 179 calls `refillSlots()` and `updateOkButton()` on the EDT, but the checkbox objects themselves were already constructed off-EDT. The existing `invokeLater` is the correct site to move construction into; only `stageNames` (a plain `List<String>`) needs to be computed on IO.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Compute `stageNames` (pure data) on the IO coroutine. Move `JBCheckBox` construction and `addActionListener` calls into the `invokeLater` block at line 179, before `refillSlots()` and `updateOkButton()`. Assign `stageCheckboxes` inside `invokeLater` so both the construction and the field write happen on the EDT.

confirmed.title: ManualStageDialog shared fields written from IO coroutine and read from EDT without @Volatile
confirmed.description: Fields `variables: List<PlanVariableData>`, `stageCheckboxes`, `stageLoadError`, `isLoading`, and `isLoadingStages` are written from within `scope.launch` (IO thread) and then read from the EDT in `populateVariablesEditor`, `populateStageSlot`, `updateOkButton`, and `doOKAction`. None of these fields carry `@Volatile`. Without `@Volatile` or explicit synchronization, the JMM does not guarantee that EDT reads will see the writes made by the IO coroutine, even though the `invokeLater` at the end of the coroutine provides a happens-before for the read paths it dispatches — `doOKAction` reads `variables` and `stageCheckboxes` independently without going through `invokeLater`.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add `@Volatile` to all fields written from the IO coroutine and read from the EDT: `variables`, `stageCheckboxes`, `stageLoadError`, `isLoading`, `isLoadingStages`. Alternatively, move all writes into the existing `invokeLater` block so they are confined to the EDT throughout, eliminating the cross-thread visibility gap entirely.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at ManualStageDialog.kt. Fields `variables` (line 104), `isLoading` (line 105), `stageCheckboxes` (line 108), `stageLoadError` (line 110), `isLoadingStages` (line 112) are plain `private var` with no `@Volatile`. They are written from the IO coroutine (`scope.launch`) at lines 147, 153, 157, 168-174, 178. `doOKAction` at line 624 reads `variables.filter { it.isPassword }` directly on the EDT without going through any `invokeLater` barrier, creating a JMM visibility gap. The `invokeLater` at line 179 establishes happens-before only for `refillSlots()` and `updateOkButton()` — not for `doOKAction`. In practice, by the time the user can click OK the `invokeLater` has already delivered, so the temporal window is narrow; this makes it a medium-severity issue rather than high, but it is a genuine JMM violation. Severity is downgraded from High to Medium because the practical race window is very narrow: `doOKAction` is only reachable after the `invokeLater` has delivered (dialog buttons are on EDT, the user must have seen the loaded content), so a torn read is theoretically possible but extremely rare.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add `@Volatile` to `variables`, `stageCheckboxes`, `stageLoadError`, `isLoading`, and `isLoadingStages`, OR (cleaner) confine all writes of these fields to the existing `invokeLater` block so they are EDT-only, matching the reads in `populateVariablesEditor`, `populateStageSlot`, `updateOkButton`, and `doOKAction`.

confirmed.title: BambooServiceImpl.client getter has TOCTOU race — @Volatile does not protect compound check-create-write
confirmed.description: The `client` getter performs a read-check-write compound operation on `cachedBaseUrl` and `cachedClient` that is not atomic. Two coroutines calling `client` concurrently from `Dispatchers.IO` can both observe `url != cachedBaseUrl || cachedClient == null` as true, both construct a new `BambooApiClient` (creating an additional OkHttpClient derived from `sharedPool`), and then both write to `cachedClient`. The second write discards the first client. More critically, `cachedBaseUrl` is written BEFORE `cachedClient` in the same if-block, so in the window between those two writes a third reader sees a stale `cachedBaseUrl` pointing to the new URL but a `cachedClient` still built for the old URL. In `triggerBuild`, `cachedBaseUrl` is then read directly to form the `link` URL — if the URL was just changed this can produce a mismatched link.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Guard the entire compound operation with `@Synchronized` on the getter, or replace both `@Volatile` fields with a single `@Volatile` holder of a `Pair<String, BambooApiClient>` so the read-check-replace is a single volatile write. The cleanest fix per the project conventions is a coroutine `Mutex` wrapping the check-and-recreate path since service methods are already `suspend`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at BambooServiceImpl.kt lines 56-72. The `client` getter performs a three-step compound operation: read `cachedBaseUrl`, compare to `url`, then conditionally write `cachedBaseUrl = url` followed by `cachedClient = BambooApiClient(...)`. Two coroutines on `Dispatchers.IO` can both observe `url != cachedBaseUrl` as true simultaneously, both construct a new client, and both write. The `@Volatile` annotations on `cachedBaseUrl` (line 44) and `cachedClient` (line 43) individually guarantee visibility of each field's latest write but do not make the compound read-check-write atomic: one writer's `cachedBaseUrl = url` write lands before the other writer's `cachedClient` assignment, creating a window where a third reader sees the new URL but the old client. The bug is also noted in `triggerBuild` which reads `cachedBaseUrl` to form the link URL. However, in typical usage there is only one configured Bamboo URL that almost never changes, and the client construction is idempotent — the worst outcome is a redundant OkHttpClient allocation. Severity is correctly assessed as Medium.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Replace the two `@Volatile` fields with a single `@Volatile` holder: `@Volatile private var cachedEntry: Pair<String, BambooApiClient>? = null`. The getter reads the pair atomically, checks `entry?.first == url`, and if mismatched creates a new pair and writes it as a single volatile store. Alternatively, add a `Mutex` and wrap the check-create-write in `mutex.withLock { }` since the callers are already in suspend context.

confirmed.title: BuildMonitorService shared mutable state has no synchronization — concurrent pollOnce calls corrupt state
confirmed.description: The fields `previousBuildNumber`, `previousStatus`, `lastLogFetchedForBuild`, `jobOrderCache`, and `poller` are plain (non-`@Volatile`, non-synchronized) `private var` fields. `pollOnce` is a public `suspend fun` called from (a) the `SmartPoller` action running in `cs` (project scope), and (b) `panelScope.launch` (Dispatchers.IO) when the user clicks Refresh, Stop, or Cancel in `BuildDashboardPanel`. These two scopes can execute `pollOnce` concurrently. A race between them on `previousBuildNumber`/`previousStatus` can cause a `BuildFinished` event to be emitted twice for the same build, or suppressed, and `lastLogFetchedForBuild` can be written by one coroutine before the other has fetched all logs — causing the 'retry on partial fetch' logic to never re-run.
confirmed.evidence:
private var previousBuildNumber: Int? = null
private var previousStatus: BuildStatus? = null
private var lastLogFetchedForBuild: Int? = null
// ...
val isFirstPoll = previousBuildNumber == null
val statusChanged = dto.buildNumber != previousBuildNumber || ...
previousBuildNumber = dto.buildNumber
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a `Mutex` (coroutine mutex from `kotlinx.coroutines.sync`) to `BuildMonitorService` and wrap the entire body of `pollOnce` in `mutex.withLock { }`. This ensures only one `pollOnce` executes at a time regardless of which scope calls it, which is the correct semantic since the fields form a consistent unit of state (previousNumber + previousStatus + lastLogFetched must all advance together). Alternatively, mark all fields `@Volatile` and use `compareAndSet` patterns — but the mutex approach is simpler and the lock contention is negligible (30s intervals).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at BuildMonitorService.kt. Fields `previousBuildNumber` (line 148), `previousStatus` (line 149), `lastLogFetchedForBuild` (line 150), `jobOrderCache` (line 158) are plain `private var` with no synchronization. `pollOnce` is `public suspend fun` called from two independent coroutine scopes: (1) the `SmartPoller` action lambda at line 188 inside `startPolling`, which runs on `cs` (the service's injected `CoroutineScope`); (2) `panelScope.launch { monitorService.pollOnce(...) }` in BuildDashboardPanel's Refresh action (line 933-935) and Stop action (line 968). These two scopes are independent so concurrent execution is possible. A race between them on `previousBuildNumber`/`previousStatus` can cause `BuildFinished` to be emitted twice for the same build (if both coroutines observe `statusChanged = true` before either writes `previousBuildNumber`) or for `lastLogFetchedForBuild` to be set by one before the other has fetched all logs — skipping the retry path. However, the Refresh button is user-initiated and typically fires outside the 30s polling window, so the overlap is rare in practice. Severity is downgraded from High to Medium.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add a `private val pollMutex = Mutex()` to `BuildMonitorService` and wrap the entire body of `pollOnce` in `pollMutex.withLock { ... }`. This ensures the `previousBuildNumber + previousStatus + lastLogFetchedForBuild` fields advance as a consistent unit regardless of which scope calls `pollOnce`.

confirmed.title: GitRepository.currentRevision and currentBranchName accessed without ReadAction on IO background thread
confirmed.description: The methods `readActionLocalHead()` (line 810) and `getCurrentBranch()` (line 807) read `GitRepository.currentRevision` and `GitRepository.currentBranchName` respectively. Despite the name `readActionLocalHead`, neither method wraps the access in `ReadAction.compute {}` or the modern `readAction {}` suspend call. Both are called from within `panelScope.launch` (which runs on `Dispatchers.IO`) — specifically in `resolveBranchPlanAndMonitor` (lines 235, 238) and in the Refresh action's `panelScope.launch` (line 934). The IntelliJ Platform requires VCS model reads to be performed inside a read action when accessed from a background thread, or a write-action interleaving can cause a torn read of the repository state.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Wrap all VCS model reads in `readAction { }` (the non-deprecated suspend variant from `com.intellij.openapi.application.readAction`). Since the callers are already in a suspend context, convert to: `val latestCommit = readAction { getGitRepo()?.currentRevision }.orEmpty()` and similarly for `getCurrentBranch()`. The `readActionLocalHead` naming implies this was intended; the actual `ReadAction` call is simply missing.
