confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at BuildDashboardPanel.kt lines 807 and 810. `getCurrentBranch()` returns `getGitRepo()?.currentBranchName` and `readActionLocalHead()` returns `getGitRepo()?.currentRevision` — both without wrapping in `readAction { }`. Both are called from `panelScope.launch` (Dispatchers.IO) in `resolveBranchPlanAndMonitor` at lines 235 and 238, and `getCurrentBranch()` is also called from the Refresh action's `panelScope.launch` at line 934. The bamboo CLAUDE.md explicitly states 'PrBar git reads use `readAction { }`', indicating this is the expected pattern in the codebase. The core CLAUDE.md also notes the deprecation of `runReadAction` in favour of `readAction { }` for 2026.1. However, `GitRepository.currentBranchName` and `currentRevision` in git4idea are properties of `GitRepositoryImpl` backed by `@Volatile` fields updated under the git repository state lock — the IntelliJ Platform formally requires a read action around PSI and file model access, but in practice these git4idea properties are thread-safe cached values. The name `readActionLocalHead` suggests the intent was to wrap in ReadAction but it was never implemented. Severity is Low because the practical risk of a torn read from this specific access pattern is very low (git4idea fields are @Volatile), but it is a platform contract violation.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Convert `readActionLocalHead()` and `getCurrentBranch()` to suspend functions that wrap their access in `readAction { }` (the `com.intellij.openapi.application.readAction` suspend variant). Update call sites in `resolveBranchPlanAndMonitor` and the Refresh action to `await` or inline the `readAction` call.

confirmed.title: getProjects() silently truncates at 100 projects — no pagination
confirmed.description: Unlike `getPlans()`, `getBranches()`, `getPlanBranches()`, and `getLinkedRepositories()` — which all use the shared `paginate()` loop — `getProjects()` makes a single GET with `max-results=100` and returns only the first page. Bamboo installations with more than 100 projects will silently return an incomplete list. The `BambooServiceImpl.getProjects()` surface is exposed to the agent (`BambooService.getProjects`) and used for project-key lookups; a user with >100 projects can never find plans belonging to projects beyond the first 100.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Wrap `getProjects()` in the same `paginate()` helper used by `getPlans()`, using `BambooProjectListResponse` and extracting `it.projects.project` as the page items. The paginator already handles both the offset advancement and the short-page termination condition, so the change is a straightforward mechanical replacement matching the pattern of `getPlans()`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at BambooApiClient.kt lines 48-51. `getProjects()` makes a single GET with `max-results=100` and returns only the first page. All sibling listing methods — `getPlans()` (line 41), `getBranches()` (line 83), `getPlanBranches()` (line 522), `getLinkedRepositories()` (line 499) — use the shared `paginate()` helper. The inconsistency is clear and intentional only by omission (no comment explains the single-page design). `getProjects()` is exposed to the agent via `BambooService.getProjects()` and is used for project-key lookups; silently returning at most 100 projects is a functional gap for large Bamboo instances. The `paginate()` helper at line 624 is a direct fit — it takes a `pageSize` and `fetchPage` lambda and handles offset advancement and the short-page termination condition. Since Bamboo project endpoints follow the same pagination contract as plans and branches, this is a safe mechanical change.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Wrap `getProjects()` in `paginate(pageSize = 100, maxPages = 50, label = "projects") { start -> get<BambooProjectListResponse>("/rest/api/latest/project?max-results=100&start-index=$start").map { it.projects.project } }`, matching the pattern of `getPlans()`.

confirmed.title: Dead private method: BuildDashboardPanel.startMonitoring() is never called
confirmed.description: The private method `startMonitoring()` (lines 775–790) was deliberately neutered as part of the T-B2/B3-b migration (polling lifecycle moved to BuildMonitorService's focusBuild subscription). The KDoc explicitly says it 'no longer calls BuildMonitorService.startPolling()'. No call site for `startMonitoring()` exists anywhere in the codebase — the three occurrences are all in comments. The method body still launches a coroutine on `panelScope` to resolve a branch and set `headerLabel.text`, which is now redundant because the stateFlow collector already updates `headerLabel.text` on every BuildState change.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete the entire `startMonitoring()` method (lines 775–790). It has zero callers and its header-label update is already covered by the stateFlow collector at line 514. No test references it as a call site (BuildDashboardPanelDropdownTest explicitly documents it is NOT called).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at BuildDashboardPanel.kt lines 775-790. Grep of the entire bamboo module returns three matches for `startMonitoring`: line 559 (a comment), line 766 (the KDoc above the method), and line 775 (the method definition itself). There are zero call sites. The test file BuildDashboardPanelDropdownTest.kt at line 204 explicitly documents '`startMonitoring()` drops `monitorService.startPolling()` → same: no monitor call', confirming the test suite knows the method is intentionally neutered. The method body at lines 775-790 launches a panelScope coroutine that only updates `headerLabel.text` — functionality already covered by the stateFlow collector at line 514. The KDoc at line 766-773 confirms this was intentionally left as a header-label updater after T-B2/B3-b migration. Removing it is a pure dead-code cleanup with no risk of regression.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Delete the entire `startMonitoring()` method (lines 775-790) and its KDoc (lines 765-774). The stateFlow collector at line 514 already handles `headerLabel.text` updates on every `BuildState` change, making this method fully redundant.

confirmed.title: Dead public method: BuildMonitorService.switchBranch() has no callers
confirmed.description: The public `switchBranch(planKey, newBranch, intervalMs)` method (line 203) is referenced only in comments in BuildDashboardPanel.kt (twice, explaining it is no longer used). The T-B2/B3-b migration removed all direct `switchBranch` call sites; the KDoc in BuildDashboardPanelDropdownTest explicitly states 'switchBranch/stopPolling are absent from the new dropdown path'. No caller outside the bamboo module exists either. The method simply calls `_stateFlow.value = null; startPolling(...)` — callers now go through the focusBuild subscription instead.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete the `switchBranch()` method (lines 203–207). Its only remaining references are comments documenting that it has been removed. `startPolling` is still reachable from the focusBuild subscription path.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. `BuildMonitorService.switchBranch()` at line 203 has no call sites in production code. All references in BuildDashboardPanel.kt (lines 266, 278, 454, 487, 656, 734) are comments explicitly documenting that the method is NO LONGER called, consistent with the T-B2/B3-b migration. The CLAUDE.md module notes for bamboo also document 'BuildDashboardPanel no longer calls startPolling, switchBranch, or stopPolling directly — all three were removed.' The test file (BuildDashboardPanelDropdownTest.kt lines 15, 22, 38, 210) references it only in KDoc comments confirming its absence. Deleting the 5-line method is safe.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Delete lines 203–207 (the `switchBranch` method body and its opening/closing braces).

confirmed.title: Dead parameter: StageDetailPanel.showLog() errors parameter is always ignored
confirmed.description: The `errors: List<BuildError>` parameter of `showLog()` (line 298) is never read in the method body — the KDoc says 'Parsed errors (unused now — ConsoleView handles highlighting natively)'. All 7 call sites in BuildDashboardPanel.kt pass either `emptyList()` (6 sites) or a live parsed list (1 site, line 1162). Either way the value is discarded. The one site that passes a non-empty list (line 1162) performs `BuildLogParser.parse(logContent)` solely to produce the argument, which is work that has zero effect.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Remove the `errors` parameter from `showLog()`. Update the signature to `fun showLog(logText: String)` and remove the corresponding argument at all 7 call sites. Separately delete the `val errors = BuildLogParser.parse(logContent)` local variable at BuildDashboardPanel.kt line 1161 (the `BuildLogParser` object and its tests are still needed for agent-side log parsing; only this local call site is dead).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. The `errors: List<BuildError>` parameter of `showLog()` at line 298 is documented as unused ('Parsed errors (unused now — ConsoleView handles highlighting natively)') and the method body does not reference it at all. There are 7 call sites: 6 pass `emptyList()` and 1 (BuildDashboardPanel.kt line 1162) performs `BuildLogParser.parse(logContent)` solely to produce the argument, which is then immediately discarded. The fix removes the parameter and also the wasted parse call at line 1161. The finding correctly notes BuildLogParser itself is still needed for agent-side log parsing — only this UI call site is dead.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Remove the `errors: List<BuildError>` parameter from `showLog()` signature; update all 7 call sites to pass only `logText`; delete the dead `val errors = BuildLogParser.parse(logContent)` at BuildDashboardPanel.kt line 1161.

confirmed.title: Dead enum value: TriggerMode.FULL_BUILD is never passed by any external caller
confirmed.description: The `TriggerMode.FULL_BUILD` enum constant (line 48) is only referenced within ManualStageDialog.kt itself (title/button-label string dispatch). Neither BuildDashboardPanel.kt nor AutomationPanel.kt (the only two external callers of ManualStageDialog) pass this value — BuildDashboardPanel uses STAGE and CUSTOM_STAGES; AutomationPanel uses only CUSTOM_STAGES. The KDoc already marks it as a 'Legacy escape hatch'. It is not a reflection/extension-point target. Dead branching code in ManualStageDialog (lines 127, 132, 638) will never execute.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete the `FULL_BUILD` enum constant and its handling branches in ManualStageDialog (lines 127, 132, 638, and the KDoc references). Both remaining modes (STAGE, CUSTOM_STAGES) are actively used. If a full-build trigger is ever needed again, it is identical to CUSTOM_STAGES with all stages selected.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. `TriggerMode.FULL_BUILD` (line 48) is never passed by any external caller. BuildDashboardPanel.kt line 1243 uses the no-arg constructor which defaults to `TriggerMode.STAGE`; line 1262 explicitly passes `TriggerMode.CUSTOM_STAGES`. AutomationPanel.kt (the only other caller, lines 1002–1006) also uses `TriggerMode.CUSTOM_STAGES`. The `FULL_BUILD` branches inside ManualStageDialog.kt (lines 127, 132, 638) are therefore unreachable dead code. The KDoc already marks it 'Legacy escape hatch'. Removing it does not affect any reachable path.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Delete the `FULL_BUILD` enum constant (line 48) and its handling branches at ManualStageDialog.kt lines 127, 132, and 638, plus the KDoc references at lines 37 and 82.

confirmed.title: Dead DTO field: BambooTestResultsDto.quarantined is deserialized but never read
confirmed.description: The `quarantined: Int = 0` field at line 307 in `BambooTestResultsDto` is deserialized from the Bamboo JSON response but never referenced in any production code. The fields `all`, `successful`, `failed`, `skipped`, `failedTests`, and `successfulTests` are all used; `quarantined` is not. No agent tool, service mapper, or UI panel reads it.
confirmed.evidence:
307:    val quarantined: Int = 0,
// rg '\.quarantined\b' --include='*.kt' src/ returns zero results
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete the `quarantined` field from `BambooTestResultsDto`. If Bamboo quarantine data is needed in the future it can be re-added with an explicit consumer.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. `BambooTestResultsDto.quarantined: Int = 0` at line 307 is deserialized from JSON but never accessed anywhere in production code. A project-wide grep returns zero `.quarantined` reads in main/ sources. Removing the field causes kotlinx.serialization to silently ignore the JSON key if Bamboo returns it — this is the correct behavior and carries no risk.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Delete the `quarantined: Int = 0` field (line 307) from `BambooTestResultsDto`.

confirmed.title: Dead DTO field: BambooJobResultDto.buildDuration is deserialized but never read
confirmed.description: The `buildDuration: Long = 0` field at line 139 in `BambooJobResultDto` duplicates `buildDurationInSeconds: Long = 0` (line 138). All mappers in `BambooBuildStructureMapper.toBuildState()` and `BambooServiceImpl.toBuildStageData()` use `buildDurationInSeconds`; `buildDuration` is never referenced.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete the `buildDuration` field from `BambooJobResultDto`. It is a Bamboo API artifact (the API returns both milliseconds and seconds representations) that the codebase never uses.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. `BambooJobResultDto.buildDuration: Long = 0` at line 139 duplicates `buildDurationInSeconds: Long = 0` at line 138. All mappers (`BambooBuildStructureMapper.toBuildState()` and `BambooServiceImpl.toBuildStageData()`) consume `buildDurationInSeconds`; zero reads of `.buildDuration` appear in main/ sources. The Bamboo API returning both millisecond and second representations is expected but the millisecond field is unused.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Delete the `buildDuration: Long = 0` field (line 139) from `BambooJobResultDto`.

confirmed.title: Dead DTO field: BambooPlanDto.shortKey is deserialized but never read
confirmed.description: The `shortKey: String = ""` field at line 51 in `BambooPlanDto` is deserialized but never accessed in any production or test file. The fields actually consumed are `key`, `name`, `shortName`, `enabled`, `type`, and `projectKey`.
confirmed.evidence:
51:    val shortKey: String = "",
// rg '\.shortKey\b' --include='*.kt' src/ returns zero results
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete the `shortKey` field from `BambooPlanDto`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. `BambooPlanDto.shortKey: String = ""` at line 51 is deserialized but never accessed in production code — zero `.shortKey` reads in main/ sources. Test files (BambooApiClientPaginationTest.kt, BambooPlanJobOrderTest.kt, BambooServiceImplGetPlansProjectKeyTest.kt) include it only as fixture JSON or constructor arguments for building test DTOs; none read the field back from a mapped object. Removal is safe.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Delete the `shortKey: String = ""` field (line 51) from `BambooPlanDto`.

confirmed.title: Pervasive dead DTO pagination metadata fields: size/maxResult/startIndex on collection wrappers are never read
confirmed.description: Every Bamboo paginated collection wrapper DTO declares `size: Int`, `maxResult: Int`, and/or `startIndex: Int` to mirror the Bamboo JSON envelope. None of these fields are ever read by production code — only the inner list field (`.plan`, `.branch`, `.result`, `.variable`, `.stage`, etc.) is accessed. Affected wrappers: BambooProjectCollection (line 15 size), BambooPlanCollection (lines 42-44), BambooBranchCollection (lines 74-76), BambooResultCollection (line 92), BambooStageCollection (line 112), BambooJobResultCollection (line 128), BambooVcsRevisionCollection (line 157), BambooVariableContextCollection (line 194), BambooVariableCollection (line 213), BambooBuildChangeCollection (line 237), BambooSearchResponse (lines 259-261), BambooTestCaseCollection (line 314), BambooChangesetResultList (line 375), BambooLinkedRepositoryListResponse (lines 399-401). The `paginate()` loop in BambooApiClient explicitly documents it does NOT trust the server-echoed `startIndex`/`size` — it uses `items.size` (the Kotlin list size) instead.
confirmed.evidence:
42:data class BambooPlanCollection(
43:    val size: Int = 0,
44:    @SerialName("max-result") val maxResult: Int = 25,
45:    @SerialName("start-index") val startIndex: Int = 0,
46:    val plan: List<BambooPlanDto> = emptyList()
// BambooApiClient.paginate() comment: "never from the server-echoed start-index/size fields"
// rg produces zero results for any read of these fields in production code
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete all `size`, `maxResult`, and `startIndex` fields from the collection wrapper DTOs. The `paginate()` loop uses `items.size` (Kotlin list length) for cursor advancement and termination, not these server-echoed values. The kotlinx.serialization `@SerialName` fields will simply be ignored during deserialization if the JSON contains them — removing the declared field is safe.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. The `paginate()` loop at BambooApiClient.kt lines 624–651 explicitly derives cursor advancement from `items.size` (the Kotlin list count of actually-returned items) and never reads the DTO-level `size`, `maxResult`, or `startIndex` fields. The method's KDoc (lines 612–614) documents this intentional design. All affected collection wrappers (BambooPlanCollection, BambooBranchCollection, BambooResultCollection, BambooStageCollection, BambooJobResultCollection, BambooVcsRevisionCollection, BambooVariableContextCollection, BambooVariableCollection, BambooBuildChangeCollection, BambooSearchResponse, BambooTestCaseCollection, BambooChangesetResultList, BambooLinkedRepositoryListResponse, BambooPlanBranchCollection) have these fields deserialized but zero production reads. Note: `BambooArtifact.size: Long` (line 358) is genuinely used (line BambooServiceImpl.kt:497) but is a file-size field on a non-collection DTO and is not part of this finding.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Delete all `size: Int`, `maxResult: Int`, and `startIndex: Int` fields from the listed collection wrapper DTOs. The `paginate()` loop correctly ignores them and uses the Kotlin list length for cursor arithmetic.

confirmed.title: Dead DTO fields: BambooProjectDetailResponse.key and .name are never accessed
confirmed.description: BambooProjectDetailResponse (line 27) declares `key: String = ""` and `name: String = ""` in addition to `plans: BambooPlanCollection`. The only caller `getProjectPlans()` in BambooApiClient.kt maps with `.map { it.plans.plan }`, so `key` and `name` are deserialized but immediately discarded. No other code path reads them.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete the `key` and `name` fields from `BambooProjectDetailResponse`. If the response envelope's project key or name is needed by a future caller, they can be re-added then.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. `BambooProjectDetailResponse.key: String = ""` and `.name: String = ""` at lines 28–29 are deserialized but immediately discarded. The sole caller `getProjectPlans()` at BambooApiClient.kt line 56–57 maps `.map { it.plans.plan }`, accessing only `it.plans`. No other code path accesses these two fields. Removing them is safe; kotlinx.serialization will silently ignore the JSON keys.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Delete the `key: String = ""` and `name: String = ""` fields (lines 28–29) from `BambooProjectDetailResponse`.

confirmed.title: Duplicate BambooApiClient instantiation in BuildFailureBridgeStartupActivity bypasses the cached project service
confirmed.description: The private `bambooClientOrNull(project)` factory (lines 145–157) duplicates the URL-read + timeout-read + CredentialStore + BambooApiClient construction that already lives in `BambooServiceImpl.client` (which is `internal` and reachable from this file in the same module). Every failed build notification creates a brand-new OkHttp client (wrapped inside BambooApiClient via HttpClientFactory) instead of reusing the cached singleton. This wastes connection-pool and thread-pool resources on every build failure, and the construction logic (URL trimming, timeout clamping) may diverge from BambooServiceImpl over time.
confirmed.evidence:
145:    private fun bambooClientOrNull(project: Project): BambooApiClient? {
146:        val settings = PluginSettings.getInstance(project)
147:        val baseUrl = settings.connections.bambooUrl.orEmpty().trimEnd('/')
148:        if (baseUrl.isBlank()) return null
149:        val timeouts = HttpClientFactory.timeoutsFromSettings(project)
150:        val credentialStore = CredentialStore()
151:        return BambooApiClient(...)
// BambooServiceImpl.client is internal and visible in this module
confirmed.suggestedFix: Replace `bambooClientOrNull(project)` with `(project.getService(BambooService::class.java) as? BambooServiceImpl)?.client`. This reuses the cached `BambooApiClient` (no new OkHttp pool) and guarantees that URL/timeout configuration stays in sync with BambooServiceImpl. Delete the private `bambooClientOrNull` method and its imports (CredentialStore, ServiceType, HttpClientFactory).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real: `BuildFailureBridgeStartupActivity.bambooClientOrNull()` (lines 145–157) constructs a new `BambooApiClient` (and a new OkHttp client with its own connection pool) on every call. `BambooServiceImpl.client` is indeed `internal` and accessible from the same `:bamboo` module. The construction logic is almost identical to `BambooServiceImpl` but lacks URL-change invalidation. However, `bambooClientOrNull` is only called from `resolveCommitSha` (triggered on `BuildFinished`/FAILED events, not every poll tick), so the actual resource waste is moderate. Severity downgraded to Low: the functional impact is minimal (no stale URL — reads fresh settings each call; no correctness problem) and the fix is mechanical. Fix is not harmful: replacing with `(project.getService(BambooServiceImpl::class.java) as? BambooServiceImpl)?.client` reuses the URL-invalidating cached client and deletes dead code.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Replace `bambooClientOrNull(project)` with `project.getService(BambooServiceImpl::class.java)?.client` and delete the private factory method and its imports (CredentialStore, ServiceType, HttpClientFactory).

confirmed.title: BuildMonitorService creates its own BambooApiClient that becomes stale after Bamboo URL changes
confirmed.description: BuildMonitorService owns a second BambooApiClient (lines 47–58) that is created once from settings and cached as `_apiClient`. Unlike BambooServiceImpl.client (which invalidates when bambooUrl changes via `cachedBaseUrl` comparison), BuildMonitorService never re-checks the URL. If the user updates the Bamboo URL in settings, the monitor keeps polling the old host until the IDE restarts. This also means there are three independent BambooApiClient instances per project (BambooServiceImpl, BuildMonitorService, BuildFailureBridgeStartupActivity per-call), each with their own OkHttp connection pool, instead of one shared pool.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Remove BuildMonitorService's private BambooApiClient entirely. Inject BambooServiceImpl (or its internal `client` getter) into BuildMonitorService so all Bamboo HTTP calls in the module share the single URL-change-aware client from BambooServiceImpl. The cleanest form: add an internal `getPollClient(): BambooApiClient?` method on BambooServiceImpl (delegating to its existing `client` getter) and call that from BuildMonitorService instead of constructing its own client.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real: `BuildMonitorService._apiClient` (lines 47–58) is constructed once with the URL from settings and cached forever (`.also { _apiClient = it }`). Unlike `BambooServiceImpl.client`, there is no `cachedBaseUrl` comparison that would rebuild the client on URL change. A user changing the Bamboo URL in settings would need to restart the IDE for `BuildMonitorService` to pick it up. That said, severity is Low not Medium: (1) URL changes are rare, expected to require a restart by most users; (2) the test constructor explicitly sets `_apiClient` directly (line 93–100), confirming the caching is intentional for that seam. The third-client issue (alongside BambooServiceImpl and BuildFailureBridgeStartupActivity) is a maintenance concern. Fix is not harmful: delegating to `BambooServiceImpl.client` via a `getPollClient()` method keeps tests working via the test-constructor seam.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add `internal fun getPollClient(): BambooApiClient? = client` on BambooServiceImpl and replace BuildMonitorService's private `_apiClient`/`apiClient` with calls to `project.getService(BambooServiceImpl::class.java)?.getPollClient()`. Keep the test constructor's explicit `apiClient` param as-is.

confirmed.title: StageDetailPanel uses raw javax.swing.JScrollPane instead of JBScrollPane for the artifacts list
confirmed.description: Line 530 constructs a raw `JScrollPane(artifactsList)` inside `showArtifacts()`. The project convention requires JetBrains UI components exclusively; JBScrollPane applies the IDE's scroll speed settings, theming, and rendering improvements. This is the only scroll pane in the file that is not a JBScrollPane (the others at lines 301 and 474 correctly use JBScrollPane).
confirmed.evidence:
val scrollPane = JScrollPane(artifactsList)   // line 530, inside showArtifacts()
arifactsPanel.add(scrollPane, BorderLayout.CENTER)
confirmed.suggestedFix: Replace `JScrollPane(artifactsList)` with `JBScrollPane(artifactsList).apply { border = JBUI.Borders.empty() }` to match the JBScrollPane usage patterns already present elsewhere in the same file (lines 301, 474).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real: line 530 in `StageDetailPanel.showArtifacts()` uses `JScrollPane(artifactsList)` (raw `javax.swing.JScrollPane`). The `javax.swing.*` wildcard import at line 53 is the source. The only `JScrollPane` in the entire file is this one at line 530 — the finding's claim that lines 301 and 474 use `JBScrollPane` appears to be a hallucination (grep shows no other JScrollPane or JBScrollPane in the file). The finding is still valid: `JScrollPane` violates the JetBrains-components-only convention, and `JBScrollPane` exists for this purpose. Fix is not harmful and is purely additive (drop-in replacement since `JBScrollPane extends JScrollPane`).
confirmed.recommendedAction: Replace `JScrollPane(artifactsList)` at line 530 with `JBScrollPane(artifactsList)` and add `import com.intellij.ui.components.JBScrollPane`.

confirmed.title: StageDetailPanel uses raw javax.swing.JButton for search navigation and log/artifact action buttons instead of JetBrains components
confirmed.description: Lines 91, 96, 147, 648, and 653 create raw `JButton` instances. The JetBrains platform provides themed button components (the `com.intellij.ui.components` package or `ActionButton` for toolbar-style buttons). Raw `JButton` does not adapt to IDE themes on macOS (especially with JetBrains Runtime's custom LAF), potentially producing visual inconsistency in dark mode or with certain platform LAFs.
confirmed.evidence:
private val prevMatchButton = JButton("<")       // line 91
private val nextMatchButton = JButton(">")       // line 96
private val openInEditorButton = JButton("Open full log in editor")  // line 147
private val downloadButton = JButton("Download") // line 648
private val openButton = JButton("Open")         // line 653
confirmed.suggestedFix: Use IntelliJ platform-recommended buttons. For toolbar-style icon buttons (prev/next match), use `com.intellij.ui.components.ActionLink` or an `ActionButton`. For labelled action buttons (Open full log, Download, Open), use `com.intellij.ui.components.JBButton` if available in the target platform version, or wrap in a `panel { button("...") { } }` DSL row; at minimum import `com.intellij.ui.components` equivalents rather than raw `javax.swing.JButton`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real: lines 91, 96, 147, 648, and 653 all use raw `JButton`. The `javax.swing.*` wildcard import provides them. There is no `JBButton` import in the file. The project convention requires JetBrains components. However, severity remains Low: in practice IntelliJ's JetBrains Runtime LAF adapts `JButton` reasonably well on macOS/Linux for the target IntelliJ IDEA 2025.1+, so the visual inconsistency is subtle rather than jarring. Fix is not harmful.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Replace `JButton` usages at lines 91, 96, 147, 648, 653 with `com.intellij.ui.components.JBButton` (a drop-in subclass). For the icon-only prev/next match buttons, `ActionLink` is an alternative but requires wiring differently.

confirmed.title: ManualStageDialog uses raw javax.swing.JTable for the variables preview instead of a JetBrains component
confirmed.description: Line 436 constructs a raw `JTable(tableModel)` for the variables preview section in CUSTOM_STAGES mode. Per project conventions, only JetBrains UI components should be used. The IDE provides `com.intellij.ui.table.JBTable` which participates in IntelliJ's row-height, selection-colors, and accessibility support — raw JTable misses these adaptations.
confirmed.evidence:
val table = JTable(tableModel).apply {  // line 436
    setDefaultEditor(Any::class.java, null)
    ...
}
confirmed.suggestedFix: Replace `JTable(tableModel)` with `com.intellij.ui.table.JBTable(tableModel)`. JBTable extends JTable, so the rest of the configuration (columnModel, rowHeight, fillsViewportHeight, etc.) is compatible without changes.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real: line 436 in `ManualStageDialog` uses `JTable(tableModel)` (raw `javax.swing.JTable`). The file imports `javax.swing.JTable` at line 29. `com.intellij.ui.table.JBTable` is the JetBrains equivalent and is a drop-in subclass. Convention violation is confirmed. Fix is not harmful: `JBTable` extends `JTable`, so all subsequent `.apply { }` block operations (setDefaultEditor, setSelectionMode, columnModel, rowHeight, fillsViewportHeight, autoResizeMode, via `updateRowHeights`) are compatible without changes.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Replace `JTable(tableModel)` at line 436 with `com.intellij.ui.table.JBTable(tableModel)` and update the import.

confirmed.title: BambooApiClient.get(): no test for 429 rate-limit or 5xx server-error HTTP responses
confirmed.description: The `get()` private method maps HTTP 429 to `RATE_LIMITED` and any other non-2xx to `SERVER_ERROR` (lines 413-414). No test in any ApiClient test file exercises 429 or 500/503 responses against any of the public endpoints (`getLatestResult`, `getPlans`, `getBranches`, etc.). The `BambooApiClientTest` only tests 401, the XSRF path, and specific 404s, leaving the two most common transient-failure codes completely untested.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add to `BambooApiClientTest`: (1) `server.enqueue(MockResponse().setResponseCode(429))` then call `getPlans()` and assert `(result as ApiResult.Error).type == ErrorType.RATE_LIMITED`; (2) `server.enqueue(MockResponse().setResponseCode(503))` and assert `ErrorType.SERVER_ERROR`. Additionally test that `getLatestResult` on a 500 returns `SERVER_ERROR`, since that method is the polling hot path. Use `@ParameterizedTest` with `@ValueSource(ints = [429, 500, 502, 503])` to keep it concise.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real: searching all test files under `bamboo/src/test/` finds zero tests exercising `429` or any 5xx response code. `BambooApiClientTest` covers 401, XSRF, 403, 404, and specific formats but not 429 or 5xx. `BambooApiClient.get()` at lines 413–414 maps 429 to `RATE_LIMITED` and all other non-2xx codes to `SERVER_ERROR` — these paths exist in production but have zero test coverage. However, severity is downgraded from High to Low: (1) the mapping logic (a `when` branch on the HTTP code) is trivially correct by inspection and not subject to regression; (2) 429 and 5xx are handled identically to 403/404 in the surrounding `when` block so there is no special logic to verify; (3) the callers all propagate `ApiResult.Error` up correctly. The missing tests represent a coverage gap but not a likely source of bugs. Fix is not harmful.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add tests to `BambooApiClientTest`: one for 429 asserting `ErrorType.RATE_LIMITED` and at least one for a 5xx (e.g. 503) asserting `ErrorType.SERVER_ERROR`. A `@ParameterizedTest` with `@ValueSource(ints = [500, 502, 503])` keeps it concise.

confirmed.title: BambooApiClient.get(): no test for IOException/network-failure propagation to NETWORK_ERROR
confirmed.description: The `get()` catch block (line 417-419) wraps `IOException` as `ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.NETWORK_ERROR)`. No existing MockWebServer-based test exercises this path — all tests enqueue valid responses. `BuildMonitorServiceTest.pollOnce handles API error gracefully` mocks the result at the mock layer, not at the HTTP layer, so the IOException branch is never triggered in any test. This path is the most common production failure (firewall drops, VPN disconnects, DNS failures).
confirmed.suggestedFix: In `BambooApiClientTest`, call `server.shutdown()` before making the request (MockWebServer returns a connection-refused IOException) and assert `(result as ApiResult.Error).type == ErrorType.NETWORK_ERROR`. Test this for at least `getLatestResult`, `getPlans`, and `queueBuildWithStageSelection` as they are the highest-traffic endpoints. Pattern: `server.shutdown(); val result = client.getLatestResult("PROJ-BUILD"); assertTrue(result is ApiResult.Error); assertEquals(ErrorType.NETWORK_ERROR, (result as ApiResult.Error).type)`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: The IOException catch block at BambooApiClient.kt lines 417-419 wraps network failures as ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.NETWORK_ERROR), which is correct production code. The gap is confirmed: all server.shutdown() calls in the test files appear only in @AfterEach teardown methods (not before a live request), so no test causes a real IOException at the HTTP layer. The production behavior is already correct — this is a pure test-coverage gap, not a behavioral bug. The suggested fix (call server.shutdown() before the request to trigger connection-refused) is safe and documents the existing contract. Severity downgraded from High to Low because the production code path is correct and already tested indirectly via mock-layer tests; only the HTTP-layer exercise is absent.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add a test in BambooApiClientTest that calls server.shutdown() before making a request and asserts ErrorType.NETWORK_ERROR. Covers at least getLatestResult and getPlans as the highest-traffic endpoints.

confirmed.title: BambooApiClient.get(): no test for non-JSON 200 response body triggering PARSE_ERROR
confirmed.description: Lines 393-399 check for an unexpected `Content-Type` on a 2xx response and return `PARSE_ERROR` (e.g., when a reverse proxy returns `text/html` for a valid endpoint). Lines 402-407 catch JSON parse failures and return `PARSE_ERROR`. Neither branch is tested. The `AUTH_REDIRECT` path (HTML body on `rerunFailedJobs`/`enablePlanBranch`) is covered via `postForm`, but the equivalent GET-path protection (non-JSON Content-Type guard on `get()`) has no test.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add to `BambooApiClientTest`: (1) `server.enqueue(MockResponse().setResponseCode(200).setHeader("Content-Type", "text/html").setBody("<html>proxy page</html>"))`; call `getPlans()` and assert `ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR`. (2) `server.enqueue(MockResponse().setBody("not-valid-json"))` then call `getLatestResult("PROJ-BUILD")` and assert `ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Both the content-type guard (lines 393-399) and the JSON parse-failure catch (lines 402-407) inside the get() method exist and are correct. No test in any BambooApiClient* test file exercises either path — all tests enqueue valid JSON responses. The AUTH_REDIRECT path in postForm is covered by rerunFailedJobs and enablePlanBranch tests, but the analogous GET-path content-type guard is not. This is a pure coverage gap; the production code is correct. Severity downgraded from Medium to Low since this is not a behavioral bug.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add two tests to BambooApiClientTest: (1) enqueue a 200 with Content-Type: text/html and assert ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR; (2) enqueue a 200 with malformed JSON body and assert ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR.

confirmed.title: BambooApiClient.paginate(): no test for error returned on a non-first page
confirmed.description: The `paginate()` helper (lines 624-651) short-circuits on any page error via `is ApiResult.Error -> return result`. The existing pagination tests only cover the success path (single-page, two-page, echoed-start-index quirk) and a single first-page 401 failure. No test exercises the case where the first page succeeds but a subsequent page returns an error (e.g., the server goes down or returns 500 mid-pagination). This means the aggregated partial result is silently discarded and the caller sees a network error rather than partial data.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add to `BambooApiClientPaginationTest`: enqueue a full page-1 success (100 items) then enqueue a 500 for page 2. Call `getPlans()` and assert the result is `ApiResult.Error` (not partial success). This documents the current contract that a mid-pagination error discards all collected data, making the behavior explicit and catchable.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: The paginate() helper at lines 634-636 returns the error immediately when any page fails, discarding all previously collected items. BambooApiClientPaginationTest covers: single-page, two-page, first-page error, and the server-echoes-start-index-0 quirk. There is no test for the case where page 1 succeeds but page 2+ returns an error. The current behavior (discard partial results on mid-pagination error) is a documented design choice, not a bug. The finding correctly identifies the gap. Severity downgraded from Medium to Low: the partial-discard semantics are intentional and the fix only adds a documenting test.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add a test that enqueues a full first page followed by a 500, calls getPlans(), and asserts ApiResult.Error to document the contract that mid-pagination errors discard all collected data.

confirmed.title: BambooApiClient.stopBuild(): no error-path tests (only verifies the X-Atlassian-Token header on 200)
confirmed.description: The only test for `stopBuild` (`stopBuild sets X-Atlassian-Token header`, `BambooApiClientTest` line 360) verifies the HTTP method and header on a 200 response. There is no test for 401 (`AUTH_FAILED`), 403 (`FORBIDDEN`), 404 (`NOT_FOUND`), the `AUTH_REDIRECT` path (200+HTML), or `NETWORK_ERROR` (IOException). The `put()` method handles all these cases (lines 597-605) but they are untested. The `BambooServiceImpl.stopBuild()` maps each error type to a specific hint string that is equally untested.
confirmed.evidence:
```kotlin
@Test
fun `stopBuild sets X-Atlassian-Token header`() = runTest {
    server.enqueue(MockResponse().setResponseCode(200))
    client.stopBuild("PROJ-BUILD-42")
    val recorded = server.takeRequest()
    assertEquals("PUT", recorded.method)
    assertEquals("no-check", recorded.getHeader("X-Atlassian-Token"))
}
```
— BambooApiClientTest.kt lines 360-368. Only one test for `stopBuild`, only the success/header path.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add to `BambooApiClientTest`: (1) `server.enqueue(MockResponse().setResponseCode(401))` → assert `ErrorType.AUTH_FAILED`; (2) 403 → `FORBIDDEN`; (3) 404 → `NOT_FOUND`; (4) 200 with `Content-Type: text/html` → `AUTH_REDIRECT` (mirrors the existing `cancelBuild` test pattern at line 337 and `rerunFailedJobs` at line 387).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: there is exactly one test for stopBuild (lines 360-368 in BambooApiClientTest.kt), covering only the happy-path header check on a 200 response. The put() method at lines 574-606 handles 401→AUTH_FAILED, 403→FORBIDDEN, 404→NOT_FOUND, and AUTH_REDIRECT on 200+HTML. None of these error paths are tested for stopBuild. All other write operations (cancelBuild, rerunFailedJobs, enablePlanBranch) have error-path tests. The production code is correct; this is a test-coverage gap. Severity downgraded from Medium to Low.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add error-path tests for stopBuild: 401→AUTH_FAILED, 403→FORBIDDEN, 404→NOT_FOUND, and 200 with text/html content-type→AUTH_REDIRECT, mirroring the existing cancelBuild and enablePlanBranch test patterns.

confirmed.title: BambooApiClient.queueBuildWithStageSelection(): no test for parse failure on the queue-response body
confirmed.description: Lines 269-275 catch a JSON parse failure on the `BambooQueueResponse` body and return `ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR`. All existing tests for `queueBuildWithStageSelection` supply well-formed JSON responses. No test exercises a successful (200) POST to the queue endpoint followed by a malformed JSON body (e.g., `{"buildResultKey":null}` missing required fields or truncated JSON). This is a real edge case when Bamboo returns a redirect or HTML page with status 200.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add to `BambooApiClientStageSelectionTest`: `server.enqueue(MockResponse().setResponseCode(200).setBody("not-json"))`; call `queueBuildWithStageSelection("PROJ-BUILD", emptyMap(), null)` and assert `(result as ApiResult.Error).type == ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Lines 269-275 catch a JSON deserialization exception when parsing the BambooQueueResponse body and return ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR. All tests in BambooApiClientStageSelectionTest supply well-formed JSON in their responses. No test enqueues a malformed body for a 200 response on the queue endpoint. The production catch clause is correct. This is a pure coverage gap. Severity downgraded from Medium to Low.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add a test in BambooApiClientStageSelectionTest that enqueues a 200 response with body 'not-json' and asserts ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR.

confirmed.title: BambooApiClient.downloadArtifact(): no tests at API-client level (only SafetyTest covers basename/containment)
confirmed.description: `downloadArtifact()` (lines 451-478) has three testable paths: (1) internal URL uses authenticated `httpClient`, external URL uses `sharedPool`; (2) non-2xx response returns `false`; (3) `IOException` returns `false`. `ArtifactDownloadSafetyTest` tests only the `StageDetailPanel.safeArtifactBasename` / `isContainedIn` helpers — it never exercises the actual `BambooApiClient.downloadArtifact()` logic. The cross-origin security boundary (line 457 `isInternal = artifactUrl.startsWith(baseUrl)`) is untested. There is no test that the authenticated client is used for same-origin URLs and the shared pool for cross-origin.
confirmed.evidence:
```kotlin
val isInternal = artifactUrl.startsWith(baseUrl)
val client = if (isInternal) httpClient else HttpClientFactory.sharedPool
```
— BambooApiClient.kt lines 457-458. No test in `src/test` calls `downloadArtifact()` directly.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a `BambooApiClientDownloadArtifactTest`: (1) use MockWebServer as the base URL, enqueue a 200 with binary body, call `downloadArtifact(server.url("/artifact").toString(), tempFile)`, and assert `true` and file content; (2) enqueue a 404, assert returns `false`; (3) for the cross-origin test, enqueue on a separate second MockWebServer and assert the request has no `Authorization` header (proving `sharedPool` was used, not `httpClient`).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed: ArtifactDownloadSafetyTest (lines 1-120) tests only the StageDetailPanel.safeArtifactBasename and isContainedIn helper methods in the UI layer. No test in any bamboo test file calls BambooApiClient.downloadArtifact() directly. The cross-origin security boundary at lines 457-458 — which intentionally uses sharedPool (no auth headers) for external URLs vs the authenticated httpClient for same-origin — is a security-relevant decision that is entirely untested. This is the most impactful of the coverage findings because the cross-origin token-leakage prevention is a security property. Keeping at Medium severity (not escalating to High/Critical) because no bug is present — the production code is correct — but the untested security boundary is more consequential than style-only coverage gaps.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add BambooApiClientDownloadArtifactTest covering: (1) same-origin 200→returns true + file written; (2) non-2xx→returns false; (3) cross-origin request has no Authorization header (proves sharedPool used, not authenticated httpClient).

confirmed.title: BambooServiceImpl.getTestResults(): no tests at service level for success, error, or not-configured paths
confirmed.description: `BambooServiceImpl.getTestResults()` (lines 200-239) maps the `BambooJobTestResultDto` into a `TestResultsData` with `FailedTestData` entries, covering three branches: (a) not configured, (b) API error, (c) success with failed/successful test counts. None of these branches has a test. The only existing `BambooTestResultConverter` tests (`BambooTestResultConverterReDoSTest`) target the static converter directly and do not go through the service layer. There is no test that skipped-test counts are correctly propagated, that an empty failed-tests list produces `failedTests = emptyList()`, or that an API error from `getTestResults()` surfaces as `isError = true`.
confirmed.evidence:
```kotlin
override suspend fun getTestResults(resultKey: String): ToolResult<TestResultsData> {
    val api = client ?: return ToolResult(
        data = TestResultsData(total = 0, passed = 0, failed = 0, skipped = 0),
        ...
    )
```
— BambooServiceImpl.kt lines 200-240. `grep -r 'getTestResults' src/test/` returns zero matches.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Create `BambooServiceImplTestResultsTest` with three cases: (1) mock `getTestResults` returning a DTO with 5 passed, 2 failed, 1 skipped — assert `data.total==8`, `data.failed==2`; (2) mock returning `ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.NOT_FOUND, "...")` — assert `isError==true`; (3) set `testClientOverride = null` (or do not set it, leaving `client` null) and assert `isError==true` with the not-configured hint.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed via grep: zero matches for 'getTestResults' in the entire bamboo/src/test/ tree. BambooServiceImpl.getTestResults() (lines 200-239) has three distinct branches: not-configured (client null), API error, and success with mapping. BambooTestResultConverterReDoSTest covers the static converter directly, not the service layer. The production code is correct. This is a pure service-layer coverage gap. Severity downgraded from Medium to Low.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Create BambooServiceImplTestResultsTest with three cases: null client→isError=true with not-configured hint; API error→isError=true; success DTO→correct data mapping (total, passed, failed, skipped counts and FailedTestData list).

confirmed.title: BambooServiceImpl.getPlanVariables(): no test for all-strategies-fail path returning isError=true
confirmed.description: `getPlanVariables()` (lines 307-372) has a three-strategy fallback: Strategy A (`variableContext`), then Strategy C (most-recent build's `getBuildVariables`). The existing `BambooServiceImplStrategyCTest` only tests the A-fails→C-succeeds branch. The all-strategies-fail path (lines 361-372) — when variableContext returns empty and Strategy C also fails — returns `isError = true`. This path has zero test coverage. Additionally, the Strategy A returning empty list (line 319 `contextResult.data.isNotEmpty()`) before falling to Strategy C is untested for the case where variableContext succeeds but with zero items.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add to `BambooServiceImplStrategyCTest` or a new file: (1) mock `getPlanVariableContext` returning `ApiResult.Error` and `getRecentResults` also returning `ApiResult.Error` — assert `result.isError == true`; (2) mock `getPlanVariableContext` returning `ApiResult.Success(emptyList())` and `getRecentResults` returning an empty build list — assert `result.isError == true` (no builds to fall back to).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: BambooServiceImplStrategyCTest has exactly one test: variableContext error→Strategy C succeeds. The all-strategies-fail path (lines 361-371 in BambooServiceImpl.kt) — where both variableContext and Strategy C fail — is not tested. Additionally, the case where variableContext returns ApiResult.Success(emptyList()) (triggering Strategy C) and then Strategy C also fails is untested. Both paths return isError=true. The production code is correct. This is a coverage gap on the error return at lines 366-371. Severity downgraded from Medium to Low.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add tests: (1) variableContext returns ApiResult.Error AND getRecentResults returns ApiResult.Error → result.isError==true; (2) variableContext returns ApiResult.Success(emptyList()) AND getRecentResults returns empty list (no builds) → result.isError==true with the 'variableContext returned empty' message.

confirmed.title: BuildMonitorService.pollOnce(): checkForNewerBuild() is silently swallowed via unregistered mock — newer-build detection path has no positive-case test
confirmed.description: `pollOnce()` calls `checkForNewerBuild()` (line 223) only on terminal builds. `checkForNewerBuild()` calls `apiClient.getRunningAndQueuedBuilds()`. In `BuildMonitorServiceTest`, `apiClient` is a strict (non-relaxed) mock with no stub for `getRunningAndQueuedBuilds`. The `catch (e: Exception)` in `checkForNewerBuild()` (lines 368-372) swallows the `MockKException` that mockk throws for unstubbed calls, so the tests pass but the newer-build detection is silently skipped in every test that produces a terminal state. No test verifies that `buildState.newerBuild` is populated when a higher-numbered build is running, or that `BuildState.newerBuild` is `null` when no newer builds exist.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add `coEvery { apiClient.getRunningAndQueuedBuilds(any()) } returns ApiResult.Success(emptyList())` to `BuildMonitorServiceTest.stubPlanStructure()` (alongside the existing `getPlanStructure` stub). Then add two tests: (1) mock `getRunningAndQueuedBuilds` returning a DTO with `buildNumber = 43` (higher than the current 42) — assert `stateFlow.value?.newerBuild?.buildNumber == 43`; (2) `getRunningAndQueuedBuilds` returns an empty list — assert `stateFlow.value?.newerBuild == null`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. BuildMonitorServiceTest.kt line 27 creates a strict (non-relaxed) MockK for apiClient. stubPlanStructure() at line 32 stubs only getPlanStructure; there is no stub for getRunningAndQueuedBuilds anywhere in the file. Every test that exercises a terminal build state (lifeCycleState='Finished' or state='Successful'/'Failed') reaches checkForNewerBuild() at BuildMonitorService.kt line 353, which calls apiClient.getRunningAndQueuedBuilds(). MockK throws MockKException for the unstubbed call. The catch block at lines 368-372 swallows any non-CancellationException and returns null, so tests pass silently but the newer-build detection path is never exercised. No test asserts stateFlow.value?.newerBuild is populated or null. This is a real silent-test-pass situation masking an untested feature path. Adding the stub to stubPlanStructure() is safe and unambiguously correct.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add coEvery { apiClient.getRunningAndQueuedBuilds(any()) } returns ApiResult.Success(emptyList()) to BuildMonitorServiceTest.stubPlanStructure() to prevent MockKException swallowing. Then add two tests: (1) getRunningAndQueuedBuilds returns a DTO with buildNumber=43 when current is 42 and assert stateFlow.value?.newerBuild?.buildNumber == 43; (2) returns empty list and assert stateFlow.value?.newerBuild == null.

confirmed.title: BuildMonitorService.pollOnce(): first-poll BuildFinished suppression behavior has no direct test
confirmed.description: `pollOnce()` suppresses `BuildFinished` events on the first poll (`isFirstPoll = previousBuildNumber == null`, lines 233-244) to prevent stale "Build Failed" notifications on IDE startup. While `pollOnce emits BuildLogReady on first terminal poll` documents that `BuildLogReady` IS emitted on first poll, there is no test asserting that `BuildFinished` is NOT emitted on the very first terminal poll. The absence of this test means removing the `!isFirstPoll` guard would go undetected by the test suite, causing spurious startup notifications.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a test: configure a terminal build (Successful/Finished) on the first `pollOnce` call and assert via `eventBus.events.test { expectNoEvents() }` that no `BuildFinished` event is emitted. This directly pins the `!isFirstPoll` guard. Pattern: `service.pollOnce("PROJ-BUILD", "main")` then `eventBus.events.test { expectNoEvents() }` (the current test only checks that `BuildLogReady` IS emitted, not that `BuildFinished` is NOT).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. The existing test 'pollOnce emits BuildLogReady on first terminal poll' (line 189) asserts only that BuildLogReady IS emitted. The comment at line 202 reads 'but NOT BuildFinished' yet the test does not assert absence of BuildFinished. The !isFirstPoll guard at BuildMonitorService.kt line 244 is the sole protection against spurious startup notifications, but removing it would not break any test. This is a real coverage gap for a load-bearing guard. The fix (adding a negative assertion on BuildFinished absence via expectNoEvents or asserting the only event is BuildLogReady) is safe and straightforward.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Extend the 'pollOnce emits BuildLogReady on first terminal poll' test to assert that no BuildFinished event is emitted on the first poll. Either assert the turbine test block sees exactly one item (BuildLogReady) and then expectNoEvents(), or add a separate test that does a single first-poll on a terminal build and asserts eventBus.events.test { expectNoEvents() } after draining to BuildLogReady.

confirmed.title: BuildLogParser.parse(): no test for empty/blank input string
confirmed.description: The `parse()` method (line 26) calls `buildLog.lineSequence()` which works on any string including empty. The tests cover: log with errors, log with warnings, clean log, and generic error. No test exercises `parse("")` or `parse("   \n   ")` (blank/whitespace input). The `.count { it == '\n' }` call on line 28 on an empty string is harmless but untested. Since `getBuildLog()` returns the raw string from the server (and documents that empty body returns `""`), a blank log passed through to the parser should produce an empty list without any indexing failure.
confirmed.evidence:
```kotlin
fun parse(buildLog: String): List<BuildError> {
    val lines = buildLog.lineSequence()
    log.info("[Bamboo:Parser] Starting build log parsing (~${buildLog.count { it == '\n' } + 1} lines)")
```
— BuildLogParser.kt lines 26-28. `BuildLogParserTest` has 4 tests, none with blank/empty input.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add to `BuildLogParserTest`: (1) `BuildLogParser.parse("")` → assert `isEmpty()`; (2) `BuildLogParser.parse("  \n  \n  ")` → assert `isEmpty()`. These are trivial to add and pin the boundary behavior.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed as a coverage gap, but with reassessed severity. The 'returns empty list for clean log' test at BuildLogParserTest.kt line 41 uses a non-empty string that produces no matches, which exercises most of the same code path as empty input. The claim that parse("") and parse("  \n  ") are untested is strictly true. The code at line 27 calls buildLog.count { it == '\n' } + 1 on an empty string which returns 1 (harmless), and lineSequence() on empty input yields no lines, returning emptyList(). There is no crash risk. The gap is real but the actual behavioral coverage difference is minimal.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add two trivial tests to BuildLogParserTest: parse("") -> assertEmpty() and parse("  \n  \n  ") -> assertEmpty(). These are safe to add and pin the boundary contract.

confirmed.title: BambooTestResultConverter.fromTestResultsData(): no tests at all — skipped/successful test output paths and mixed-status suites untested
confirmed.description: `fromTestResultsData()` (lines 94-117) builds a synthetic `BambooTestResultsDto` from the shared domain model and delegates to `toTeamCityMessages()`. It is never called by any test. The existing `BambooTestResultConverterReDoSTest` only calls `toTeamCityMessages()` directly. Additionally, `toTeamCityMessages()` line 78 has a `"skipped"` branch emitting `testIgnored` — no test verifies that a skipped test produces `##teamcity[testIgnored ...]`. Line 33 returns `emptyList()` when `allTests.isEmpty()` — also untested. A test with only passed tests (no failures) is not covered.
confirmed.evidence:
```kotlin
fun fromTestResultsData(testResults: TestResultsData, buildLog: String? = null): List<String> {
    val failedDtos = testResults.failedTests.map { ft ->
        BambooTestCaseDto(className = ft.className, methodName = ft.methodName, status = "failed")
    }
```
— BambooTestResultConverter.kt lines 94-117. `grep -r 'fromTestResultsData' src/test/` returns zero matches.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Create `BambooTestResultConverterTest`: (1) call `fromTestResultsData(TestResultsData(total=3, passed=2, failed=1, skipped=1, failedTests=[...]))` and assert the message list contains `testCount count='3'`, `testFailed`, `testIgnored`, and `testFinished`; (2) call with `TestResultsData(total=0, ...)` and assert `emptyList()`; (3) call `toTeamCityMessages` with a `BambooTestResultsDto` where `failedTests` is empty and `successfulTests` has 2 entries — assert only `testFinished` lines are present (no `testFailed`).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. grep confirms fromTestResultsData() is called only from BuildDashboardPanel.kt line 1210 in production and has zero test coverage. BambooTestResultConverterReDoSTest calls toTeamCityMessages() directly. Three untested paths are real: (1) the 'skipped' branch at BambooTestResultConverter.kt line 76 that emits testIgnored; (2) the allTests.isEmpty() early return at line 34 when called via fromTestResultsData with zero total tests; (3) a passing-only result set (no testFailed lines). The method constructs a synthetic BambooTestResultsDto with successfulTests always empty (line 113), meaning passed tests are silently dropped — this is a real behavioral gap beyond just coverage. The suggested fix is safe and does not risk removing any load-bearing fallback.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Create BambooTestResultConverterTest covering: (1) fromTestResultsData with a mixed result containing skipped tests — assert testIgnored lines present; (2) fromTestResultsData with TestResultsData(total=0, ...) — assert emptyList(); (3) toTeamCityMessages with a dto where failedTests is empty and successfulTests has entries — assert only testFinished lines, no testFailed. Also verify that the successfulTests=empty construction in fromTestResultsData (line 113) is intentional, since it discards passed test names.

confirmed.title: BambooServiceImpl.client lazy property: URL-change invalidation not tested — stale cached client risk
confirmed.description: The `client` property (lines 56-72) caches a `BambooApiClient` keyed by `cachedBaseUrl`. If `settings.connections.bambooUrl` changes mid-session (e.g., the user updates the settings page), `url != cachedBaseUrl` triggers a rebuild. No test exercises this state transition: all service tests inject `testClientOverride` and bypass the caching logic entirely. A bug in the invalidation path (e.g., a threading race on `@Volatile` without synchronization when the URL and client are written non-atomically) would go undetected.
confirmed.evidence:
```kotlin
if (url != cachedBaseUrl || cachedClient == null) {
    cachedBaseUrl = url
    ...
    cachedClient = BambooApiClient(...)
}
return cachedClient
```
— BambooServiceImpl.kt lines 61-70. No test covers the URL-change branch; all tests set `testClientOverride`.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a test (without `testClientOverride`) that sets `settings.connections.bambooUrl` to URL-A, calls `service.client`, then changes to URL-B, calls `service.client` again, and asserts the returned client targets URL-B (not URL-A). Since this requires controlling `PluginSettings`, use a mock `Project` that returns a controllable settings instance — already the pattern used in `BambooServiceImplStrategyCTest`. Assert that two different `BambooApiClient` instances are returned, not the same cached one.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed as a coverage gap. All tests that exercise BambooServiceImpl use testClientOverride, bypassing the client property entirely (BambooServiceImpl.kt lines 57-72). The URL-change invalidation branch (line 61: url != cachedBaseUrl) is never reached by any test. The @Volatile on cachedBaseUrl and cachedClient provides visibility guarantees but not atomicity — a thread reading between the cachedBaseUrl = url write (line 62) and the cachedClient = BambooApiClient(...) write (line 66) sees a stale client for one URL under a new URL. This is a real but low-probability race on the cache miss path. Severity is reassessed to Low because: (1) URL changes are rare user-driven events, not concurrent API calls; (2) a stale client for one missed call simply fails and retries on next poll. Adding the test requires a controllable PluginSettings, which is complex.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add a test that creates BambooServiceImpl without testClientOverride, uses a mock Project returning a controllable PluginSettings instance, calls service.client with URL-A, changes the bambooUrl to URL-B, calls service.client again, and asserts the second call returns a different BambooApiClient instance targeting URL-B. Alternatively, document the non-atomic write as accepted risk.

confirmed.title: BuildMonitorService.switchBranch(): stateFlow reset to null before re-poll not tested
confirmed.description: `switchBranch()` (lines 203-207) resets `_stateFlow.value = null` then calls `startPolling()`. This reset is important for the UI — the panel must clear the displayed build state before showing the new branch's build. No test verifies that `stateFlow.value` is null immediately after `switchBranch()` is called and before the poller fires the first result. `BuildMonitorFocusLifecycleTest` covers the polling lifecycle via the focus flow (which calls `startPolling`) but does NOT test the `switchBranch` path or the intermediate null state.
confirmed.evidence:
```kotlin
fun switchBranch(planKey: String, newBranch: String, intervalMs: Long = 30_000) {
    log.info("[Bamboo:Monitor] Switching branch to '$newBranch' for planKey=$planKey")
    _stateFlow.value = null
    startPolling(planKey, newBranch, intervalMs)
}
```
— BuildMonitorService.kt lines 203-207. No test in `BuildMonitorServiceTest` or `BuildMonitorFocusLifecycleTest` calls `switchBranch()`.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add to `BuildMonitorServiceTest`: call `pollOnce` to populate `stateFlow` with a non-null value, then call `switchBranch("PROJ-BUILD", "other-branch")`, and assert `service.stateFlow.value == null` immediately after (before the next poll). Use turbine to snapshot the flow: `stateFlow.test { ... awaitItem() shouldBe null ... }`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. No test in BuildMonitorServiceTest or BuildMonitorFocusLifecycleTest calls switchBranch(). BuildMonitorService.kt lines 203-207 resets _stateFlow.value = null then calls startPolling. The intermediate null state is load-bearing for the UI (panel must clear before showing new branch data) but is completely untested. BuildMonitorFocusLifecycleTest scenario 5 tests that stateFlow is null when startPolling is intercepted, but this does not cover the switchBranch path which explicitly sets null then starts polling. The fix is safe to add.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add a test to BuildMonitorServiceTest: call pollOnce to populate stateFlow, then call service.switchBranch('PROJ-BUILD', 'other-branch'), assert service.stateFlow.value == null immediately after (before the next poll completes). Use turbine to snapshot the intermediate null emission.

confirmed.title: PlanDetectionService.validate(): concurrent-call race on Mutex not tested — two concurrent validators for same key both make API calls
confirmed.description: `validate()` (lines 128-156) releases the `cacheMutex` around the HTTP call (intentionally, to avoid blocking) and re-acquires it to write the result. The comment says "Two concurrent validators racing on the same candidate may both call the API once, but the writes are idempotent". No test exercises concurrent validation of the same key. The `in-memory positive cache avoids repeated API calls within a session` test (line 550 in `PlanDetectionServiceTest`) calls `validate()` serially, not concurrently. A concurrent call could expose a bug if the mutex is incorrectly held across the network call.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a test that launches two coroutines simultaneously both calling `service.validate("PROJ-MAIN")` with the API client mock configured with a small delay. Use `coVerify(atMost = 2) { apiClient.validatePlan("PROJ-MAIN") }` to assert the API is called at most twice (not more, which would indicate the cache read is broken). Use `runTest` with two `launch { validate(...) }` blocks and `joinAll()`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed as a coverage gap. PlanDetectionServiceTest line 550 calls validate() serially twice and verifies coVerify(exactly = 1) that the API was called once (positive cache hit). No test exercises two concurrent coroutines both calling validate() for the same key simultaneously. The validate() implementation at PlanDetectionService.kt lines 128-156 intentionally drops cacheMutex around the HTTP call (lines 137-144) and re-acquires it only for the write (lines 147-155), as documented in the KDoc at lines 119-126. The design is correct: both concurrent callers make one API call each (at most 2 total), and writes are idempotent. The risk of an actual concurrency bug in the current implementation is very low given the documented intent and the two-phase lock design. This is a coverage gap for a documented concurrent design, not evidence of a bug.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add a test using runTest with two parallel launch blocks both calling service.validate('PROJ-MAIN') with a coEvery stub that introduces a small delay. After joinAll(), verify coVerify(atMost = 2) { apiClient.validatePlan('PROJ-MAIN') } and that both calls returned true. This pins the documented behavior without risking breakage.

confirmed.title: SonarApiClient.get() swallows SerializationException — silent refresh failure on malformed/empty body
confirmed.description: The `get<T>()` method catches only `IOException`. A `kotlinx.serialization.SerializationException` thrown by `json.decodeFromString<T>(bodyStr)` is not caught. This happens when: (a) the response body is null (`it.body?.string() ?: ""` produces `""`), and `""` is invalid JSON for any data class; or (b) the `Content-Type` header is absent/empty (bypassing the guard at lines 526-532) but the body is HTML from a proxy/WAF. The uncaught exception propagates through `paginateSonarItems` / the `async` block in `refreshWith`'s `coroutineScope {}`, which cancels all sibling HTTP calls and propagates to `cs.launch` in `refreshForBranch`. Because `cs` uses `SupervisorJob`, the launch silently fails — `_stateFlow.value` is never updated and the UI stays on loading state indefinitely with no error surfaced.
confirmed.suggestedFix: In `get<T>()`, broaden the catch to `catch (e: Exception)` (re-throwing `CancellationException`) or add a separate `catch (e: SerializationException)` block after the `IOException` handler, returning `ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR, "Invalid JSON from SonarQube: ${e.message}")`. Also remove the `?: ""` fallback for null body and instead return `ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR, "Empty response body")` directly.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at /sonar/src/main/kotlin/com/workflow/orchestrator/sonar/api/SonarApiClient.kt lines 534-562. The `get<T>()` method's only catch is `catch (e: IOException)` at line 559. Lines 534-535 produce `val bodyStr = it.body?.string() ?: ""` then `ApiResult.Success(json.decodeFromString<T>(bodyStr))`. An empty string from a null body, or HTML from a proxy/WAF when Content-Type is absent or blank (the guard at lines 526-532 only fires when Content-Type is non-blank AND not JSON), will throw `SerializationException` which is uncaught. It propagates out of the `async { client.getIssuesWithPaging(...) }` blocks inside `refreshWith`'s `coroutineScope {}` at SonarDataService.kt:302, cancelling all sibling HTTP children via structured concurrency, and the job launched by `cs.launch` at line 183 silently fails without updating `_stateFlow`. UI stays on loading state indefinitely. No test covers this path. Severity is High because it silently breaks the entire Quality tab on any proxy/WAF environment that returns HTML error pages.
confirmed.recommendedAction: In `get<T>()`, add a `catch (e: kotlinx.serialization.SerializationException)` block returning `ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR, ...)`, and replace the `?: ""` null-body fallback with an explicit `ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR, "Empty response body")` guard before the decode call.

confirmed.title: Race on unguarded refreshDebounceJob/activeRefreshJob plain vars accessed from EDT and background thread simultaneously
confirmed.description: `refreshDebounceJob` and `activeRefreshJob` are plain `var` with no `@Volatile` and no mutex. `refreshForBranch()` is called both from the platform-injected `cs` coroutine (default/background thread, via `subscribeToQualityScopeFlow`) and from EDT (via `QualityDashboardPanel`'s scope). A concurrent read-cancel-assign sequence can result in: (1) a freshly-created job being cancelled by the other thread's stale `activeRefreshJob?.cancel()` read, allowing two `refreshWith` invocations to run concurrently; or (2) the cancel of an in-flight job being missed because the other thread saw the old value. Two concurrent `refreshWith` calls then race on `previousGateStatus` (also a plain unguarded `var` at line 47) and can double-fire `QualityGateResult` events or fire them with wrong transition logic.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Mark `refreshDebounceJob` and `activeRefreshJob` as `@Volatile`, or better: make `refreshForBranch` a suspend function and protect the job read-cancel-assign block under the existing `apiClientMutex` (it already guards the analogous check-then-act on `cachedApiClient`). Alternatively, confine all calls to `refreshForBranch` to a single dispatcher by making it `private` and exposing only a suspend wrapper. Also annotate `previousGateStatus` as `@Volatile` or move it inside the mutex.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at SonarDataService.kt lines 60, 64 (plain `var` fields, no `@Volatile`) and line 176 (`refreshForBranch` is a plain fun with no synchronization). `refreshForBranch` is called from the EDT by QualityDashboardPanel (scope at QualityDashboardPanel.kt:53 uses `Dispatchers.EDT`) at lines 306, 404, 657, AND from a background coroutine via `subscribeToQualityScopeFlow`'s `cs.launch` block at line 145. The read-cancel-assign sequence on `refreshDebounceJob` and `activeRefreshJob` is unguarded against concurrent access from these two different threads. However, `previousGateStatus` (line 47) is only ever read/written inside `refreshWith`, which runs exclusively on the background `cs` scope, so the double-fire claim for `previousGateStatus` is weaker — but the job variable race is real and can allow two `refreshWith` invocations to run concurrently, producing duplicate or out-of-order `QualityGateResult` events. Severity is Medium (not Critical) because the EDT and background thread races on job assignment cause double-refresh at worst, not data loss.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Mark `refreshDebounceJob` and `activeRefreshJob` as `@Volatile`, or confine all `refreshForBranch` calls to a single dispatcher. Annotating `previousGateStatus` as `@Volatile` is also recommended for visibility safety.

confirmed.title: getLineCoverage fallback branch hardcoded to 'develop' returns wrong coverage for projects not on develop
confirmed.description: When the caller passes `branch = null` (e.g. the owning git repository could not be resolved), `getLineCoverage` silently falls back to `"develop"`. Most projects use `main` or `master` as the default branch. Querying Sonar for branch `develop` on a project whose default branch is `main` returns 404 or empty coverage — all editor gutter markers go dark. The caller `CoverageLineMarkerProvider` sets `branch` from `resolver.findRepositoryForPath(...)?.currentBranchName` which can return null if the file is not in a git repo. There is no log warning that the fallback is being used.
confirmed.evidence: val effectiveBranch = branch?.takeIf { it.isNotBlank() } ?: "develop"
confirmed.suggestedFix: Remove the `?: "develop"` fallback. When `branch` is null, return `emptyMap()` early with a debug log (the same approach as `effectiveKey.isBlank()` on line 234). The gutter marker provider already handles `null` lineCoverage gracefully. Alternatively, fallback to the `branches` list in the current `SonarState` to find the main branch name dynamically.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at SonarDataService.kt line 244: `val effectiveBranch = branch?.takeIf { it.isNotBlank() } ?: "develop"`. When `branch` is null (e.g. the file's owning git repository could not be resolved by the caller), Sonar is queried for branch `develop`. For projects whose default branch is `main` or `master`, this returns 404 or empty coverage — all editor gutter markers go dark silently. The KDoc at lines 220-225 documents this fallback as intentional for single-repo projects where the repo couldn't be resolved, but the comment also says 'Falls back to `"develop"` so the Sonar query still issues', which means the intention is to make a best-effort query. However, querying the wrong branch returns incorrect (not missing) coverage, which is actively misleading. The fix (return emptyMap() with a debug log) aligns with the existing `effectiveKey.isBlank()` guard pattern at line 234.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Replace `?: "develop"` with an early `return emptyMap()` guarded by a `log.debug("[Sonar:LineCoverage] Cannot determine branch for '$relativePath' — skipping coverage fetch")`. The gutter marker provider already handles null line coverage gracefully.

confirmed.title: SonarCoverageAnnotator uses project.basePath instead of per-file repo root — coverage lookup always misses in multi-repo projects
confirmed.description: `getFileCoverageInformationString` calls `SonarPathResolver.computeRelativePath(filePath, vcsRootPath = project.basePath, projectBasePath = project.basePath)`. The `vcsRootPath` and `projectBasePath` are the same value (the IDE project/aggregator root), so `computeRelativePath` computes a project-root-relative path. But `fileCoverage` in `SonarState` is keyed by Sonar's REPO-relative path (computed by `CoverageMapper.mapMeasures` using Sonar's `comp.path` field, which is relative to the individual repo root). In multi-repo setups where secondary repos are not at the project root, the computed path includes the extra directory prefix and the map lookup always returns null. The `CoverageLineMarkerProvider` and `SonarIssueAnnotator` correctly use `RepoContextResolver.resolveFromFile` to get the repo root; this class does not.
confirmed.evidence:
val relativePath = SonarPathResolver.computeRelativePath(
    filePath = vFile.path,
    vcsRootPath = project.basePath,
    projectBasePath = project.basePath,
)
val fileCoverage = sonarService.stateFlow.value.fileCoverage[relativePath]
confirmed.suggestedFix: Replace the `computeRelativePath(vcsRootPath = project.basePath, ...)` call with `SonarPathResolver.resolveContext(project, vFile)` (which uses `RepoContextResolver.resolveFromFile`) to get both the correct `relativePath` and the `sonarProjectKey`. Then look up `sonarService.stateFlow.value.fileCoverage[context.relativePath]` instead.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at SonarCoverageAnnotator.kt lines 43-47. `computeRelativePath` is called with `vcsRootPath = project.basePath` and `projectBasePath = project.basePath` — both the same value (the IDE project root). In `SonarPathResolver.computeRelativePath`, `vcsRootPath` is tried first as a prefix strip. For a file at `/project/repo_2/src/App.kt` with `project.basePath = /project`, the result is `repo_2/src/App.kt`. But `CoverageMapper.mapMeasures` keys `fileCoverage` by the repo-relative path `src/App.kt` (using `comp.path` from the Sonar API, which is relative to the individual repo root). The lookup at line 48 always returns null for secondary repos, silently disabling the Coverage tool-window column for those repos. `CoverageLineMarkerProvider` and `SonarIssueAnnotator` both correctly use `SonarPathResolver.resolveContext(project, vFile)` which calls `RepoContextResolver.resolveFromFile`; this annotator does not.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Replace the `computeRelativePath(vcsRootPath = project.basePath, ...)` call with `SonarPathResolver.resolveContext(project, vFile)` to get the correct repo-relative path, then look up `sonarService.stateFlow.value.fileCoverage[context.relativePath]`.

confirmed.title: buildBranchQualityReportSummary truncation message hardcodes maxFilesDefault constant instead of the actual maxFiles parameter
confirmed.description: `buildBranchQualityReportSummary` appends `", showing top $maxFilesDefault"` when `data.truncatedFiles` is true. But `maxFilesDefault` is the companion-object constant (20), not the `maxFiles` parameter actually passed to `getBranchQualityReport`. If a caller passes `maxFiles = 5` or `maxFiles = 50`, the summary message reports the wrong limit. The agent's LLM prompt will be told the wrong number of files were shown.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Pass the actual `maxFiles` parameter into `buildBranchQualityReportSummary` as an argument, or capture it in the closure. Change the function signature to `private fun buildBranchQualityReportSummary(data: BranchQualityReportData, projectKey: String, maxFiles: Int): String` and replace `$maxFilesDefault` with `$maxFiles` at line 1204.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at SonarServiceImpl.kt line 1204: `append(", showing top $maxFilesDefault")` uses the companion-object constant `maxFilesDefault = 20` (line 1476) instead of the actual `maxFiles` parameter passed to `getBranchQualityReport` (line 866). The call to `buildBranchQualityReportSummary(data, projectKey)` at line 1051 does not pass `maxFiles`. If a caller passes `maxFiles = 5` or `maxFiles = 50`, the summary message will report '20' regardless. The agent's LLM prompt receives the wrong count of files shown. However, in practice `maxFilesDefault` is the only value currently passed at line 866's default parameter and the agent tool always uses the default, so the incorrect message will only surface if `maxFiles` is ever overridden. Severity is Low (LLM prompt misinformation, not user-facing crash).
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add `maxFiles: Int` parameter to `buildBranchQualityReportSummary` and pass it through from the call site at line 1051. Change `$maxFilesDefault` to `$maxFiles` at line 1204.

confirmed.title: getLineCoverage check-then-act on ConcurrentHashMap allows duplicate in-flight HTTP requests for the same file
confirmed.description: `getLineCoverage` checks the cache with `lineCoverageCache[key]?.let { return it }` and then issues an HTTP request if the key is absent.
