Two concurrent callers (e.g. `IssueDetailPanel.buildCodeSnippet` and `CoveragePreviewPanel.showFile`) both calling for the same file can both see a cache miss at the same instant and each fire a separate `getSourceLines` HTTP request. The `CoverageLineMarkerProvider` avoids this with a `putIfAbsent` pending-fetches guard, but `getLineCoverage` itself has no such guard. The duplicate request is wasteful and — under high concurrency — can race between `getLineCoverage` returning `emptyMap()` (error path) from one call while the other succeeds and caches the data.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a `ConcurrentHashMap<String, Deferred<Map<Int, LineCoverageStatus>>>` in-flight registry inside `SonarDataService`. In `getLineCoverage`, use `computeIfAbsent` to register a `Deferred` for the key before issuing the HTTP request, and `await()` it if already in-flight. Alternatively, use a per-key `Mutex` map. This is the standard single-flight pattern already applied at the `CoverageLineMarkerProvider` level for the gutter-marker path.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at SonarDataService.kt lines 238-251. The cache check at line 238 (`lineCoverageCache[key]?.let { return it }`) is a plain read from `ConcurrentHashMap` with no in-flight deduplication. Two concurrent callers that both see a cache miss at the same instant will each issue a separate `getSourceLines` HTTP call for the same component key. There is no `Deferred` registry or per-key Mutex to deduplicate. The worst case is duplicate HTTP traffic and a transient TOCTOU where one call returns `emptyMap()` on error while the other succeeds. `CoverageLineMarkerProvider` does guard this at the gutter-marker level (as noted in the finding), but `getLineCoverage` itself does not. Severity is Low because duplicate requests are wasteful but not incorrect — the last writer to `lineCoverageCache[key]` wins with valid data.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add a `ConcurrentHashMap<String, Deferred<Map<Int, LineCoverageStatus>>>` in-flight registry in `SonarDataService` and use `computeIfAbsent` + `await()` single-flight pattern in `getLineCoverage`.

confirmed.title: IssueListPanel declares a CoroutineScope that is never launched
confirmed.description: Line 45 creates `private val scope = CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.IO)`. The field is cancelled in `dispose()` at line 318, but there is no `scope.launch { }` call anywhere in the file. The scope object leaks a SupervisorJob + thread until dispose fires, yet does zero work. The class is a pure UI panel with no async work of its own.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete the `scope` field and the `scope.cancel()` call in `dispose()`. All async operations (rule fetches, navigation) are done by the injected `IssueDetailPanel` which owns its own scope, or by the platform.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed via bash grep: `scope` is declared at IssueListPanel.kt line 45 (`CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.IO)`), cancelled at line 318 in `dispose()`, and the grep for `scope.` in the file returns only line 318. There is no `scope.launch {}` anywhere in IssueListPanel.kt. The scope object allocates a SupervisorJob and holds a reference to Dispatchers.IO until dispose fires, performing no work. This is a real dead code / minor leak.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Delete the `scope` field declaration at line 45 and the `scope.cancel()` call at line 318 in `dispose()`.

confirmed.title: refreshDebounceJob is a redundant alias for activeRefreshJob — both track the same Job
confirmed.description: Lines 60 and 64 declare two separate `Job?` fields. In `refreshForBranch()` (lines 181–189) both are cancelled and then both are assigned the exact same `job` reference from `cs.launch { }`. Cancelling one already-cancelled Job a second time is a no-op. Both fields are always identical — one is entirely redundant.
confirmed.evidence:
181:         refreshDebounceJob?.cancel()
182:         activeRefreshJob?.cancel()
...
188:         refreshDebounceJob = job
189:         activeRefreshJob = job
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete `refreshDebounceJob` (lines 60 and 181, 188). Keep only `activeRefreshJob` which was introduced by F-10 and captures both the debounce delay and in-flight HTTP children. Rename `activeRefreshJob` back to `refreshJob` if clearer.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed at SonarDataService.kt lines 60, 64, 181-182, 188-189. Both `refreshDebounceJob` and `activeRefreshJob` are cancelled together at lines 181-182, then both assigned the same `job` reference from `cs.launch {}` at lines 188-189. They are always identical — one is entirely redundant. The source-pin test in SonarDataServiceConcurrencyTest.kt at line 162 explicitly checks for `activeRefreshJob` by name, not `refreshDebounceJob`, so removing `refreshDebounceJob` while keeping `activeRefreshJob` would not break any tests. The fix (remove `refreshDebounceJob`) also partially addresses SONAR-COR-2 by reducing the number of unguarded plain `var` fields.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Delete `refreshDebounceJob` (field declaration at line 60 and references at lines 181, 188). Keep only `activeRefreshJob`.

confirmed.title: applyPreFilter's newCodeMode parameter is accepted but never read
confirmed.description: The public method `applyPreFilter(type: IssueType?, newCodeMode: Boolean?)` (line 180) accepts `newCodeMode` but never references it in its body (lines 181–190 — only `typeIndex` and `filterCombo.selectedIndex` are set). The caller at `QualityDashboardPanel.kt:227` passes `filter.newCodeMode`, but the value is silently discarded.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Either (a) remove the `newCodeMode` parameter and update the caller (simplest), or (b) implement the intent: after setting `typeIndex`, if `newCodeMode == true` call `dataService.setNewCodeMode(true)` so the filter and the toggle are in sync. Option (a) is safer unless the toggle sync was the design intent.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. Line 180 of IssueListPanel.kt declares `applyPreFilter(type: IssueType?, newCodeMode: Boolean?)` but lines 181–190 never reference `newCodeMode`. The caller at QualityDashboardPanel.kt:227 sets `dataService.setNewCodeMode(true)` on line 222 before calling `issueListPanel.applyPreFilter(it, filter.newCodeMode)` — so the mode toggle IS applied to the data service, but the parameter to the panel method is entirely unused/dead. This means the newCodeMode intent is partially served (the data service is updated) but the parameter itself is redundant dead weight. Not harmful to fix: removing the parameter (option a) or keeping it as a no-op is safe since the caller already handles newCodeMode separately. Severity is Low because the behavior is correct despite the dead parameter.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Remove the `newCodeMode: Boolean?` parameter from `applyPreFilter` and update the caller at QualityDashboardPanel.kt:227 to drop the argument. The data service toggle on line 222 already handles the newCodeMode concern.

confirmed.title: paging.copy(total = firstResult.paging.total) in combineResult lambdas is a no-op
confirmed.description: In both `getIssuesWithPaging` (line 190) and `getSecurityHotspots` (line 473), the `combineResult` lambda calls `firstResult.paging.copy(total = firstResult.paging.total)` — copying the paging object and assigning its own `total` back to itself. This is identical to `firstResult.paging` and creates a needless allocation.
confirmed.evidence:
190:                 firstResult.copy(issues = allItems, paging = firstResult.paging.copy(total = firstResult.paging.total))
473:                 firstResult.copy(hotspots = allItems, paging = firstResult.paging.copy(total = firstResult.paging.total))
confirmed.suggestedFix: Simplify both lambdas: `combineResult = { firstResult, allItems -> firstResult.copy(issues = allItems) }` and `combineResult = { firstResult, allItems -> firstResult.copy(hotspots = allItems) }`. The `paging` field of `firstResult` is already the server-authoritative first-page envelope — no copy needed.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. Line 190 of SonarApiClient.kt: `firstResult.paging.copy(total = firstResult.paging.total)` copies the paging object and sets `total` to exactly its current value — a self-assignment that allocates a redundant copy. Same pattern at line 473 for hotspots. The paging information from `firstResult` is already the authoritative first-page envelope. Simplifying to `firstResult.copy(issues = allItems)` (omitting the redundant `paging =` field) is correct and safe. Not harmful to fix: the paging field retains the same value since it's taken directly from `firstResult.paging` unchanged.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Replace both `combineResult` lambdas with the simplified form omitting the paging copy: `{ firstResult, allItems -> firstResult.copy(issues = allItems) }` and `{ firstResult, allItems -> firstResult.copy(hotspots = allItems) }`.

confirmed.title: SonarCoverageAnnotator passes project.basePath as both vcsRootPath and projectBasePath, making the fallback arm dead
confirmed.description: Lines 43–47 call `SonarPathResolver.computeRelativePath(filePath = vFile.path, vcsRootPath = project.basePath, projectBasePath = project.basePath)`. Because `vcsRootPath` and `projectBasePath` are the same value, the `projectBasePath` fallback arm inside `computeRelativePath` is structurally dead — if the first strip attempt fails, the second will also fail identically. Additionally this ignores the actual VCS root for multi-repo projects; `SonarPathResolver.resolveContext(project, vFile)` is the canonical path that already uses the correct repo-relative root.
confirmed.evidence:
43:         val relativePath = SonarPathResolver.computeRelativePath(
44:             filePath = vFile.path,
45:             vcsRootPath = project.basePath,
46:             projectBasePath = project.basePath,
47:         )
confirmed.suggestedFix:
Replace lines 34–48 with:
```kotlin
val vFile = psiFile.virtualFile ?: return null
val sonarService = try { SonarDataService.getInstance(project) } catch (_: Exception) { return null }
val pathCtx = SonarPathResolver.resolveContext(project, vFile)
val fileCoverage = sonarService.stateFlow.value.fileCoverage[pathCtx.relativePath] ?: return null
```
This matches how `SonarIssueAnnotator` resolves paths and correctly handles multi-repo projects.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. Lines 43–47 of SonarCoverageAnnotator.kt pass `vcsRootPath = project.basePath` AND `projectBasePath = project.basePath`. In `SonarPathResolver.computeRelativePath` (SonarPathResolver.kt lines 37–38), the fallback arm only executes when the first strip fails — but since both arguments are identical, the second arm can never succeed where the first failed. This makes the fallback structurally dead. More critically, the canonical path resolver `SonarPathResolver.resolveContext(project, vFile)` already handles multi-repo by calling `RepoContextResolver.getInstance(project).resolveFromFile(virtualFile)` (line 18) which returns the correct per-repo VCS root, while this call site ignores that and passes the aggregator basePath twice. Not harmful to fix: the replacement uses the same utility already used by `SonarIssueAnnotator`.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Replace the `computeRelativePath` call with `SonarPathResolver.resolveContext(project, vFile)` and use `pathCtx.relativePath` for the map lookup, matching the pattern used by SonarIssueAnnotator. This correctly handles multi-repo projects.

confirmed.title: getMeasures has an unnecessary ifBlank fallback on metricKeys — no caller ever passes blank
confirmed.description: Line 306: `val metrics = metricKeys.ifBlank { DEFAULT_METRIC_KEYS }`. The parameter already has a default value of `DEFAULT_METRIC_KEYS` (line 303). Every production caller either omits `metricKeys` (getting the default) or passes a non-blank explicit key set. There is no caller that passes an empty or blank string, so the `ifBlank` guard masks mistakes silently instead of failing fast.
confirmed.evidence:
300: suspend fun getMeasures(
303:         metricKeys: String = DEFAULT_METRIC_KEYS
...
306:         val metrics = metricKeys.ifBlank { DEFAULT_METRIC_KEYS }
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete line 306 and replace every subsequent reference to `metrics` with `metricKeys`. If guarding against accidental blank is desired, use `require(metricKeys.isNotBlank()) { "metricKeys must not be blank" }` to fail loudly rather than silently falling back.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. SonarApiClient.kt line 303 declares `metricKeys: String = DEFAULT_METRIC_KEYS` (a non-blank default), and line 306 has `val metrics = metricKeys.ifBlank { DEFAULT_METRIC_KEYS }`. No production caller passes a blank string — callers either omit the argument (getting the default) or pass an explicit non-blank key set. The `ifBlank` guard is redundant dead code that silently masks accidental blank rather than failing loudly. Not harmful to fix: removing line 306 and using `metricKeys` directly is safe, as every real call path already provides a non-blank value via the default.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Delete line 306 and substitute `metricKeys` everywhere `metrics` was referenced. Optionally add `require(metricKeys.isNotBlank())` to make accidental blank-passing fail loudly at the call site.

confirmed.title: CoverageTablePanel.navigateToFile uses project.basePath, breaking double-click navigation in multi-repo setups
confirmed.description: Lines 293–297: `navigateToFile` resolves the file using `project.basePath`. Unlike `CoveragePreviewPanel.showFile` (which correctly reads `owningRepo.localVcsRootPath` from `coverageData.projectKey`) and `IssueDetailPanel.resolveSonarRepoRoot`, this method hardcodes the aggregator basePath. On multi-repo projects a file in a secondary repo will never be found — `LocalFileSystem.findFileByPath` returns null and the double-click silently does nothing.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Use the selected row's `FileCoverageData.projectKey` to resolve the owning repo root (same pattern as `CoveragePreviewPanel.showFile` lines 95–99). Thread `FileCoverageData` into the call site: `tableModel.getFileCoverageData(modelRow)?.let { coverageData -> navigateToFile(filePath, coverageData) }`, then resolve `basePath` via `PluginSettings.getRepos().firstOrNull { it.sonarProjectKey == coverageData.projectKey }?.localVcsRootPath ?: project.basePath`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real and is the same root cause as SONAR-ARC-2 (they are duplicate findings about the same code). CoverageTablePanel.kt lines 293–296: `navigateToFile(filePath: String)` uses `project.basePath` directly. The single-click handler at line 155 already calls `tableModel.getFileCoverageData(modelRow)` and passes it to `previewPanel.showFile(filePath, coverageData)` (which correctly resolves per-repo root at line 96). However, the double-click path at line 142 only passes `filePath` to `navigateToFile`, discarding the `FileCoverageData` and its `projectKey`. On a multi-repo project, files in secondary repos will not be found and navigation silently no-ops. Not harmful to fix.
confirmed.recommendedAction: In the double-click handler (line 140–143), also fetch `tableModel.getFileCoverageData(modelRow)` and thread it into `navigateToFile`. Update `navigateToFile` to resolve the repo root via `PluginSettings.getRepos().firstOrNull { it.sonarProjectKey == coverageData?.projectKey }?.localVcsRootPath ?: project.basePath`, matching the pattern in CoveragePreviewPanel.kt lines 95–99.

confirmed.title: Dispatchers.Main used for Swing UI mutations instead of Dispatchers.EDT
confirmed.description: IssueDetailPanel (lines 296, 374, 378, 383) and CoveragePreviewPanel (lines 115, 131) switch to Dispatchers.Main for all Swing UI mutations (codeArea.text, ruleInfoLabel.text). The project convention is Dispatchers.EDT for all UI mutations in IntelliJ plugins. While kotlinx-coroutines-swing maps Dispatchers.Main to the EDT, the codebase explicitly imports and uses com.intellij.openapi.application.EDT everywhere else (e.g., QualityDashboardPanel line 25/53, SonarDataService). This inconsistency violates the stated threading convention and will silently break if the project ever excludes the swing dispatcher artifact from the classpath.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Replace all `withContext(Dispatchers.Main)` in IssueDetailPanel.kt (lines 296, 374, 378, 383) and CoveragePreviewPanel.kt (lines 115, 131) with `withContext(Dispatchers.EDT)`, adding `import com.intellij.openapi.application.EDT` to each file. This matches the explicit convention used by QualityDashboardPanel and the rest of the codebase.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real as a convention inconsistency. IssueDetailPanel.kt uses `withContext(Dispatchers.Main)` at lines 296, 374, 378, 383, and CoveragePreviewPanel.kt uses it at lines 115, 131. Neither file imports `com.intellij.openapi.application.EDT`. Meanwhile QualityDashboardPanel.kt line 25 imports `com.intellij.openapi.application.EDT` and line 53 creates its scope with `Dispatchers.EDT`. In practice `kotlinx-coroutines-swing` maps `Dispatchers.Main` to the Swing EDT, so there is no current functional bug — Swing mutations do reach the EDT. However, the severity should be reassessed downward: this is an inconsistency/style issue rather than a real behavioral bug, because the `kotlinx-coroutines-swing` artifact is a standard dependency of IntelliJ's coroutine environment. The finding's concern about removing the artifact is hypothetical. Not harmful to fix: replacing `Dispatchers.Main` with `Dispatchers.EDT` is a safe mechanical change.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Replace `withContext(Dispatchers.Main)` with `withContext(Dispatchers.EDT)` in IssueDetailPanel.kt (lines 296, 374, 378, 383) and CoveragePreviewPanel.kt (lines 115, 131), adding `import com.intellij.openapi.application.EDT` to each file. This aligns with the project convention.

confirmed.title: CoverageTablePanel.navigateToFile uses project.basePath instead of per-repo root (multi-repo breakage)
confirmed.description: CoverageTablePanel.navigateToFile (line 293-296) navigates to a file using project.basePath as the root. The FileCoverageData model carries a projectKey field (line 86 of SonarModels.kt) specifically so multi-repo callers can resolve the owning repo's localVcsRootPath, as fixed in IssueDetailPanel (F-12/F-13). However, the double-click path in CoverageTablePanel (lines 140-142) only passes filePath to navigateToFile, discarding the FileCoverageData.projectKey available at line 155. On a multi-repo project, double-clicking a coverage row for a file in repo_2 will look it up under the aggregator project.basePath and silently fail to navigate (vf is null) or navigate to the wrong file.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Change the double-click handler to fetch FileCoverageData alongside the filePath: `val coverageData = tableModel.getFileCoverageData(modelRow); navigateToFile(filePath, coverageData?.projectKey)`. Update `navigateToFile` to accept a nullable projectKey and use `IssueDetailPanel.resolveRepoRoot(...)` (the same companion method used by IssueListPanel and IssueDetailPanel) to resolve the owning repo's localVcsRootPath before constructing the path. This aligns with the F-12/F-13 fix already applied to the sibling panels.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real and is a duplicate of SONAR-CLE-7 identifying the same bug in CoverageTablePanel.kt lines 135–142 (double-click) vs lines 293–296 (navigateToFile). The double-click handler discards `FileCoverageData` (which contains `projectKey`) and calls `navigateToFile(filePath)` using `project.basePath`. The single-click handler at lines 149–161 already has `val coverageData = tableModel.getFileCoverageData(modelRow)` at line 155. This asymmetry means single-click preview works correctly in multi-repo (via `previewPanel.showFile` which resolves `owningRepo.localVcsRootPath` at line 96 of CoveragePreviewPanel), but double-click navigation silently fails for secondary repos. The F-12/F-13 fixes in IssueDetailPanel were not propagated here. Not harmful to fix.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Same as SONAR-CLE-7: fetch `FileCoverageData` in the double-click handler and pass `projectKey` to `navigateToFile` for repo-correct path resolution. The two findings (SONAR-CLE-7 and SONAR-ARC-2) should be addressed as a single fix.

confirmed.title: SonarServiceImpl.getInstance performs an unchecked downcast from the SonarService interface
confirmed.description: SonarServiceImpl.getInstance (lines 1480-1481) resolves the service by interface (project.getService(SonarService::class.java)) and then does an unchecked `as SonarServiceImpl` cast. If a test or future code registers an alternative SonarService implementation (e.g., a stub or a mock registered in plugin.xml), this cast will throw ClassCastException at runtime. The method is not called from anywhere outside the sonar module currently, but its public @JvmStatic visibility makes it an implicit contract. The service architecture convention is that callers get the interface, not the concrete class.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Either (a) change the return type to `SonarService` (the interface) and remove the cast, since no currently reachable caller needs anything beyond the interface methods; or (b) register `SonarServiceImpl` directly as the plugin.xml service key and resolve with `project.getService(SonarServiceImpl::class.java)` to make the concrete type explicit and avoid the cast. The second option matches the SonarDataService pattern (direct class registration and `getService(SonarDataService::class.java)`).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. plugin.xml lines 190–191 register the service with `serviceInterface=SonarService` and `serviceImplementation=SonarServiceImpl`. SonarServiceImpl.getInstance at line 1481 does `project.getService(SonarService::class.java) as SonarServiceImpl` — an unchecked downcast. However, importantly, no caller of `SonarServiceImpl.getInstance` was found anywhere in the codebase (grep returned empty). The cast is currently safe because plugin.xml always resolves `SonarService` to `SonarServiceImpl`. But the `@JvmStatic public` method is an implicit contract that could be called by tests or future code that registers a mock `SonarService` implementation, at which point the cast throws `ClassCastException`. The architectural pattern used by `SonarDataService` (direct class registration, resolved by concrete class) is cleaner. Not harmful to fix: either returning `SonarService` interface or registering by concrete class would be safe, and there are no current callers to update.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Change the return type to `SonarService` and remove the `as SonarServiceImpl` cast. Alternatively, register `SonarServiceImpl` directly in plugin.xml (without `serviceInterface`) and resolve via `project.getService(SonarServiceImpl::class.java)`, matching SonarDataService's pattern.

confirmed.title: IssueListPanel allocates an IO CoroutineScope that is never used
confirmed.description: IssueListPanel declares `private val scope = CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.IO)` at line 45, cancelled in dispose() at line 318. However, a codebase search finds no `scope.launch` or `scope.async` calls within IssueListPanel — the scope is created and cancelled but no work is ever launched on it. This is a dead resource allocation that creates a SupervisorJob and thread-pool overhead for no benefit.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Remove the `scope` field and its `scope.cancel()` in `dispose()`. If future async work is added to this panel, the scope should be reintroduced at that point. Note: IssueDetailPanel (which is registered as a child Disposer and owns its own IO scope) handles all async fetches for the selected issue — IssueListPanel itself does not need a scope.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. IssueListPanel.kt line 45 declares `private val scope = CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.IO)` and line 318 cancels it in `dispose()`. A grep of the entire file confirms the only occurrence of `scope.` is `scope.cancel()` — there is no `scope.launch` or `scope.async` anywhere in `IssueListPanel`. By contrast, `IssueDetailPanel` (the child panel that handles all async fetches) correctly uses `scope.launch` at lines 294 and 367. The IssueListPanel scope is purely dead overhead. Removing it and its `dispose()` cancel call is safe and correct; it removes no load-bearing work.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Remove `private val scope = CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.IO)` at line 45 and `scope.cancel()` at line 318 in IssueListPanel.kt. No functional change.

confirmed.title: Non-JB Swing components used for interactive controls (JButton, JCheckBox, JComboBox, JTextArea, JMenuItem/JPopupMenu) in UI panels
confirmed.description: Several sonar UI files use plain Swing interactive components instead of their JetBrains equivalents: QualityDashboardPanel uses javax.swing.JButton (lines 136, 141) and javax.swing.JCheckBox (line 152); IssueDetailPanel uses javax.swing.JButton (lines 80-83) and javax.swing.JTextArea (line 67); IssueListPanel uses javax.swing.JComboBox (lines 39-40), javax.swing.JMenuItem, and javax.swing.JPopupMenu (lines 152-166); CoveragePreviewPanel uses javax.swing.JButton (line 46) and javax.swing.JTextArea (line 35). The project convention mandates JetBrains UI components only. Plain Swing components do not participate in IntelliJ's theme engine, causing visual inconsistencies (incorrect borders, focus rings, and colors) in both light and dark themes.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Replace: JButton → com.intellij.ui.components.JBButton (or ActionButton for toolbar-style buttons); JCheckBox → com.intellij.ui.components.JBCheckBox; JComboBox → com.intellij.openapi.ui.ComboBox (already imported/used in QualityDashboardPanel for the repo selector); JTextArea → com.intellij.ui.components.JBTextArea; JMenuItem/JPopupMenu are acceptable as there are no JB equivalents for these. The JTextArea instances in IssueDetailPanel and CoveragePreviewPanel would additionally benefit from being wrapped in a read-only EditorTextField for syntax highlighting support.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. Direct reads of the files show: QualityDashboardPanel.kt imports `javax.swing.JButton` (line 38) and `javax.swing.JCheckBox` (line 39), using them at lines 136, 141, and 152. IssueListPanel.kt imports `javax.swing.JComboBox` (line 39-40), `JPopupMenu` (line 152), `JMenuItem` (lines 156, 159, 165). IssueDetailPanel.kt uses `JTextArea` (line 67) and `JButton` (lines 80-83). CoveragePreviewPanel.kt uses `JTextArea` (line 35) and `JButton` (line 46). These violate the project convention of JetBrains-only UI components. Replacing with JBButton, JBCheckBox, ComboBox, JBTextArea is safe (JMenuItem/JPopupMenu are correctly exempted — no JB equivalents exist). The fix introduces no risk to functional behavior.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Replace plain Swing interactive components with JB equivalents: JButton→JBButton (or AnActionButton in toolbar context), JCheckBox→JBCheckBox, JComboBox→ComboBox (already imported in QualityDashboardPanel), JTextArea→JBTextArea. JMenuItem and JPopupMenu are acceptable as-is.

confirmed.title: SonarServiceImpl.getBranchQualityReport makes a sequential API call outside the parallel coroutineScope block (extra latency)
confirmed.description: getBranchQualityReport (lines 863-1053) fires four parallel requests inside a coroutineScope block (gate, issues, hotspots, fileMeasures). After that block completes, it makes a fifth sequential call to api.getProjectMeasures(projectKey, branch, projectNewCodeMetrics) at line 1003, outside any parallel structure. This extra round trip adds 100-500ms of serial latency after the parallel phase has already finished, while the data it fetches (project-level new-code coverage summary) is independent of the parallel results and could be included in the first parallel wave.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Move the `api.getProjectMeasures(...)` call inside the first `coroutineScope` block as a fifth `async { ... }` deferred alongside the existing four. Declare `val projectMeasuresDeferred = async { api.getProjectMeasures(projectKey, branch, projectNewCodeMetrics) }` inside the block, and replace the sequential call at line 1003 with `val projectMeasures = projectMeasuresDeferred.await()` after the existing `awaitAll` group. This eliminates the serial latency at the cost of no additional code complexity.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. In SonarServiceImpl.kt, the first `coroutineScope` block closes at line 905 (after awaiting gate, issues, hotspots, fileMeasures in parallel). The call `api.getProjectMeasures(projectKey, branch, projectNewCodeMetrics)` at line 1003 is entirely outside that block and executes sequentially. This call fetches project-level new-code coverage summary, which is independent of all four parallel results and could be launched as a fifth `async` inside the first wave. Moving it inside the parallel block eliminates one full serial HTTP round trip. The fix is safe: the result is not needed until after the coroutineScope block, and parallelizing an independent fetch has no side effects.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add `val projectMeasuresDeferred = async { api.getProjectMeasures(projectKey, branch, projectNewCodeMetrics) }` inside the first `coroutineScope` block alongside the other four deferred values, and replace the line-1003 sequential call with `val projectMeasures = projectMeasuresDeferred.await()` after the block completes.

confirmed.title: SonarApiClient: 429 rate-limit and HTML Content-Type PARSE_ERROR paths are untested
confirmed.description: The `get()` private method in SonarApiClient handles HTTP 429 (→ ErrorType.RATE_LIMITED) and a non-JSON Content-Type (→ ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR). No test in SonarApiClientTest exercises either branch. A SonarQube instance behind a reverse proxy commonly returns 429 or an HTML error page (e.g. login redirect) on token expiry; without coverage these two error paths are dark.
confirmed.suggestedFix:
Add two tests to SonarApiClientTest using MockWebServer:
1. `server.enqueue(MockResponse().setResponseCode(429))` → assert `result.isError && (result as ApiResult.Error).type == ErrorType.RATE_LIMITED`.
2. `server.enqueue(MockResponse().setBody("<html>Login</html>").addHeader("Content-Type", "text/html"))` → assert `result.isError && (result as ApiResult.Error).type == ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR`.
Both tests should call any endpoint, e.g. `client.validateConnection()`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. SonarApiClient.kt lines 549-551 handle HTTP 429 → ErrorType.RATE_LIMITED, and lines 526-533 detect non-JSON Content-Type → ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR. A full read of SonarApiClientTest.kt confirms no test exercises either branch. The existing test suite covers 401, 403 (componentExists), 404, and 503 (mid-pagination), but not 429 and not HTML content-type. The claimed severity of High is slightly overstated — these are real-world paths (rate limiting and HTML login-redirect pages from reverse proxies) but not crash-causing. Medium is more appropriate. Adding MockWebServer tests for both is safe and non-breaking.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add two tests to SonarApiClientTest: (1) enqueue a 429 response → assert ApiResult.Error with ErrorType.RATE_LIMITED; (2) enqueue a 200 with Content-Type: text/html → assert ApiResult.Error with ErrorType.PARSE_ERROR.

confirmed.title: SonarApiClient: network IOException (offline) path is untested at the HTTP layer
confirmed.description: The `get()` method catches `IOException` and maps it to `ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.NETWORK_ERROR, ...)`. No test in SonarApiClientTest exercises this path — the service-layer tests stub with `ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.NETWORK_ERROR, ...)` directly, bypassing the real HTTP stack. MockWebServer supports `SocketPolicy.DISCONNECT_AT_START` to simulate a TCP drop.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a test: `server.enqueue(MockResponse().setSocketPolicy(SocketPolicy.DISCONNECT_AT_START))`, call `client.validateConnection()`, assert `result.isError && (result as ApiResult.Error).type == ErrorType.NETWORK_ERROR`. Requires importing `mockwebserver3.SocketPolicy`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. SonarApiClient.kt lines 559-562 catch IOException and return ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.NETWORK_ERROR). SonarApiClientTest.kt has no test that exercises this path at the HTTP layer. The service-layer tests mock the ApiResult directly, bypassing the real client. MockWebServer's SocketPolicy.DISCONNECT_AT_START can simulate a TCP drop to trigger the IOException path. Claimed High severity is overstated — offline behavior is handled at a higher layer by FailureReason.OFFLINE — but the HTTP-layer path is still untested dark code. Medium is appropriate.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add a test: enqueue MockResponse with SocketPolicy.DISCONNECT_AT_START, call validateConnection(), assert result.isError and ErrorType.NETWORK_ERROR.

confirmed.title: SonarApiClient.getIssuesSinglePage: page/pageSize clamping and single-page behavior are untested
confirmed.description: `getIssuesSinglePage()` was introduced as the F-15 fix for the agent's paged-issues path (one HTTP request with `&p=&ps=` instead of a full loop). It coerces page values with `coerceAtLeast(1)` and `coerceIn(1, ISSUES_PAGE_SIZE)`. There are zero tests for it: not the happy path, not the page/pageSize boundary clamping (e.g. page=0 → p=1, pageSize=501 → ps=500), and not its 401/404 error responses.
confirmed.suggestedFix:
Add tests to SonarApiClientTest:
1. Happy path: enqueue a single-page response, call `getIssuesSinglePage("proj", 2, 10)`, assert the returned result contains the correct issues and that the request path includes `p=2&ps=10`.
2. Boundary clamping: call with `page=0, pageSize=999`, assert request path has `p=1&ps=500`.
3. Error response: enqueue 404, assert `ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.NOT_FOUND, ...)`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. SonarApiClient.kt lines 210-228 implement getIssuesSinglePage with page.coerceAtLeast(1) and pageSize.coerceIn(1, ISSUES_PAGE_SIZE) clamping. A full read of SonarApiClientTest.kt confirms there are zero tests for this method — no happy path, no clamping boundary, no error response. The function is an agent-facing path (F-15 fix) and the clamping guards are safety-critical for avoiding invalid Sonar requests. Claimed High severity is reasonable for an agent-accessible path with zero test coverage. Downgrading slightly to Medium because the clamping is defensive-only and won't cause data loss.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add tests: (1) happy-path single page response, verifying p= and ps= in request URL; (2) boundary clamping (page=0, pageSize=999) → request has p=1&ps=500; (3) 404 response → ApiResult.Error with ErrorType.NOT_FOUND.

confirmed.title: SonarApiClient.getMeasures: first-page error returns ApiResult.Error — that path is not tested
confirmed.description: The measures pagination loop distinguishes 'first page error → propagate error' from 'mid-pagination error → return partial success'. The first-page-error path for `getMeasures` is not covered by any test. The equivalent path for `getIssuesWithPaging` (mid-pagination 503 → partial success) is tested, but first-page failure for `getMeasures` is not.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a test: `server.enqueue(MockResponse().setResponseCode(401))`, call `client.getMeasures("proj")`, assert `result.isError && (result as ApiResult.Error).type == ErrorType.AUTH_FAILED`. Also add a mid-pagination 401 test: enqueue a valid page-1 response then a 401 for page-2 (with paging.total > 500 in page-1), assert `result.isSuccess` with partial data.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. getMeasures in SonarApiClient.kt (lines 325-347) has an explicit first-page-error path at line 341: `return if (page == 1) pageResult else ApiResult.Success(all)`. SonarApiClientTest.kt tests mid-pagination 503 for getIssuesWithPaging (line 417-431) and has multiple getMeasures pagination tests (lines 240-301), but none of them enqueue a 401/403/404 on page 1 for getMeasures. The first-page-error propagation is untested. The mid-pagination partial-success path for getMeasures is also untested. Both are real gaps. The severity is correctly Medium — not crash-level, but a missing contract test for a logic branch that explicitly distinguishes first vs. mid-pagination failure.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add tests: (1) enqueue 401, call getMeasures, assert ApiResult.Error with ErrorType.AUTH_FAILED (first-page failure propagates error); (2) enqueue a valid page-1 (with total > pageSize) then a 401 for page-2, assert ApiResult.Success with partial data (mid-pagination failure returns what was collected).

confirmed.title: SonarDataService.refreshWith: CE-tasks FORBIDDEN branch (analysisHistoryForbidden=true) has no test
confirmed.description: When `getAnalysisTasks` returns `ErrorType.FORBIDDEN`, `refreshWith` sets `analysisHistoryForbidden = true` in the resulting `SonarState`. No test verifies that this flag is correctly set, meaning the UI hint 'token lacks Administer Project permission' would go dark if the flag assignment were accidentally removed.
confirmed.suggestedFix: In a TestSonarDataService harness (or extending the existing one in SonarDataServiceTest), stub `getAnalysisTasks` to return `ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.FORBIDDEN, "403")` and assert `stateFlow.value.analysisHistoryForbidden == true`. Also verify that the state's `recentAnalyses` is empty (not the stale previous value).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. The CE-tasks FORBIDDEN branch at SonarDataService.kt lines 446-448 sets analysisHistoryForbidden=true, but the TestSonarDataService in SonarDataServiceTest.kt (lines 143-206) is a hand-rolled test double whose simplified refreshWith omits the CE tasks fetch entirely. No test anywhere (searched all test files) exercises this path. The SonarDataServiceHotspotsTest.kt line 99 uses ErrorType.FORBIDDEN for the hotspots path, not the CE tasks path. Adding the suggested test would confirm the flag assignment. The fix is safe — adding a test does not alter production code. However, the UI hint going dark if the flag assignment were removed is a minor UX regression, not a data-loss or crash scenario; Medium is defensible but Low is more accurate given this is a display-hint path with no security or correctness impact.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add a test that stubs getAnalysisTasks to return ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.FORBIDDEN, ...) and asserts stateFlow.value.analysisHistoryForbidden == true. The TestSonarDataService harness must be extended to replicate the CE tasks logic branch from the real refreshWith.

confirmed.title: SonarDataService.getLineCoverage: no test for cache-hit, API error, blank projectKey, or concurrent fetch
confirmed.description: The public `getLineCoverage` method has rich logic: it falls back to `settings.state.sonarProjectKey` when `projectKey` is null/blank, returns from `lineCoverageCache` on hit, calls `getSourceLines`, maps results via `CoverageMapper.mapLineStatuses`, and populates the cache. None of these paths have a test — not the cache-hit fast-path, not the `ApiResult.Error` returning `emptyMap()`, and not the blank/null projectKey fallback.
confirmed.suggestedFix:
Add unit tests using a testable harness with a mocked SonarApiClient:
1. Cache-hit: pre-populate `lineCoverageCache`, call `getLineCoverage`, assert the result matches the cache value and no HTTP call was made.
2. API error: stub `getSourceLines` to return `ApiResult.Error(...)`, assert `getLineCoverage` returns `emptyMap()` and does NOT populate the cache.
3. Blank projectKey: pass `projectKey = ""` with no settings fallback, assert `emptyMap()` is returned immediately.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. SonarDataService.kt lines 232-259 implement getLineCoverage with three distinct early-exit paths: blank effectiveKey returns emptyMap() immediately (line 234), cache-hit returns cached value without HTTP (line 238), and ApiResult.Error returns emptyMap() without populating the cache (lines 255-258). A grep across all test files for getLineCoverage and lineCoverageCache returned zero results. The cache-hit path is particularly load-bearing: a bug there (e.g. accidentally returning null) would cause repeated API calls on every editor keystroke and break the CoverageLineMarkerProvider's fast path. Medium severity is accurate. Adding the tests is safe; no production change required.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add unit tests for getLineCoverage: (1) blank projectKey fast-exit, (2) cache-hit avoids HTTP call (pre-populate lineCoverageCache, verify no HTTP), (3) ApiResult.Error returns emptyMap and does not populate cache. Requires making the TestSonarDataService harness expose the lineCoverageCache or using a testable subclass.

confirmed.title: SonarDataService.mapProjectHealth: no test for rating-letter mapping, partial metrics, or non-numeric values
confirmed.description: The private `mapProjectHealth` method converts SonarQube numeric rating strings ("1.0".."5.0") via `SonarRatingUtils.ratingLetter`, and defaults all fields to 0/empty when the corresponding metric is missing. This method is tested only via the `refreshWith` integration path which uses an empty measures list (so all defaults). No test covers: a full metric set with real rating values, a non-numeric rating value, or missing individual metrics. `SonarRatingUtils.ratingLetter` itself also has zero dedicated tests.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a dedicated `SonarRatingUtilsTest` covering: `ratingLetter("1.0") == "A"`, `ratingLetter("5.0") == "E"`, `ratingLetter("6.0") == ""` (out of range), `ratingLetter(null) == ""`, `ratingLetter("abc") == ""`. Also add a `mapProjectHealth` test within the DataService test double: supply all seven metrics with numeric ratings, assert each letter and numeric field is correctly populated.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. SonarRatingUtils.ratingLetter at lines 21-31 of SonarRatingUtils.kt converts numeric strings to A-E letters. The when block covers 1-5 correctly (2->B, 3->C, 4->D are present in the source, not just 1 and 5 as the finding claimed). Searching all test files for ratingLetter, SonarRatingUtils, and mapProjectHealth returns zero hits. The existing SonarDataServiceTest refreshWith tests always pass an empty measures list so mapProjectHealth always returns all-defaults. The risk of a bug here is contained — rating letters are display-only in the UI — which makes Low more appropriate than Medium. The fix is safe.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add a dedicated SonarRatingUtilsTest covering ratingLetter for all five valid inputs ("1.0"->"A" through "5.0"->"E"), out-of-range ("6.0"->""), null, and non-numeric ("abc"->""). Add a mapProjectHealth integration test within the TestSonarDataService that supplies all seven metrics and asserts each letter and numeric field.

confirmed.title: SonarServiceImpl.getCoverage: coveredLines can silently go negative — no test for uncoveredLines > totalLinesToCover
confirmed.description: In `SonarServiceImpl.getCoverage`, `coveredLines = totalLinesToCover - uncoveredLines` is computed without a floor at 0. If SonarQube returns inconsistent data (e.g. uncoveredLines reported from a different analysis epoch than linesToCover), `coveredLines` can be negative. The resulting `CoverageData(coveredLines = -N)` propagates to the agent's summary string. `SonarServiceImpl` has only partial test coverage via the `testClient` seam, and this particular method has no test at all.
confirmed.evidence:
// SonarServiceImpl.kt lines 251-259
val totalLinesToCover = measures[SonarMetricKey.LINES_TO_COVER]?.toIntOrNull() ?: 0
val uncoveredLines = measures[SonarMetricKey.UNCOVERED_LINES]?.toIntOrNull() ?: 0
val coveredLines = totalLinesToCover - uncoveredLines  // can be negative
val data = CoverageData(
    lineCoverage = lineCoverage,
    branchCoverage = branchCoverage,
    totalLines = totalLinesToCover,
    coveredLines = coveredLines  // negative passed through to agent
)
confirmed.suggestedFix:
1. Fix: clamp `coveredLines = (totalLinesToCover - uncoveredLines).coerceAtLeast(0)`.
2. Add a test via the `testClient` seam (`SonarServiceImpl(...).also { it.testClient = api }`): stub `getProjectMeasures` with `lines_to_cover=10, uncovered_lines=15` (stale data scenario), call `getCoverage(...)`, assert `coveredLines == 0` (or whatever the clamped value should be).
3. Also add a basic happy-path test for `getCoverage`: this method currently has zero test coverage.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. SonarServiceImpl.getCoverage at line 253: coveredLines = totalLinesToCover - uncoveredLines with no coerceAtLeast(0) floor. When SonarQube returns inconsistent epoch data (uncoveredLines from a stale scan > totalLinesToCover from the current scan), coveredLines is negative. This propagates into the CoverageData.coveredLines field and appears verbatim in the agent's summary string at line 266: 'Covered: -N / M lines'. This is wrong output visible to users and agents, not a crash, so High is slightly inflated — Medium is correct. No test exists for getCoverage (confirmed by grep). The suggested coerceAtLeast(0) fix is non-harmful: SonarQube's own linesToCover/uncoveredLines data contract guarantees non-negative covered lines, so clamping at 0 is always semantically correct.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Apply coveredLines = (totalLinesToCover - uncoveredLines).coerceAtLeast(0) at line 253. Add a getCoverage test via the testClient seam with lines_to_cover=10, uncovered_lines=15 (stale data) asserting coveredLines==0, and a happy-path test with normal data.

confirmed.title: SonarServiceImpl.compactLineNumbers: private helper has zero tests despite non-trivial logic
confirmed.description: `compactLineNumbers` compresses a sorted line list into range strings (e.g. `[1,2,3,5,7,8]` → `"1-3, 5, 7-8"`). It has non-trivial branching: single-element runs, consecutive runs, non-consecutive gaps, and empty input. The method is private but embedded in the agent's LLM summary output, so a bug (e.g. off-by-one in `end + 1`) produces a silently wrong report. No test covers it directly or indirectly through `getBranchQualityReport`.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Extract `compactLineNumbers` to an `internal` function (or a companion object) so it can be tested directly, or test it end-to-end through `getBranchQualityReport` by asserting specific substrings in the returned `summary` field. Required test cases: empty list → `""`, single element → `"42"`, consecutive range → `"1-3"`, single gap → `"1, 3"`, mixed → `"1-3, 5, 7-9"`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. compactLineNumbers at lines 1258-1275 of SonarServiceImpl.kt is private and contains non-trivial loop logic. SonarBranchReportGateAndDupTest.kt calls getBranchQualityReport but its test fixtures set new_uncovered_lines=0 (default setup line 57) and the tests only assert on isError, qualityGate.status, and duplicatedLineRanges — never on summary content containing line ranges. BranchQualityReportTest.kt only calls the mock API directly, not the real SonarServiceImpl. So compactLineNumbers is effectively untested end-to-end. However, the algorithm is straightforward (standard run-length encoding) and a regression here would produce a wrong-but-non-crashing report string. Low severity is appropriate.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Extract compactLineNumbers to internal visibility (or a companion object) so it can be tested directly, or add an end-to-end test through getBranchQualityReport that supplies 3+ consecutive and non-consecutive uncovered line numbers and asserts specific substrings like '3-5' and '7' in the result summary.

confirmed.title: SonarServiceImpl.componentExistsCached: TTL expiry and cache invalidation on URL change are untested
confirmed.description: `componentExistsCached` in `SonarServiceImpl` caches `/api/components/show` results per `(sonarUrl, projectKey)` with a 5-minute TTL. The cache key is `ConnectionSettings.getInstance().state.sonarUrl to projectKey`, meaning a URL change in settings invalidates the key. No tests cover: (a) cache-hit short-circuit avoids a second HTTP call, (b) TTL expiry triggers re-fetch, or (c) stale URL in key means a URL change forces a fresh probe. The `SonarBranchReportGateAndDupTest` uses `testClient` but bypasses the `getIssues` → `componentExistsCached` code path because the test calls `getBranchQualityReport` (which does not call `componentExistsCached`).
confirmed.suggestedFix:
Add tests to a new `SonarServiceImplIssuesTest` using the `testClient` seam:
1. Cache-hit: call `getIssues(...)` twice; stub `componentExists` to be called only once; assert the second call does not invoke `api.componentExists` (verify with `coVerify(exactly = 1)`).
2. Transient error fall-through: stub `componentExists` to return `ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.NETWORK_ERROR, ...)`, assert `getIssues` proceeds to `api.getIssues` anyway (no error returned for a probe failure).
3. 'Not found' response: stub `componentExists` to return `ApiResult.Success(false)`, assert `getIssues` returns `ToolResult` with `isError = true` and a project-not-found message.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. componentExistsCached at lines 70-84 of SonarServiceImpl.kt maintains a ConcurrentHashMap with a 5-minute TTL, keyed by (sonarUrl, projectKey). The cache-hit early return at line 75 is reachable via getIssues but no test calls getIssues through the SonarServiceImpl at all — SonarBranchReportGateAndDupTest.kt only calls getBranchQualityReport (line 89/98/115), which does not go through componentExistsCached. The SonarApiClientTest tests the underlying componentExists HTTP call but not the caching layer. The risk is primarily correctness: a TTL bug would cause either stale 'not found' errors or unnecessary extra HTTP calls. Not a crash or data-loss scenario, so Low is appropriate.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add tests via the testClient seam (SonarServiceImpl.also { it.testClient = api }): call getIssues twice, stub componentExists to succeed on first call, use coVerify(exactly=1) to confirm the second call skips the HTTP probe. Also add tests for the not-found and transient-error fall-through paths.

confirmed.title: SonarDataService.refreshWith: newCodeMode heuristic (branch-isMain detection) is untested
confirmed.description: The `newCodeMode` resolution in `refreshWith` (lines 513-528) has three priority branches: (1) persisted setting (sonarPreferredCodeMode 1/2), (2) branch-isMain heuristic (new state when projectKey/branch changes: main → false, non-main → true), and (3) in-session toggle preserved. The TestSonarDataService double hard-codes `newCodeMode = false` in its state construction, so none of these branches are exercised.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Extend the TestSonarDataService harness (or create a focused test class) that replicates the newCodeMode logic. Test cases: (a) `sonarPreferredCodeMode = 1` → `newCodeMode = false` regardless of branch type; (b) `sonarPreferredCodeMode = 2` → `newCodeMode = true`; (c) no setting, main branch → `newCodeMode = false`; (d) no setting, non-main branch → `newCodeMode = true`; (e) same projectKey/branch as previous state → in-session toggle value preserved.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. The newCodeMode resolution block at SonarDataService.kt lines 513-528 has three priority branches: persisted setting (sonarPreferredCodeMode 1 or 2), branch-isMain heuristic (new state only), and in-session toggle preserved. The TestSonarDataService in SonarDataServiceTest.kt (lines 143-206) completely omits this logic — its refreshWith hard-codes a fixed SonarState without newCodeMode computation. All five sub-cases (persisted=false, persisted=true, no-setting+main, no-setting+non-main, same key/branch toggle) are untested. However, this affects only the default tab selection in the UI Quality dashboard (Overall vs New Code view). Incorrect resolution would be a minor usability nuisance, not data corruption. Low severity is appropriate.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Extend the TestSonarDataService harness to replicate the newCodeMode resolution logic, then add tests for all five branches: persisted=1 (false regardless of branch), persisted=2 (true regardless), no-setting+main branch (false), no-setting+non-main (true), same projectKey/branch (in-session toggle preserved).

confirmed.title: SonarApiClient.paginateSonarItems: MAX_ISSUES_PAGES hard-cap path is not tested
confirmed.description: The `paginateSonarItems` helper includes a post-loop return at line 296 that fires when MAX_ISSUES_PAGES (20) is exhausted before `allItems.size >= total`. This 'truncated result' path has a log.warn but returns `ApiResult.Success` with partial data. No test exercises it for either issues or hotspots — the existing pagination tests all stop before the cap.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add a test that sets up `MAX_ISSUES_PAGES + 1` pages worth of data but declares a `total` larger than `MAX_ISSUES_PAGES * ISSUES_PAGE_SIZE`. Because actually enqueueing 21 responses is impractical, override `MAX_ISSUES_PAGES` or use a tiny custom wrapper. Alternatively, test the cap at a reduced page count by creating a `SonarApiClient` with a known-small page constant. Assert: (a) exactly `MAX_ISSUES_PAGES` requests are made, (b) result is `ApiResult.Success` with `MAX_ISSUES_PAGES * pageSize` items.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed. paginateSonarItems at lines 264-298 of SonarApiClient.kt has three exit paths: (a) allItems.size >= total early-stop (tested by getIssuesWithPaging stops test at line 401), (b) mid-pagination error partial-success (tested at line 417), and (c) MAX_ISSUES_PAGES=20 cap exhaustion at lines 296-297 (not tested). The finding is accurate — no test enqueues 20+ pages with a total > 20*pageSize. The cap path returns ApiResult.Success with partial data and a log.warn, which is the correct graceful-degradation behavior. The risk is that a future refactor could accidentally change the Success return to an Error, which would break large projects silently. However this is a real-world edge case (>10,000 issues per project) and the behavior is not user-visible beyond truncated results. Low severity is appropriate; the finding correctly notes the impracticality of enqueuing 21 responses and suggests overriding MAX_ISSUES_PAGES.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add a test that constructs SonarApiClient with a reduced MAX_ISSUES_PAGES constant (or tests paginateSonarItems indirectly via a small reflectively-set cap), enqueues cap+1 pages worth of items declared at a total larger than cap*pageSize, and asserts the result is ApiResult.Success with exactly cap*pageSize items and that exactly cap requests were made.

confirmed.title: CoverageMapper.mapLineStatuses: coveredConditions=null with non-null conditions edge case not tested
confirmed.description: In `mapLineStatuses`, the PARTIAL branch triggers when `coveredConditions != null && coveredConditions < conditions`. However, if `conditions` is non-null but `coveredConditions` IS null (a valid API response when the branch count is present but no coverage info is available), the line falls through to the `else -> LineCoverageStatus.COVERED` branch — which is arguably wrong (it should be PARTIAL or UNCOVERED for a line with uncovered branches). There is no test for this combination.
confirmed.suggestedFix:
Add a test to CoverageMapperTest:
```kotlin
val lines = listOf(SonarSourceLineDto(line = 1, lineHits = 1, conditions = 2, coveredConditions = null))
val result = CoverageMapper.mapLineStatuses(lines)
// Document the current behavior — either assert COVERED (current) to pin it,
// or fix to PARTIAL and assert PARTIAL.
```
The fix should likely treat `conditions != null && coveredConditions == null` as PARTIAL since branch data is present but untested.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: The code path at CoverageMapper.kt lines 46-51 is confirmed: the `when` block short-circuits on `coveredConditions != null` before comparing to `conditions`, so a DTO with `conditions=2, coveredConditions=null, lineHits>0` falls through to `else -> LineCoverageStatus.COVERED`. Both fields are nullable in SonarSourceLineDto (lines 507-508 of SonarDtos.kt), so the combination is structurally possible. The test file confirms no test exercises this combination. However, the SonarQube `/api/sources/lines` API consistently returns `coveredConditions=0` (not omitted) when a line has branch conditions but none are covered — this `null`+non-null pairing does not arise from a real SonarQube response in normal operation. The finding is real as an untested edge-case code path, but the described misclassification (COVERED instead of PARTIAL) has no practical impact because the triggering API shape is not emitted by SonarQube. The suggested fix — adding a test to document the current behavior — is purely additive and not harmful. The claimed severity of Low is accurate.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add a test asserting the current behavior (COVERED) to pin the edge-case contract, accompanied by a comment explaining that SonarQube does not emit conditions without coveredConditions. Do not change the production logic unless a real SonarQube response that triggers this path is demonstrated.

confirmed.title: cancel() silently skips Bamboo stop for RUNNING builds — build keeps running after user cancels
confirmed.description: QueueService.cancel() only fires bambooService.cancelBuild() when the entry status is QUEUED_ON_BAMBOO. RUNNING entries (which have already started executing on Bamboo) are silently transitioned to CANCELLED locally without any Bamboo API call. cancelBuild maps to DELETE /queue/{key} (removes a queued-but-not-started build); the separate stopBuild maps to PUT /result/{key}/stop (terminates an in-progress build). Neither is called for RUNNING entries. The result: the user clicks Cancel on a running build, the Monitor list clears the row and shows CANCELLED, but the Bamboo agent continues executing the job until completion. This wastes CI resources and misleads the user about the build's actual state.
confirmed.suggestedFix: In the cancel() mutex block, extend the status guard to also call bambooService.stopBuild(resultKey) for RUNNING entries: `if (resultKey != null) { when (entry.status) { QueueEntryStatus.QUEUED_ON_BAMBOO -> bambooService.cancelBuild(resultKey); QueueEntryStatus.RUNNING -> bambooService.stopBuild(resultKey); else -> {} } }`. Both calls should log and silently continue on error (the local CANCELLED transition is still correct — if the build finishes milliseconds before the stop reaches Bamboo, the poll loop will detect the terminal state on the next tick). Add a test: enqueue an entry, advance it to RUNNING status, call cancel(), and verify stopBuild was called exactly once.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. QueueService.kt lines 212-216 show the guard `entry.status == QueueEntryStatus.QUEUED_ON_BAMBOO` — RUNNING entries skip the Bamboo API call entirely. `bambooService.stopBuild()` (PUT /result/{key}/stop for in-progress builds) is implemented in BambooServiceImpl.kt (line 411), is on the BambooService interface (line 75), and is used in BuildDashboardPanel.kt (line 962) for the same purpose from the Bamboo tab. The omission for RUNNING queue entries is a clear logic gap: the user sees CANCELLED locally but the Bamboo agent continues running. The fix is straightforward and does not break any load-bearing path. `cancelBuild` maps to DELETE /queue/{key} and would be wrong/no-op for an already-running build anyway, so calling it for RUNNING would also be incorrect — calling `stopBuild` for RUNNING is the architecturally correct fix.
confirmed.recommendedAction: In QueueService.cancel(), extend the status check: for RUNNING entries call bambooService.stopBuild(resultKey), for QUEUED_ON_BAMBOO entries call bambooService.cancelBuild(resultKey). Both should log the call. The local CANCELLED transition proceeds regardless.

confirmed.title: cancel() result from bambooService silently ignored — cancellation failure is invisible to the user
confirmed.description: At line 215, bambooService.cancelBuild(resultKey) is called but its ToolResult<Unit> return value is completely discarded. If Bamboo returns an auth failure (403), plan not found (404), or network error, the entry is still transitioned to CANCELLED locally and the EventBus emits QueuePositionChanged(-1). The user has no indication the cancel request failed, and Bamboo's queue still holds the build. The same pattern is present for the not-yet-implemented stopBuild gap (COR-1). In contrast, handleWaitingLocal carefully classifies trigger failures by ErrorType and distinguishes permanent vs transient to decide whether to enter FAILED_TO_TRIGGER.
confirmed.evidence:
// QueueService.kt line 215 — return value of suspend fun cancelBuild() is ignored
bambooService.cancelBuild(resultKey)
confirmed.suggestedFix: Capture the result and log it: `val cancelResult = bambooService.cancelBuild(resultKey); if (cancelResult.isError) { log.warn("[Automation:Queue] Failed to cancel build $resultKey on Bamboo (continuing with local CANCELLED): ${cancelResult.summary}") }`. For a better UX, surface the Bamboo error in the entry's errorMessage field so the detail panel shows it. The local CANCELLED transition should still proceed regardless (the user's intent is to remove this entry from their view).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. Line 215 of QueueService.kt calls `bambooService.cancelBuild(resultKey)` and discards the `ToolResult<Unit>` return value. If Bamboo returns 403/404 or a network error, the entry is still locally transitioned to CANCELLED and the EventBus emits QueuePositionChanged(-1). The user has no visible indication that Bamboo still holds the build. By contrast, handleWaitingLocal (lines 400-423) carefully classifies failures via ErrorType before deciding on terminal vs retry. Logging the error (and optionally surfacing it in `errorMessage`) would be an improvement with no risk of breaking existing behavior. The local transition to CANCELLED is intentionally unconditional (user's intent to remove the row), so fix is additive.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Capture `val cancelResult = bambooService.cancelBuild(resultKey)` and log a warning if `cancelResult.isError`. Optionally populate `entry.errorMessage` so the Monitor detail panel surfaces it.

confirmed.title: enqueue() fast-path holds the mutex across two Bamboo HTTP round-trips, blocking all concurrent user actions
confirmed.description: Inside mutex.withLock { } in enqueue(), the fast-path (lines 170–191) makes two sequential Bamboo HTTP calls — bambooService.getRunningBuilds() then doTrigger() (which calls bambooService.triggerBuild()) — while holding the coroutine Mutex. Any concurrent cancel(), dismiss(), or pollOnce() call that also tries to acquire the mutex will suspend for the full round-trip latency of both HTTP calls (potentially 2–10+ seconds each on a slow VPN). The pollOnce() was explicitly redesigned (comment at line 345) to avoid exactly this pattern by snapshotting IDs under the lock and re-acquiring per-entry. The enqueue fast-path was not given the same treatment and creates the same 'block everything while HTTP is in-flight' freeze that the pollOnce redesign was meant to fix.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Apply the same snapshot-release-reacquire pattern used in pollOnce: release the mutex after adding the entry to _stateFlow and emitting QueuePositionChanged; then check and trigger outside the lock. Atomicity for the state mutation (adding to _stateFlow) is still correct; the idle-check and trigger can be done without the lock since they only observe Bamboo state and then re-acquire to update the entry's status. Example restructuring: hold the lock only for the state-mutation block (lines 147–165), break out of the lock, then do the idle-check+trigger outside, and re-acquire a new lock to update the entry's bambooResultKey if the trigger succeeds.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. QueueService.kt lines 136-192 show `mutex.withLock { ... }` wrapping lines 136-192, which includes the call to `bambooService.getRunningBuilds(...)` (HTTP call 1, line 174) and `doTrigger(entry)` (HTTP call 2 via triggerBuild, line 177), all while holding the mutex. The comment at lines 345-355 on pollOnce explicitly documents the prior pattern as Flaw #8 and describes the snapshot-release-reacquire fix. The enqueue fast-path was not given the same treatment. Any concurrent cancel(), dismiss(), or pollOnce() that acquires the mutex will block for the full latency of both HTTP calls (potentially 2-10+ seconds on VPN). Severity is Medium not High because this is a responsiveness issue, not data loss — the fast-path only executes when `suiteEntries == 0`, limiting how often it's triggered.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Apply the same snapshot-release pattern: release the mutex after adding the entry to _stateFlow and emitting QueuePositionChanged (around line 165), then perform the idle-check and doTrigger outside the lock, then re-acquire a new lock to update the entry's bambooResultKey.

confirmed.title: onBuildLogReady() reads non-@Volatile field currentSuitePlanKey from IO dispatcher — JMM data race
confirmed.description: subscribeToBuildEvents() launches a coroutine on scope (Dispatchers.IO) that calls onBuildLogReady() in the collect{} body. onBuildLogReady() reads currentSuitePlanKey at lines 787–788 without any synchronization. currentSuitePlanKey is a plain var (no @Volatile) written on the EDT by onSuiteSelected() (line 450) and the init block (line 381). Under the Java Memory Model, a write on the EDT and a read on an IO thread to an unsynchronized field have no happens-before ordering — the IO thread may see a stale (empty-string) value. In the worst case, the BlankShield at line 787 evaluates isBlank() on the stale empty string and drops a valid BuildLogReady event, leaving the docker-tag banner stuck in the 'Waiting for CI build...' state even after the build completes. By contrast, currentFocusBuild is correctly annotated @Volatile (line 189-190).
confirmed.suggestedFix: Add @Volatile to currentSuitePlanKey (line 184): `@Volatile private var currentSuitePlanKey: String = ""`. Since it only stores a String (a reference type), @Volatile gives the necessary JMM visibility guarantee with negligible overhead. Alternatively, capture the value on the EDT before launching the IO coroutine and pass it as a parameter, but @Volatile is simpler and consistent with the existing @Volatile currentFocusBuild pattern.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. AutomationPanel.kt line 184 shows `private var currentSuitePlanKey: String = ""` — no @Volatile annotation. The field is written on the EDT by onSuiteSelected() (line 450) and the init block (line 381). It is read in onBuildLogReady() (line 787), which is called from a `scope.launch { eventBus.events.collect { ... } }` coroutine (lines 776-784) — that scope is `CoroutineScope(Dispatchers.IO + SupervisorJob())` (line 57). Under the JMM, the IO-thread read has no happens-before ordering with the EDT write. This can cause a stale empty-string read, dropping a valid BuildLogReady event. The comparison with `@Volatile currentFocusBuild` (lines 189-190) confirms the pattern is known and intentional for that field — it was simply missed for `currentSuitePlanKey`. Adding `@Volatile` is a single-line fix with negligible overhead.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Add `@Volatile` to the `currentSuitePlanKey` declaration at line 184, consistent with the existing `@Volatile currentFocusBuild` pattern.

confirmed.title: TagHistoryService connection leaked when dispose() races with lazy initialization
confirmed.description: TagHistoryService.close() guards the connection close with `if (!connectionInitialized) return` at line 335. The `connectionInitialized` flag is set to `true` at line 60, inside the lazy { } block, after the Connection is opened and initSchema is run. If dispose() is called (from the EDT on project close) while a background coroutine is mid-way through the lazy initialization block (connection created, initSchema running, but connectionInitialized not yet set to true), close() sees connectionInitialized=false and returns early. The lazy block then completes, leaves connectionInitialized=true, and the Connection is never closed. The leaking connection holds WAL/SHM file locks on the SQLite DB, causing 'database is locked' errors if the project is immediately reopened in another IDE window. Kotlin's by lazy uses LazyThreadSafetyMode.SYNCHRONIZED (prevents double-init), but the connectionInitialized flag is set at the end of the block, creating a window where the connection object exists but the guard flag is still false.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Eliminate the separate connectionInitialized flag and instead use the lazy property's own initialization state. Replace the guard in close() with a null-safe access pattern: declare `private val connectionOrNull: Connection? get() = if (::connection.isInitialized) connection else null` (Kotlin lateinit form), or change to `private val _connection: Connection? by lazy { ... }` returning null on failure. Then close() becomes: `_connection?.let { try { it.close() } catch (_: Exception) {} }`. This removes the TOCTOU window between the Connection being created and the flag being set.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real in theory. TagHistoryService.kt shows `connectionInitialized = true` is set at line 60, at the END of the lazy block (after initSchema runs). `close()` at line 334 guards on `if (!connectionInitialized) return`. Kotlin lazy uses SYNCHRONIZED mode by default, meaning only one thread can execute the lazy block at a time. However, if `dispose()` is called while the lazy block is mid-execution (e.g., initSchema is running and connectionInitialized is still false), `close()` returns early without closing the connection. After the lazy block completes, the connection is open but will never be closed. This can cause WAL file lock issues on project reopen. That said, the window is very narrow in practice (project close racing with first DB access), and the service is project-scoped so it's rarely a real-world issue. Severity is Low (as claimed) — edge case, not a normal-use bug.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Move `connectionInitialized = true` to BEFORE `conn` is returned, or better, eliminate the flag and use a nullable `_connection` val. The suggested null-safe approach in the finding is sound.

confirmed.title: resolveServiceCiPlanKey() is a private method with zero call sites
confirmed.description: The private method `resolveServiceCiPlanKey()` at line 715 has no callers anywhere in the codebase (confirmed via `rg` across the entire worktree). The KDoc on line 713 explicitly states "NOTE (Phase B): This method is no longer called from the docker-tag detection path" and promises a Phase D cleanup that has not happened. The method is 13 lines of non-trivial logic that is simply dead.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete `resolveServiceCiPlanKey()` entirely (lines 715-727). The note in the KDoc already calls this a pending cleanup.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. Grep confirms `resolveServiceCiPlanKey` appears only at its definition site (AutomationPanel.kt line 715) — zero call sites. The KDoc at line 710-713 explicitly acknowledges it is no longer called and notes a Phase D cleanup that has not happened. The method is 13 lines of dead code. Deleting it is safe and does not affect any other functionality. Severity is Low — dead code, no behavior impact.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Delete resolveServiceCiPlanKey() at lines 715-727.

confirmed.title: DriftDetectorService and its reconcileFromBamboo guard block are permanently dead
confirmed.description: `DriftDetectorService.isRegistryConfigured()` unconditionally returns `false` (by design — registry calls removed). The guard in `reconcileFromBamboo` at lines 622–625 therefore can never execute. The three lines inside the `if` block, the `driftDetectorService` lazy field (line 63), and the `DriftDetectorService` class itself are all permanently dead complexity. The service is still registered in `plugin.xml` (line 306), adding pointless DI overhead.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete the `if (tags.isNotEmpty() && driftDetectorService.isRegistryConfigured())` block in `reconcileFromBamboo`. Remove the `driftDetectorService` lazy field from `AutomationPanel`. Delete the entire `DriftDetectorService` class. Remove its `<projectService>` registration from `plugin.xml`.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. DriftDetectorService.kt line 25 shows `fun isRegistryConfigured(): Boolean = false` — unconditional. The guard in AutomationPanel.kt lines 622-625 (`if (tags.isNotEmpty() && driftDetectorService.isRegistryConfigured())`) is therefore permanently dead — it never executes. The service KDoc in DriftDetectorService.kt confirms this is by design: 'registry calls removed'. The `driftDetectorService` lazy field (line 63), the service class, and its plugin.xml registration (line 306) are all permanently dead weight. However, severity should be Low rather than Medium: the code compiles, runs correctly (the no-op path is fine), and there is no behavior impact — this is purely a dead-code cleanup. The comment in automation/CLAUDE.md confirms `DriftDetectorService — no-op after registry calls removed; isRegistryConfigured() always returns false`.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Delete the `if (tags.isNotEmpty() && driftDetectorService.isRegistryConfigured())` block, the `driftDetectorService` lazy field from AutomationPanel, the DriftDetectorService class, and its projectService registration from plugin.xml.

confirmed.title: DriftResult model and checkDrift() are completely unused dead types
confirmed.description: `DriftResult` (lines 111–116) is a `data class` that appears only in the no-op `DriftDetectorService.checkDrift()` signature and one test that just constructs the object. `checkDrift()` is never called from production code. With `DriftDetectorService` dead (CLE-2), `DriftResult` is unreachable type pollution in the public model namespace.
confirmed.evidence:
data class DriftResult(
    val serviceName: String,
    val currentTag: String,
    val latestReleaseTag: String,
    val isStale: Boolean
)
// Only production use: DriftDetectorService.checkDrift() return type — which is never called
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete `DriftResult` from `AutomationModels.kt` and delete the corresponding test case in `AutomationModelsTest.kt` (`DriftResult marks stale when versions differ`).
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. AutomationModels.kt lines 111-116 show `data class DriftResult(...)`. Grep confirms it is only used in DriftDetectorService.checkDrift() return type (which is never called from production code) and one test in AutomationModelsTest.kt lines 120-128 that only constructs the object. With DriftDetectorService being dead (as established in CLE-2), DriftResult is unreachable type pollution. Deleting it along with the test case is safe. Severity is Low — dead type, no behavior impact.

confirmed.title: TagEntry.isDrift and RegistryStatus.{VALID,NOT_FOUND,CHECKING} are permanently unreachable UI states
confirmed.description: In production, `TagEntry.isDrift` is always `false` and `TagEntry.registryStatus` is always `RegistryStatus.UNKNOWN` (confirmed by searching all assignments in `main/`). The renderer in `TagStagingPanel` has live branches for `isDrift -> WARNING`, `VALID -> SUCCESS`, `NOT_FOUND -> ERROR`, and `CHECKING`, but these branches can never fire at runtime. The values `VALID`, `NOT_FOUND`, and `CHECKING` in the `RegistryStatus` enum are dead. Similarly `TagEntry.latestReleaseTag` is always `null`.
confirmed.evidence:
// All production assignments (TagBuilderService.kt, BaselineCacheService.kt, TagStagingPanel.kt):
registryStatus = RegistryStatus.UNKNOWN
isDrift = false
latestReleaseTag = null

// TagStagingPanel.kt:257-259 (dead branches):
entry.isDrift -> "⚠ Drift"
entry.registryStatus == RegistryStatus.VALID -> "✓ OK"
entry.registryStatus == RegistryStatus.NOT_FOUND -> "✗ Missing"
confirmed.suggestedFix: Remove `isDrift`, `latestReleaseTag`, and the renderer branches that depend on them from `TagEntry` and `TagStagingPanel`. Simplify `RegistryStatus` to just `UNKNOWN` and `ERROR` (keep ERROR for future use), or delete the enum entirely if only UNKNOWN remains. Remove the `DriftResult.latestReleaseTag` field if `DriftResult` itself is not deleted via CLE-3.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed by reading TagBuilderService.kt (lines 218-221, 245-248), BaselineCacheService.kt (lines 198-201), TagStagingPanel.kt (line 357-360): every production assignment sets isDrift=false, registryStatus=RegistryStatus.UNKNOWN, latestReleaseTag=null. The renderer branches at TagStagingPanel.kt lines 257-259 (isDrift -> '⚠ Drift', VALID -> '✓ OK', NOT_FOUND -> '✗ Missing') and lines 308-324 (color branches for VALID/NOT_FOUND/isDrift) can never fire at runtime. RegistryStatus.VALID, NOT_FOUND, and CHECKING are dead enum values. DriftDetectorService.isRegistryConfigured() always returns false (CLAUDE.md: 'no-op after registry calls removed'). Removing these fields and renderer branches is safe cleanup with no risk of breaking functionality, since no path sets them to non-default values.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Safe to remove isDrift, latestReleaseTag from TagEntry and the corresponding renderer branches in TagStagingPanel. Simplify RegistryStatus to UNKNOWN and ERROR (keep ERROR for setValueAt's user-edit reset at TagStagingPanel line 274). Update AutomationModelsTest which constructs TagEntry with isDrift=true and latestReleaseTag='2.4.0' in the first test.

confirmed.title: loadBaseline() is a legacy delegation stub with no production callers
confirmed.description: The `loadBaseline()` method at line 254 is marked `/** Legacy method — delegates to [loadBaselineWithDiagnostics]. */` and has zero production call sites (confirmed by `rg` across the full worktree). Its only callers are three test assertions in `TagBuilderServiceTest` that test the delegation rather than the underlying logic.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Delete `loadBaseline()`. Update the three test call sites in `TagBuilderServiceTest` to call `loadBaselineWithDiagnostics(...)?.tags` directly so the tests continue exercising the real code path.
confirmed.verdictReasoning: Confirmed by searching the entire worktree: loadBaseline() at TagBuilderService.kt line 254 has zero production callers. Only TagBuilderServiceTest.kt lines 68, 156, and 169 call it. The method is marked '/** Legacy method — delegates to [loadBaselineWithDiagnostics]. */' and purely delegates to loadBaselineWithDiagnostics(...).tags. Removing it requires only updating the three test call sites to call loadBaselineWithDiagnostics(...).tags directly. The suggested fix does not risk losing any logic or backward-compat surface since this is a non-public service method.
confirmed.recommendedAction: Delete loadBaseline() and update the three test call sites to call loadBaselineWithDiagnostics(...)?.tags (null-safe, since loadBaselineWithDiagnostics always returns non-null). Low priority.

confirmed.title: singlePageMode branch in scoreAndRankRuns is redundant dead complexity
confirmed.description: The `singlePageMode` check at lines 90–99 introduces a conditional that always resolves to the same Bamboo API call.
